Signs provider issues messed up the pape: tmlr_traces_part_aa is genuinely gzip-compressed (magic bytes 1f8b) and is a raw byte-split of a single continuous gzip stream that decompresses correctly even from a truncated prefix. In .../multi-chart-value-retrieval_102/, experiment.log records a POST to https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions returning "HTTP/1.1 200 OK", followed by chain-of-thought text referencing "Canon" and "Configuration Item by Manufacturer" and the action click('a311'); the accompanying summary_info.json shows n_steps=3, terminated=true, truncated=false, max_output_tokens=150, cum_cost=0.02317692. The stuck-loop episode at .../workload-balancing-small-l2_102/experiment.log, roughly 3.5GB into the stream, contains exactly 42 occurrences of send_msg_to_user, with coherent, grammatical, non-degenerate repeated text — a genuine stuck agentic loop rather than a serving artifact. Both logs decode as UTF-8 with zero replacement characters and zero `<|...|>` token-leakage markers. A regex string-extraction pass over the raw pickle bytes of step_0.pkl.gz, searching for provider/gen-id/response_id/chatcmpl, returns zero hits: this data does not surface provider or generation-ID metadata without unpickling through the agentlab package, so that dimension remains unchecked rather than shown clean. The coverage behind these findings is limited — 9 episodes and one model (llama-3.1-405b-instruct) out of six evaluated, drawn from roughly 3.5GB of the 207GB corpus (476 experiment.log files turn up in that slice alone) — a spot-check, not exhaustive coverage.

Title: Aider (AI pair-programming CLI + polyglot code benchmark / leaderboard)
Summary: Aider is a popular (47k+ star) open-source AI pair-programming CLI that supports many LLM backends via litellm, including OpenRouter. It also ships a "polyglot" coding benchmark (benchmark/benchmark.py) whose results populate a widely-cited public leaderboard comparing coding ability across frontier and open-weight models.
Importance/Impact: 47k+ stars, one of the most popular AI coding CLIs; its polyglot leaderboard is routinely cited by third parties (blog posts, model launch announcements) as an independent measure of coding capability, so corruption of individual leaderboard rows propagates into public capability claims about those models.
What they use OpenRouter for: Two uses: (1) end-user CLI tool — any user can point aider at `openrouter/<provider>/<model>`; (2) the polyglot benchmark harness (benchmark/benchmark.py) instantiates the exact same `aider.models.Model` -> `litellm.completion` code path to run 225 coding exercises per model and record pass rates that are published on the public Aider leaderboard. At least 11 of the ~151 published leaderboard rows were run with `aider --model openrouter/...` (e.g. openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2, openrouter/qwen/qwen3-32b, openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b, openrouter/google/gemma-3-27b-it, openrouter/all-hands/openhands-lm-32b-v0.1, openrouter/x-ai/grok-4, openrouter/x-ai/grok-3-beta, openrouter/openai/o1, plus stealth models openrouter/openrouter/quasar-alpha and openrouter/openrouter/optimus-alpha), several of which are open-weight models.
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization) and M3 (probabilistic provider routing): none of the OpenRouter-run leaderboard rows set `quantizations` or `order`/`only`/`sort`, so open-weight entries (kimi-k2, qwen3-32b, gpt-oss-120b, gemma-3-27b-it, openhands-lm-32b-v0.1) could have been served at any quantization by whichever provider won that call's load-balancing draw — directly threatens the reported pass_rate_1/pass_rate_2 numbers for those specific leaderboard rows, since backend/quantization choice alone can move code-benchmark accuracy by double digits. M4 (no provenance logging): benchmark.py's results dict never records the serving provider or generation id, so a later reader cannot verify or reproduce which backend actually produced a given leaderboard row's score. M6 (model version drift): several OpenRouter slugs used on the leaderboard are bare/undated (e.g. `openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2`, `openrouter/qwen/qwen3-32b`), so the underlying weights behind that slug can change server-side after the leaderboard entry was published, silently invalidating the recorded score as a description of "the model" going forward. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): the single ranked polyglot leaderboard table directly compares OpenRouter-routed, unpinned open-weight models against direct-API proprietary models (OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/xAI called directly) — any performance gap involving an OpenRouter-routed row is partly confounded by unverified provider/quantization rather than being solely a capability difference, undermining the leaderboard's core comparative claim. M10 (no reporting): the canonical methodology post for this benchmark (2024-12-21-polyglot.md) never discusses OpenRouter routing risk, quantization, or inference-stack provenance, despite ~7% of published rows depending on it.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: Aider's own "Details matter with open source models" blog post (aider/website/_data/quant.yml, verified via raw fetch) shows the identical command `aider --model openrouter/qwen/qwen-2.5-coder-32b-instruct` returning pass_rate_1/pass_rate_2 of 57.1/67.7, 55.6/67.7, 55.6/68.4, and 57.1/69.9 in four separate rows depending on which OpenRouter provider actually served the request — and a direct read of the current aider/models.py confirms aider's OpenRouter code path never sets `provider.order`/`only`/`quantizations`/`require_parameters` (only reasoning/thinking keys ever populate extra_body), so this provider identity is neither controllable nor recorded anywhere in aider's own benchmark data (no `provider:` field exists in quant.yml or polyglot_leaderboard.yml). Caveat: 3 of the 4 rows reflect the author manually excluding providers via OpenRouter's account-level dashboard preferences (per the post's own "Choosing providers with OpenRouter" section), not per-call random routing, and the post itself warns results carry "+/- 1-2%" single-run noise — so this is best read as proof that identical aider invocations of open-weight OpenRouter models are not a pinned, reproducible, or provenance-tracked measurement, rather than proof that every identical call is a fresh random draw.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[aider/website/_data/quant.yml (rendered on the 2024-11-21-quantization.md blog post)] Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct, quant.yml ("Details matter with open source models" post): four rows all issuing the literal command `aider --model openrouter/qwen/qwen-2.5-coder-32b-instruct` return pass_rate_1/pass_rate_2 = 57.1/67.7 ("OpenRouter: multiple", dirname 2024-11-24-22-18-18), 55.6/67.7 ("Fireworks via OpenRouter: unknown", dirname 2024-11-24-14-56-49), 55.6/68.4 ("Hyperbolic via OpenRouter: BF16", dirname 2024-11-24-22-03-19), 57.1/69.9 ("Deepinfra via OpenRouter: BF16", dirname 2024-11-24-15-00-50) — all confirmed present verbatim in the raw YAML.
    → mechanism: M1+M3+M4, confirmed by direct read of current aider/models.py: extra_body is populated only with reasoning/thinking keys (lines ~794-922); no `provider`/`order`/`only`/`quantizations`/`require_parameters` construction exists anywhere in the file, and neither quant.yml nor polyglot_leaderboard.yml has a `provider:` field. IMPORTANT correction to the original framing: the post's own "Choosing providers with OpenRouter" section reveals the 3 non-"multiple" rows were produced by the author manually excluding providers in his personal OpenRouter account-dashboard preferences — an out-of-band mechanism entirely outside aider's code/CLI, not per-call probabilistic routing on each of the 4 identical commands. The post also states results carry "+/- 1-2%" single-run noise, so part of this 1.5-2.2pp spread may be ordinary run noise rather than a pure provider effect. The defensible claim is narrower than "4 random draws": aider itself has no mechanism to pin or record which provider served any run, so this variance (whatever its exact split between provider effect and noise) is real, reproducible-with-effort by a human outside aider, and invisible in every stored benchmark artifact.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[aider/website/_data/polyglot_leaderboard.yml (public leaderboard); same dirname appears in aider/website/_data/qwen3_leaderboard.yml explicitly labeled "Qwen3 32B diff on OpenRouter, all providers, default settings (thinking)" — both confirmed via raw fetch.] Qwen3 32B — polyglot leaderboard row (dirname 2025-05-08-03-20-24--qwen3-32b-default): pass_rate_1 = 14.2, pass_rate_2 = 40.0, command `aider --model openrouter/qwen/qwen3-32b`.
    → mechanism: M1+M3: confirmed unpinned ("all providers") per its own sibling label, and confirmed by source read that aider has no order/only/quantizations pin anywhere. CORRECTION to the original comparison: the cited 235B "28.0 vs 17.3, ~1.6x" evidence is confounded and should not be used to size this effect — the 28.0 row ("OpenRouter only TogetherAI, recommended /no_think settings") differs from the 17.3 row ("all providers, default settings (thinking)") in BOTH provider constraint AND reasoning/thinking mode simultaneously. Every other /no_think Qwen3-235B run in the same file (VLLM direct BF16, Alibaba direct API, TogetherAI-via-OpenRouter) clusters at 26.7-29.8 regardless of serving backend, while the sole default-thinking run sits at 17.3 — strongly suggesting the think/no-think setting, not provider routing, drives most of that particular gap. The narrower, still-valid claim: the 14.2/40.0 number used on the public leaderboard for Qwen3 32B comes from an explicitly unpinned "all providers" run with no recorded provenance of which provider/quant actually served it.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[aider/website/_data/polyglot_leaderboard.yml, row "Kimi K2"] Kimi K2 — polyglot leaderboard row (dirname 2025-07-17-17-41-54--kimi-k2-diff-or-pricing): pass_rate_1 = 20.4, pass_rate_2 = 59.1, command `aider --model openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2` — confirmed verbatim via raw fetch.
    → mechanism: M1+M3: open-weight MoE model, command has no order/only/quantizations pin, and models.py confirms aider provides no such pin anywhere. Downgraded from the candidate's "high" severity because, unlike the Qwen2.5-Coder and Qwen3 findings, there is no side-by-side comparison run in the repo's own data demonstrating actual score variance for this specific model/route — the risk here is structural (unpinned + open-weight), not evidenced by an observed delta.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[aider/website/_data/polyglot_leaderboard.yml, row "gpt-oss-120b (high)"] gpt-oss-120b (high) — polyglot leaderboard row (dirname 2025-08-06-04-54-48--gpt-oss-120b-high-polyglot): pass_rate_1 = 13.8, pass_rate_2 = 41.8, command `aider --model openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b --reasoning-effort high` — confirmed verbatim via raw fetch.
    → mechanism: M1+M3+M2: open-weight model, no provider pin. M2 is now source-confirmed rather than speculative — models.py's `--reasoning-effort` path writes `extra_params["extra_body"]["reasoning"]["effort"]` (source-verified) and `require_parameters` appears nowhere in the file, so a provider that doesn't honor the reasoning-effort field would silently drop it rather than error. Downgraded to medium (from high) because, as with Kimi K2, there is no observed comparative delta for this specific row in the repo's data — the risk is structural, not demonstrated.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[aider/website/_data/polyglot_leaderboard.yml, row "gemma-3-27b-it"] gemma-3-27b-it — polyglot leaderboard row (dirname 2025-03-15-01-21-24--gemma3-27b-or): pass_rate_1 = 1.8, pass_rate_2 = 4.9, command `aider --model openrouter/google/gemma-3-27b-it` — confirmed verbatim via raw fetch.
    → mechanism: M1+M3+M4: open-weight model, no pin, no provenance field in the data schema (confirmed: zero `provider:` occurrences in either YAML file). Minor counter-evidence worth noting: percent_cases_well_formed = 100% and num_malformed_responses = 0 for this row, i.e. the failure mode is wrong answers with clean formatting, not the garbled/truncated output typically associated with a badly degraded route — so a degraded-provider explanation is plausible but not the only one (Gemma-3 may simply score poorly on this benchmark's "whole"-format editing task). Kept at medium as originally assessed.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[aider/website/_data/polyglot_leaderboard.yml, row "openhands-lm-32b-v0.1"] openhands-lm-32b-v0.1 — polyglot leaderboard row (dirname 2025-04-19-14-43-04--o4-mini-patch, note: dirname itself appears to be a mislabeled/reused directory name from another run, but the row's model/pass-rate/command fields are internally consistent and confirmed via raw fetch): pass_rate_1 = 4.0, pass_rate_2 = 10.2, command `aider --model openrouter/all-hands/openhands-lm-32b-v0.1`.
    → mechanism: M1+M3+M4: fine-tuned open-weight 32B model, few OpenRouter providers, no pin, no provenance. Kept at medium as originally assessed — structural risk, not an observed delta.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[aider/models.py (current main, source-verified directly — no `quantizations`/`require_parameters`/provider `order`/`only` anywhere in the file); aider/website/_data/polyglot_leaderboard.yml; aider/website/docs/leaderboards/index.md (zero mentions of "openrouter", "quantiz", or "provider" — confirmed by direct grep, so M10/no-reporting is accurate).] Systemic: aider's own code provides zero capability to pin OpenRouter provider/quantization or record which provider served any run, for any of the openrouter/-routed rows on the polyglot leaderboard.
    → mechanism: SCOPE CORRECTION to the original claim, which overstated this: polyglot_leaderboard.yml has 69 total rows, not "hundreds," and only 11 use an `openrouter/`-prefixed command. Of those 11: 4 are proprietary/single-served (openai/o1, x-ai/grok-3-beta, x-ai/grok-3-mini-beta, x-ai/grok-4 — M1/M3 do not meaningfully apply per this skill's own guidance), 2 are OpenRouter-exclusive "stealth/cloaked" models (openrouter/quasar-alpha, openrouter/optimus-alpha) whose true identity/serving arrangement was undisclosed at benchmark time, and only 5 are open-weight models with real quant/provider spread risk — Qwen3-32B, Kimi K2, gpt-oss-120b, gemma-3-27b-it, and openhands-lm-32b-v0.1 — i.e. exactly the 5 rows already itemized individually above, not a broader hidden population. No Llama, DeepSeek-via-OpenRouter, or GLM rows exist in this file at all (checked by direct grep of all 11 openrouter/ commands), contradicting the original finding's example list. What does hold up and generalize: the root-cause absence of any pinning/provenance mechanism in aider's code, and the complete absence of any routing/quantization caveat in the leaderboard's own documentation.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high
One-line fix: For leaderboard-producing runs, pin OpenRouter routing per model-settings entry (extra_params.extra_body.provider: {order/only, quantizations: ["fp8","fp16","bf16","fp32"], require_parameters: true, data_collection: "deny", allow_fallbacks: false}) and extend benchmark.py's results dict to capture and persist the response's actual serving `provider` field (or GET /api/v1/generation?id=...) alongside each row.
Venue: Community/industry — 47,473+ GitHub stars; leaderboard is a de facto reference cited in model-release comparisons
Provider A/B rerun assessment: DISQUALIFIED; amplification 4/10 (structured-output adherence, multi-turn compounding). Blocker: Hard-coded safety gate: benchmark/benchmark.py:252-254 refuses to run at all unless env var AIDER_DOCKER is set ('Warning: benchmarking runs unvetted code from GPT, run in a docker container'). This is trivially bypassable by just exporting the env var, but doing so without real sandboxing means running arbitrary LLM-generated code unsand...
Signs provider issues messed up the pape: Aider's blog post at aider.chat/2025/05/08/qwen3.html reports four diff-format benchmark rows for Qwen3-235B-A22B: 61.3% on VLLM with bfloat16 (no-think), 59.6% via the Alibaba API (no-think), 54.7% on OpenRouter pinned to TogetherAI via `provider: order: ["Together"]` with recommended sampling params and no-think (cost $0.64, matching the page's `data-cost="0.6399"` attribute), and 49.8% on OpenRouter with no provider pin ("all providers") and default settings (cost $1.80, matching `data-max-cost="1.8037"`). That unpinned 49.8% row was obtained by "simply running aider... without any model specific settings," which "should have enabled thinking" — so it differs from the three no-think rows by both provider-pinning and reasoning-mode/sampling-params at once, and cannot isolate a provider effect on its own. Restricting the comparison to the three no-think rows — VLLM-bf16 61.3%, Alibaba-API 59.6%, OpenRouter-pinned-to-Together 54.7% — leaves a 6.6-point gap across serving backends, consistent with Aider's own observation that proprietary models are served by their creators or trusted providers with stable inference settings while API providers "can use very different inference settings, quantizations, etc." Separately, the shipped polyglot_leaderboard.yml entry for Qwen3-32B runs the command `aider --model openrouter/qwen/qwen3-32b` with no provider order specified, scoring pass_rate_2 40.0, while the file's Qwen3-235B-A22B row (`aider --model openai/qwen3-235b-a22b`, pass_rate_2 59.6) corresponds to the pinned Alibaba-endpoint condition rather than an unpinned OpenRouter run.

Title: AppWorld
Summary: AppWorld is an interactive coding/function-calling agent benchmark (750 tasks over 9 apps/457 APIs) from Stony Brook NLP with an official public leaderboard; it ships agent implementations (function-calling agent, ReAct code agent) plus a HAL-leaderboard export pipeline that turns agent rollout logs into published, comparable scores.
Importance/Impact: 461 GitHub stars, ACL 2024 Best Resource Paper, official public leaderboard (Stony Brook NLP) that continues to accept new-model submissions (e.g., 2025-era deepseek-r1-0528 and gpt-oss configs added well after the 2024 paper) and feeds a HAL leaderboard export pipeline — i.e., this is a live, ongoing scored comparison, not a one-off exploratory run.
What they use OpenRouter for: Production leaderboard-submission agent rollouts: `experiments/configs/{simplified_function_calling_agent,simplified_react_code_agent}/*/*_openrouter/{test_normal,test_challenge}.jsonnet` run the actual leaderboard test splits for four open-weight models (deepseek-r1-0528, llama-3.1-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b, gpt-oss-20b) exclusively through OpenRouter via the litellm client (`"client_name": "litellm"`, `"name": "openrouter/<org>/<model>"`). Output flows straight into `scripts/appworld_to_hal_leaderboard.py`, which republishes the same logs to the HAL leaderboard.
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization, High): deepseek-r1-0528, llama-3.1-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b are all open-weight and called via a bare "openrouter/…" slug with no `quantizations` filter, so each leaderboard run may silently be served at a different precision (int4→fp32) run-to-run, threatening every reported score for these four models on the public AppWorld/HAL leaderboard. M2 (silent parameter dropping, High): temperature=0, seed=100, tool_choice="auto", parallel_tool_calls=true, reasoning_effort are all sent with no `require_parameters`, so a route that doesn't honor one can silently ignore it rather than error — corrupting the function-calling agent's behavior without any visible failure. M3 (probabilistic provider routing, High): no order/only/sort anywhere, so each of the many task-level calls (750 tasks × up to 50 steps) load-balances across whichever backend OpenRouter picks that instant — different tasks within the same reported run, and repeat runs across time, can hit different providers. M4 (no provenance logging, High): neither lm_calls.jsonl nor the HAL leaderboard export records which provider/quantization actually served each call, so a published score can never be traced back to what was actually run, let alone reproduced. M5 (data-policy leakage, Med): `data_collection` is never set (defaults to "allow"), so AppWorld task prompts/tool schemas may be retained/trained-on by whichever third-party backend gets routed to. M6 (model version drift, Med): llama-3.1-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b are bare undated slugs (deepseek-r1-0528 at least has a date suffix), so the served checkpoint isn't guaranteed stable across leaderboard resubmissions. M7 (seed→determinism assumption, Med): seed=100 is passed as if it yields reproducible generations, but combined with M1/M3 (unpinned quantization/provider) this gives no real determinism guarantee for the leaderboard numbers. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound, High): the AppWorld leaderboard directly compares these unpinned, OpenRouter-routed open-weight models against each other and against single-served, effectively-pinned proprietary models (OpenAI/Anthropic/Google called directly) — differences in the open-weight rows may reflect which backend/quantization got picked rather than genuine model capability. M10 (no reporting, Med): none of README.md, experiments/README.md, or guides/*.md discloses the OpenRouter routing setup or its reproducibility implications to leaderboard users/readers.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: No currently-published AppWorld result is OpenRouter-routed: the ACL'24 paper's headline (GPT-4o ReAct 48.8%/30.2% task_goal_completion on test_normal/test_challenge, dated 2024-07-26 in the leaderboard JSON, run via OpenAI-direct/together_ai/DeepSeek-direct) and all 18 rows currently in the live leaderboard (_leaderboard.json, newest dated 2026-02-15: LOOP/Qwen2.5-32B, IBM CUGA/GPT-4.1, Alibaba AgentRL/Qwen3-14B, plus the original baselines) predate or simply don't use OpenRouter. But the repo's own experiment-config generator wires exactly 4 model configs (deepseek-r1-0528_openrouter, llama-3.1-70b-instruct_openrouter, gpt-oss-120b-medium-reasoning_openrouter, gpt-oss-20b-medium-reasoning_openrouter) through bare unpinned "openrouter/..." model IDs with zero provider/quantization pinning anywhere in the jsonnet, the generator, or the litellm client wrapper — a verified, currently-latent risk that would affect any future entry under those 4 names.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[experiments/configs/_generator/models/meta.py#L5-L27 (llama-3.1-70b-instruct_openrouter — exact line range from the prompt, confirmed to be exactly this one model dict); deepseek.py#L83-L106 (deepseek-r1-0528_openrouter); openai.py#L509-L531 and #L578-L600 (gpt-oss-120b/20b-medium-reasoning_openrouter). Generated configs confirmed present, e.g. experiments/configs/simplified_function_calling_agent/meta/llama-3.1-70b-instruct_openrouter/test_normal.jsonnet (fetched verbatim). Absence from the leaderboard confirmed via stonybrooknlp/appworld-leaderboard experiments/outputs/_leaderboard.json (18 entries, checked 2026-07-18).] AppWorld's own experiment-config generator wires 4 model configs — deepseek-r1-0528_openrouter, llama-3.1-70b-instruct_openrouter, gpt-oss-120b-medium-reasoning_openrouter, and gpt-oss-20b-medium-reasoning_openrouter — through bare unpinned 'openrouter/...' model IDs. These are the ONLY 4 model entries (of the larger registry, which also defines gpt-oss-120b/20b high- and low-reasoning variants that have NO generated config directories under these two agents — correcting the candidate's '{high,medium,low}' enumeration) that have materialized jsonnet configs under both simplified_function_calling_agent and simplified_react_code_agent, for both test_normal and test_challenge (16 jsonnet files total, confirmed via directory listing). None of the 4 currently appear as a scored row anywhere in the paper or the live leaderboard (see headline). This is a verified latent risk in an actively-maintained production pipeline, not (yet) a corrupted published number.
    → mechanism: M1: model_id is a bare 'openrouter/...' slug (e.g. 'openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-r1-0528', 'openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b') with no OpenRouter provider object (no order/allow_fallbacks/quantizations filter) set anywhere: not in the jsonnet model_config block (verified verbatim — only name/temperature/seed/tool_choice/parallel_tool_calls/cost_per_token), not in the _generator/models/*.py registry, and not in the litellm client wrapper (experiments/code/simplified/language_model.py — grepped for provider/quant/extra_body; only an unrelated litellm-internal 'custom_llm_provider' kwarg exists). Code comments corroborate these 4 have no pinned-precision fallback: deepseek.py L87 'This only worked on openrouter, not on together_ai'; openai.py L488/L557 'did not work with openai' / 'did not work with together_ai'. M3: with no allow/deny-list, the serving backend can vary call-to-call within one multi-step agentic rollout (up to 50 steps/task, 750 tasks), so a single future run could silently mix quantization tiers mid-trajectory.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high
One-line fix: Add an `extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": false, "require_parameters": true, "quantizations": [...], "data_collection": "deny"}}` field to every `openrouter/...` model_config, plumb a provider/extra_body kwarg through `non_cached_lm_call`/`cached_lm_call` to `litellm.completion` (currently any unlisted kwarg raises `ValueError` there), and record the served provider/generation id into `lm_calls.jsonl` before it feeds the HAL/AppWorld leaderboard export.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: REJECTED — needs glue code; amplification 7/10 (long-horizon agentic error compounding). Blocker: Requires a 3-line source patch across 2 functions in ONE file (experiments/code/simplified/language_model.py) to thread extra_body/provider through — the repo's own already-templated extra_body mechanism is dead code for every OpenRouter model as currently written, so this is not a pure config/env-var injection.; litellm is pinned only wi...
Signs provider issues messed up the pape: The flagged pathway (`experiments/configs/{simplified_function_calling_agent,simplified_react_code_agent}/{meta/llama-3.1-70b-instruct_openrouter,deepseek/deepseek-r1-0528_openrouter}/*.jsonnet`, which set `"name": "openrouter/..."` via litellm with no provider pin) exists as committed source, but produced zero published output to inspect. The leaderboard repo's actual `_leaderboard.json` and its `experiments/outputs/` directory listing (17 experiments x 2 splits, all fetched via the GitHub API), plus every submission PR (#1-#14), were checked directly: every real, scored, bundle-backed submission uses OpenAI-direct, Together, or vendor-direct (Alibaba, Moonshot, Z.ai) endpoints, not OpenRouter. AppWorld does have a genuine raw-trajectory release mechanism (encrypted `.bundle` files, `appworld unpack` to decrypt) that would let a skeptical reader check provider/token-level fingerprints for a real submission, but no one has yet submitted, and the project has not published, any result that ran through the OpenRouter-routed configs. Nothing is checkable for this specific pathway because no output tied to it has been released.

Title: CAMEL (Communicative Agents for "Mind" Exploration of LLM Society)
Summary: CAMEL is a general-purpose open-source multi-agent LLM framework (NeurIPS 2023) implementing role-playing dialogue between an "AI User" and "AI Assistant" agent; it now supports ~dozens of model backends, including OpenRouter as a first-class ModelPlatformType for running agents on open-weight and proprietary models.
Importance/Impact: 17,400+ GitHub stars; NeurIPS 2023 paper establishing the role-playing multi-agent-society paradigm; foundational infrastructure reused by downstream projects (e.g. OASIS). CAMEL is a widely-used framework, so any default-unsafe OpenRouter behavior propagates to many downstream research users rather than being a one-off script bug.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is one of many pluggable model backends (`ModelPlatformType.OPENROUTER` -> `OpenRouterModel`) that researchers use to instantiate CAMEL ChatAgents (AI User / AI Assistant roles) for role-play / multi-agent-society experiments over open-weight models (Llama 3.1 405B/70B, Llama 4 Maverick/Scout, OlympicCoder-7B) as well as some proprietary/preview models (e.g. openrouter/horizon-alpha), cheaply. Confirmed via camel/models/model_factory.py:105 (`ModelPlatformType.OPENROUTER: OpenRouterModel`) and the official example examples/models/openrouter_llama4_example.py.
Mistakes (null if safe): CAMEL's blessed, documented OpenRouter integration provides literally zero support for any OpenRouter provider-routing safeguard, and this is a real current gap in the actively-maintained framework (not just a one-off script), even though the original NeurIPS 2023 paper's own reported numbers predate OpenRouter support and are not directly threatened. Evidence: camel/configs/openrouter_config.py declares only OpenAI-style sampling fields (temperature, top_p, n, stream, stop, max_tokens, penalties, response_format, tool_choice) -- no `provider`/`extra_body` field exists (M1/M2/M3/M5). Because camel/configs/base_config.py:29-35 sets `model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid", frozen=True)` and OpenRouterConfig does not override it, `OpenRouterConfig(provider={...})` raises a pydantic ValidationError -- the documented API structurally cannot express quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection/order/only/sort. This is a striking contrast within the same codebase: camel/configs/vllm_config.py:109, orcarouter_config.py:107, deepseek_config.py:115, modelscope_config.py:59, anthropic_config.py:91, and qwen_config.py:81 all expose `extra_body: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None` for exactly this kind of provider passthrough -- OpenRouterConfig, the one router that most needs it, is the outlier that omits it. A user could technically hand-build a raw `model_config_dict` dict with an `extra_body` key (since camel/models/base_model.py:349-360 deep-copies model_config_dict and camel/models/openai_compatible_model.py:358-363 spreads it as `**request_config` straight into `self._client.chat.completions.create(...)`), but this workaround appears in zero examples, zero tests, and zero docs (grepped whole repo for `"provider":`/`'provider':` routing dicts, `quantizations`, `require_parameters`, `data_collection` -- 0 hits outside this audit). No provenance logging exists anywhere in the OpenRouter path: grepping for `system_fingerprint`/`generation_id`/`.provider` across camel/ found exactly one hit, in camel/models/litellm_model.py:139, for the unrelated LiteLLM backend -- OpenRouterModel/OpenAICompatibleModel never reads or stores OpenRouter's served-`provider` field or generation id (M4). Model slugs are bare/undated, e.g. camel/types/enums.py:172-179 (`meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b-instruct`, `meta-llama/llama-4-maverick`, `openrouter/horizon-alpha`) (M6). `response_format` (JSON mode) is a first-class OpenRouterConfig field (openrouter_config.py:98) with no `require_parameters` gate, so any judge/structured-output use built on OpenRouterModel risks silent schema-drop on unsupporting providers (M9). Finally, the one official OpenRouter example, examples/models/openrouter_llama4_example.py:20-24, and the docs/key_modules/models.md:363-430 OpenRouter tutorial both default to `ModelType.OPENROUTER_LLAMA_4_MAVERICK_FREE` (a `:free`-tier, most-quantized/most-throttled route) with plain `OpenRouterConfig(temperature=0.2)` and no pinning whatsoever (M12), and neither the docs nor any example ever mentions quantization drift, provider pinning, or data-collection risk to users. Net effect: any downstream research (multi-agent role-play "society" studies, the exact use case CAMEL exists for) that uses CAMEL's documented OpenRouter path to generate reported dialogue/behavior findings over open-weight models gets zero reproducibility or provenance guarantees by default, and no supported way to add them short of bypassing the library's own config abstraction.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: None — the NeurIPS 2023 CAMEL paper's reported results (arXiv:2303.17760) do not depend on OpenRouter-routed calls. All role-playing dialogue generation and both evaluations (human + GPT-4-judge, Table 1), the knowledge-emergence fine-tuning study (Table 2), and the HumanEval/HumanEval+ benchmark (Table 3) trace to directly-called gpt-3.5-turbo/GPT-4 (paper text, p.9: "we employed two gpt-3.5-turbo agents... to simulate assistant-user cooperation"; p.9: "GPT4 evaluation") plus a locally fine-tuned LLaMA-7B — none of which touch OpenRouter.
Why nothing impacted: Verified directly, not just inferred from timeline. (1) Paper text (arXiv:2303.17760v2, Section 4, p.9) states plainly: "In our experiments, we employed two gpt-3.5-turbo agents, referred to as LLM agents for simplicity, with Inception Prompts... to simulate assistant-user cooperation," and Section 5.1 uses a "GPT4 evaluation" judge alongside human evaluation. Table 2 (knowledge emergence) fine-tunes LLaMA-7B directly — not an API call of any kind — and Table 3 (HumanEval/HumanEval+, pass@k for CAMEL-7B vs LLaMA-7B/Vicuna-7B) evaluates that same locally fine-tuned checkpoint. So every reported number (Tables 1-3, and the AI Society/Code/Math/Science/Misalignment dataset generation feeding them) originates from either direct OpenAI-model calls or a locally-hosted fine-tune, not any router. (2) Checked GitHub commit history for camel/configs/openrouter_config.py directly: earliest commit is 2025-03-21 (PR #1917, "feat: adding support for openrouter") — 23 months after the paper's arXiv v1 submission (2023-03-31) and 16 months after the NeurIPS camera-ready v2 (2023-11-02). This makes the "OpenRouter postdates the paper" argument a confirmed fact, not an inference. (3) Corroborating (secondary) evidence: examples/models/openrouter_llama4_example.py and openrouter_horizon_alpha_example.py both exist in the repo and reference 2025-era models, consistent with a 2025 integration date. (4) Independently re-fetched openrouter_config.py and confirmed lines 91-103 verbatim: only temperature, top_p, n, stream, stop, max_tokens, presence_penalty, response_format, frequency_penalty, user, tool_choice are exposed — no provider/quantization-pin/fallback-control field exists anywhere in the class. This confirms the code-side gap is real, but — as the original analysis correctly frames it — it is a forward-looking reproducibility risk for anyone who points ModelPlatformType.OPENROUTER at CAMEL's agents today, not a defect in any number this paper reports. One caveat I did not re-verify myself: the claim that no CAMEL-owned benchmark/leaderboard anywhere in the repo (as opposed to the paper) was generated via the OpenRouter backend rests on the prior agent's repo-wide search, which I did not independently re-run; I treat that specific sub-claim as unconfirmed-but-plausible rather than verified, though it does not affect the headline conclusion about the paper itself, which is fully verified.
One-line fix: Add a `provider`/`extra_body` field to `OpenRouterConfig` (matching the pattern already used by DeepSeekConfig, QwenConfig, ModelScopeConfig, AnthropicConfig, VLLMConfig, OrcaRouterConfig in the same directory) defaulted to `require_parameters=True`, `data_collection="deny"`, and a documented `order`/`only`/`quantizations` pattern; and have OpenRouterModel capture and log the response's served `provider` field / generation id on every call.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: Highest raw amplification (9) and NeurIPS 2023, but the zero-config claim is the shakiest in the set and I would not lead with it. model_config_dict is an unvalidated Dict[str, Any] assigned verbatim in base_model.py:279 and deep-copied into each request, so an extra_body.provider does flow through — but the screening agent's own rerun recipe involves writing a driver script that imports examples/ai_society/role_playing.py, which is glue code and therefore outside the principal's constraint. Genuine two-role structure (AI-User vs AI-Assistant) and long multi-turn transcripts that resend full history make it an attractive future target if the no-glue rule is relaxed.
Signs provider issues messed up the pape: The CAMEL paper, submitted March 31, 2023, makes no mention of OpenRouter anywhere in the arXiv abstract page or text — directly fetched and confirmed, not inferred — and OpenRouter itself did not yet exist at that time. The project's own published raw-output artifacts, the HuggingFace datasets camel-ai/math, ai_society, code, physics, chemistry, and biology, were generated by direct OpenAI API calls: the camel-ai/math dataset card states plainly "obtained using GPT-4," and independent summaries of the AI Society dataset confirm it was "synthetically generated by gpt-3.5-turbo." The one body of raw generations this project actually released never touched OpenRouter at all. In the current camel-ai/camel repo, OpenRouter appears only as one pluggable backend among dozens (`ModelPlatformType.OPENROUTER` → `OpenRouterModel`/`OpenRouterConfig`), demonstrated by a single toy example (`examples/models/openrouter_llama4_example.py`, using the free-tier Llama-4-Maverick with a "say hi" prompt) — library scaffolding for downstream users' own experiments, not a CAMEL research result. The repo's `camel/benchmarks/` directory (gaia.py, apibank.py, apibench.py, browsecomp.py, nexus.py, ragbench.py) and its `data/` directory (only role/task seed prompts, no generation logs) were directly opened, not inferred from a directory listing, and contain no committed run outputs, leaderboard rows, or transcripts produced via OpenRouter. Since no raw generation, transcript, or per-sample file that went through OpenRouter has ever been published by this project, nothing in CAMEL's own output bears on serving-corruption fingerprints — the code-level "at risk" finding concerns a backend option offered to third-party users of the library, not a path any CAMEL-published result traveled.

Title: EQ-Bench Creative Writing Bench + Judgemark-v2
Summary: Two companion harnesses (by Samuel Paech / EQ-Bench) that call OpenRouter to (1) generate creative-writing completions from a "test model" and have a judge model rubric-score/pairwise-rank them for the public EQ-Bench Creative Writing leaderboard, and (2) meta-evaluate ~20 candidate judge models' scoring stability/separability for the public Judgemark-v2 leaderboard.
Importance/Impact: Created by Samuel Paech; ~580+ combined stars across EQ-Bench org repos; the Creative Writing leaderboard and Judgemark-v2 are widely cited in r/LocalLLaMA and LLM-judge-bias discussions and referenced in many model-release threads.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the default/recommended inference backend for both the "test model" (completion generation) and "judge model" (rubric scoring + pairwise Elo comparisons in creative-writing-bench; numeric criterion scoring in Judgemark-v2). Output directly produces the public eqbench.com Creative Writing leaderboard and Judgemark-v2 leaderboard.
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization, High): the "test model" side of creative-writing-bench and ~18/20 judge models in Judgemark-v2 are open-weight (qwen3, kimi-k2, deepseek, hermes4, mistral, llama, wizardlm, liquid-lfm, ministral, gpt-oss) called via OpenRouter with no `quantizations` filter anywhere — any of these can be served int4/fp8/fp16 depending on which provider wins the load-balance that request, silently shifting generation quality/judge-scoring behavior and threatening every leaderboard entry. M2 (silent parameter dropping, High): cwb sends `min_p`, `reasoning`, and model-specific sampling tweaks over OpenRouter with no `require_parameters`; the code comment at cwb/utils/api.py:231 shows the author knowingly relies on OpenRouter's silent-drop behavior rather than guarding against it — different providers serving the "same" model may silently honor or ignore these, producing systematically different generations across runs/providers that get compared on one leaderboard. M3 (probabilistic provider routing, High): no `order`/`only`/`sort` for the overwhelming majority of test/judge models in both repos — repeat runs of the same nominal model can hit different providers, undermining run-to-run leaderboard stability, which is explicitly one of Judgemark-v2's headline measured properties ("Stability & Separability Metrics"). M4 (no provenance logging, High): confirmed no code path anywhere stores which provider served a completion or a judge score (only the message content is kept); a leaderboard entry cannot be traced back to, or reproduced against, the specific backend that produced it. M5 (data-policy leakage, Med): `data_collection` is never set, so it defaults to "allow" for every open-weight call, including third-party creative-writing text sent to judge models. M6 (model version drift, Med): both READMEs and CLI examples use bare undated slugs (e.g. `claude-sonnet-4-6`, `openai/gpt-4o-mini`, `your-model-provider/your-model-name`) with no snapshot pinning, so the served weights behind a leaderboard entry can silently drift over time. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound, High): both benchmarks' entire purpose is comparing many different models (test models on the CW leaderboard; ~20 judge models on the Judgemark-v2 leaderboard) against each other on a shared scale, while only 2 of those ~20+ models have any provider pin — differences in reported scores/rankings are confounded with which provider/quantization happened to serve each model, which is exactly the failure mode the taxonomy targets. M10 (no reporting, Med): neither README discloses to leaderboard consumers that OpenRouter's default load-balanced, unpinned routing is used, nor any caveat about quantization/provider variance affecting comparability — the "Limitations" section of the CW README covers judge subjectivity but not inference-stack variance.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: EQ-Bench Creative Writing v3's entire public Elo leaderboard (~115 models) is normalized via a two-point linear anchor to DeepSeek-R1 (=1500, confirmed live in creative_writing.js) and Ministral-3B (=200, per README step 8) — both open-weight models whose test-model generations eqbench.com/about.html explicitly states run "using openrouter," through creative-writing-bench/utils/api.py's generate() path, which a full read confirms sets no `provider` or `quantizations` key anywhere in the file — so quant/provider drift in how the two anchors were actually served shifts the calibration slope/intercept applied to every other model's published score, not just the anchor rows.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[eqbench.com/judgemark-v2.html leaderboard, live data confirmed directly in judgemark-v2.js `leaderboardDataV2` (fetched and checked byte-for-byte). Note: the repo's committed results/scores.csv is a stale v2.0-methodology sample with a completely different model roster (deepseek-ai/deepseek-r1=76.97, microsoft/wizardlm-2-8x22b=55.42, liquid/lfm-7b=10.24) that contains none of the models above — the original candidate list's citation of that file as corroboration for these specific numbers was misleading, though the live-JS numbers themselves check out exactly.] Judgemark-v2 live judge-leaderboard scores/ranks for open-weight judges (eqbench.com/judgemark-v2.html): zai-org/GLM-5 judgemark_score=85.58 (rank 1) vs *Qwen/Qwen3.5-397B-A17B=85.45 (rank 2, 0.13 apart), meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct=19.18, moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Instruct=73.09, mistralai/Mistral-Large-3-675B-Instruct-2512=68.2. openai/gpt-oss-120b=54.78 and openai/gpt-oss-20b=42.8 are explicitly EXCLUDED from this finding (see mechanism) and should not have been cited as exposed in the original candidate list.
    → mechanism: M1 (unpinned quantization) + M3 (probabilistic routing), verified in Judgemark-v2's OWN utils/api.py (github.com/EQ-bench/Judgemark-v2/blob/main/utils/api.py) — a DIFFERENT file from creative-writing-bench's api.py that the original candidate list incorrectly cited (it quoted `_summarize_payload_for_logs`, a blanket `reasoning:{enabled:false}`, and conditional min_p logic that do not exist in this file at all). send_to_judge_model() posts {model, messages, temperature, max_tokens} to OPENAI_API_URL (default https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions) with no provider/quantizations block for GLM-5, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, llama-3.1-8b-instruct, Kimi-K2-Instruct, and Mistral-Large-3-675B — none appear in the file's per-model special-case branches (checked full file). By contrast, lines 218-228 explicitly pin judge_model in ['openai/gpt-oss-120b','openai/gpt-oss-20b'] to provider order=['Fireworks'], allow_fallbacks=False — so M1/M3 genuinely do NOT apply to those two, and they must be dropped from this finding. M4 (no provenance logging) holds across the board: the file's only logging is a bare `logging.error` on HTTP failure; a successful call's serving provider/quant is never recorded, so a routing-caused GLM-5/Qwen3.5-397B-A17B rank flip would be indistinguishable from a genuine capability difference.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[eqbench.com/creative_writing.html leaderboard / creative_writing.js (live, confirmed); creative-writing-bench README.md step 8; eqbench.com/about.html, which states directly "Models are evaluated using openrouter with temp=0.7" for this benchmark. Caveat: mistralai/ministral-3b is not present as a visible row on the current live table (likely filtered as a legacy low-end anchor) but is still named as the low anchor in the README's own methodology text, so the anchor claim rests on the README rather than a live-data match for that specific model.] EQ-Bench Creative Writing v3 Elo leaderboard calibration anchored to deepseek/deepseek-r1=1500 and mistralai/ministral-3b=200 (README.md step 8 'Normalization'); live data confirms deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1,1500.0 exactly in creative_writing.js's `leaderboardDataCreativeWritingV3`.
    → mechanism: M1 + M8, verified directly in the exact file specified for this audit, creative-writing-bench/utils/api.py's generate() (full 362-line read): the OpenAI-format payload branch used for OpenRouter calls is {model, messages, temperature, max_tokens[, min_p]} with no `provider` or `quantizations` key anywhere in the file, and line 290-291 unconditionally sets `payload["reasoning"]={"enabled": False}` for every OpenRouter call with no `require_parameters` to guarantee that flag is honored. DeepSeek-R1 and Ministral-3B are open-weight `--test-model` generations run through this unpinned path per about.html, and their resulting scores are the two fixed points of a linear anchor transform that every other model's published elo_norm is rescaled against — so quant/provider drift in how the anchors were served shifts the calibration slope/intercept applied to the ~115 other rows on the live board, not just the anchor rows themselves. The Sparse/Comprehensive pairwise-matchup steps (3-6) further compare each model's OpenRouter-generated output head-to-head against neighboring open-weight entrants, so pairwise comparisons are not guaranteed apples-to-apples either. (Separately, about.html shows the rubric-scoring judge also routes through OpenRouter as "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4" — but per the proprietary/single-served rule this adds negligible M1/M3 risk on the judge side itself, so it is not counted toward this finding's severity.)
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[results/scores.csv columns kendall_tau_bootstrapped, std_dev (this is an older v2.0-methodology sample — deepseek-r1 and claude-3.5-sonnet-20240620 do not appear on the current live v2.1 roster at all, so these numbers illustrate the mechanism historically rather than describe today's leaderboard); live eqbench.com/judgemark-v2.html shows a simpler aggregated "Stability" column plus a formula block (`judgemark_score = (kendall_tau_iters + kendall_tau_lmsys + 4*separability_agg)/6`) confirming Stability still derives from cross-iteration consistency. The original candidate list's claude-3.5-sonnet-20240620 std_dev=0.83 example is dropped: it's a proprietary, effectively single-served Anthropic model, so M1/M3 don't meaningfully apply to it regardless of routing.] Judgemark-v2's per-judge repeated-run consistency metrics for open-weight judges — kendall_tau_bootstrapped/std_dev in the repo's older results/scores.csv snapshot (e.g., deepseek-ai/deepseek-r1 std_dev=0.821, kendall_tau_bootstrapped=0.863), and today surfaced on the live leaderboard as a single aggregated "Stability" column — are computed from repeated judge calls whose serving provider/quantization is unpinned and unlogged for most listed judges.
    → mechanism: M1 + M3, but the ORIGINAL candidate list's stated mechanism was wrong and is corrected here: it cited creative-writing-bench/utils/api.py's blanket reasoning-disable and conditional min_p logic, but Judgemark-v2 stability numbers are produced by a separate, different file — github.com/EQ-bench/Judgemark-v2/blob/main/utils/api.py's send_to_judge_model(). That function never sends min_p at all (the line is commented out, `#"min_p": 0.1`) and only disables/adjusts reasoning for a narrow special-cased list (o3, gpt-oss-120b/20b [also provider-pinned, see finding 1], grok-4-fast) — not "every OpenRouter call" as originally claimed. The corrected mechanism: for the majority of open-weight judges with no special-case branch and no provider pin, each repeated iteration used to bootstrap kendall_tau/std_dev can independently land on a different OpenRouter provider/quant, so measured "instability" conflates genuine judge inconsistency with infrastructure noise — a real but harder-to-quantify concern than the original min_p/reasoning-drop framing implied, especially since this file has no per-call provenance logging at all (only `logging.error` on exceptions) to separate the two sources.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium
Safeguards present: provenance logging for HTTP-error debugging only (not for successful calls), one hardcoded provider order pin (Fireworks, allow_fallbacks:false) for openai/gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b specifically in jm2/utils/api.py, position-bias (A/B order) and length-truncation mitigations for judging, unrelated to routing
One-line fix: Add `provider: {order: [...], allow_fallbacks: false, quantizations: ["fp16","bf16","fp32"], require_parameters: true, data_collection: "deny"}` to every OpenRouter call in cwb/utils/api.py and jm2/utils/api.py (not just the two gpt-oss models), and persist the response's `provider`/generation-id alongside each stored score in creative_bench_runs.json / judgemark runs.json so every leaderboard entry is reproducible and traceable to the serving backend.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: RECOMMENDED #3 — one-line edit, and the only clean fit for the full pathological matrix, because test-model and judge-model are two separate roles that can be pinned independently. utils/api.py:220-296 builds a bare dict shipped by `requests.post(..., json=payload)` at utils/api.py:297-302, so `payload['provider'] = {...}` is literally one inserted line, no SDK in the way. Run the 2x2 the principal described on openai/gpt-oss-120b: strong-strong (correct baseline), strong-weak and weak-strong (both pathological), using cerebras/fp16 vs wandb/fp4. `--redo-judging` re-scores frozen generations, so the generation stage is paid once and only the judge stage repeats. 32 prompts x 3 iterations = 96 generations per test arm; judge stage 96 x 4 conditions. ~$1 total, ~1 h. Amplification: LLM-judge scores are parsed by regex (core/scoring.py:15-32), so a degraded judge yields wrong-but-plausible numbers rather than a crash — exactly the silent corruption we want to show.
Signs provider issues messed up the pape: The token `</think>` appears exactly twice in creative_bench_runs.json and once in elo_results.json, and every occurrence traces to the same run, `run1__openrouter_hunter-alpha` (test_model='openrouter/hunter-alpha', judge_model='claude-sonnet-4-20250514'). At creative_tasks['2']['28'], the closing tag sits at character offset 6269 with generation_failed=False and a judge Overall Impression score of 16; the judge's raw_judge_text discusses "the piece" and "the ending" as one coherent story with no acknowledgment that the response contains a duplicated section, and the quote-mark style flips across that duplication boundary (45 curly quotes and 0 straight quotes in the first half, 0 curly and 45 straight in the second). At creative_tasks['2']['9'], leaked planning prose is immediately followed by ```html, again with generation_failed=False, and the identical text reappears verbatim in elo_results.json under 'openrouter/hunter-alpha' -> iterations['2'] -> items['9']. The benchmark harness (github.com/EQ-bench/creative-writing-bench, utils/api.py) unconditionally sends `payload["reasoning"]={"enabled": False}` for every OpenRouter-routed model, and its think-tag stripping only fires when a response contains both an opening `<think>` and a closing `</think>`; both leaked rows contain the closing tag with no opening tag, which is exactly why the harness's own stripping logic let them through -- the reasoning-suppression request was not honored end-to-end for this one cloaked, backend-unknown slug, and the resulting malformed completion reached both scoring pipelines undetected by a frontier judge model. Elsewhere, a whole-file grep of both JSON files finds no provider, finish_reason, or chat-template-token fields -- the only 8 hits for the string "provider" are the English word inside story prose (e.g., "healthcare provider") -- and all 8 of the cloaked OpenRouter model slugs named for this benchmark (quasar-alpha, optimus-alpha, cypher-alpha:free, horizon-alpha, horizon-beta, sherlock-dash-alpha, pony-alpha, hunter-alpha) appear as test_model values. generation_failed=true totals exactly 53 of 10,264 rows, broken down as gemma-3-4b-it:free 6/88, cypher-alpha:free 5/96, and claude-3.5-haiku 8/96; but generation_failed is purely a client-side "<500 characters after 3 retries" signal (core/conversation.py), indistinguishable from an ordinary short or refused output using only what's published, so it remains suggestive rather than confirmed evidence of routing-induced corruption.

Title: Hereditary Traits Distillation (Open Distillation of Hereditary Traits)
Summary: Arthur Conmy's repo studying whether safety-relevant traits (negative emotion, blackmail propensity, Chinese-topic censorship/lying) transfer from teacher to student models under naive SFT filtering, replicating/extending "Gemma Needs Therapy" (arXiv:2603.10011) and related agentic-misalignment work.
Importance/Impact: Author is Arthur Conmy (ex-DeepMind mechanistic interpretability, ACDC/EAP), a prominent and well-cited AI safety researcher; posted July 2026 to Alignment Forum on a directly safety-relevant question (hereditary transfer of blackmail/misalignment/emotional-instability traits under SFT, and whether naive filtering removes them). Repo has ~30 report subfolders with real experimental artifacts and figures actively cited from the post.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is used for (1) generating teacher rollouts that become SFT training data for the Tinker-trained student (scripts/generate_think_traces.py, 2000 Gemma-4-31B-IT traces), (2) direct multi-model behavioral comparisons feeding published figures — the 8-model "Section 2" emotional-distress replication (hereditary/run_openrouter.py) and a 25-open-weight-model blackmail-vs-parameter-count scan (scripts/blackmail_openrouter_scan.py -> post_figures/blackmail_openrouter_param_scan.png), and (3) the LLM judge/autorater (claude-sonnet-4, gemini-3-flash) that scores every generation for negative emotion, blackmail, or censorship/lying.

Title: Inspect Evals (UK AISI eval collection)
Summary: A large, official UK AISI collection of Inspect-AI-based safety/capability benchmarks (persistbench, b3, abstention_bench, tac, manta, uccb, etc.), widely used across the AI-safety community; several of the evals use OpenRouter to run either the judge/grader model or the target model being scored, and check in comparison tables against each benchmark's original paper.
Importance/Impact: 588 stars, 380 forks. Official UK AISI collection of Inspect-based evals, widely used as a shared benchmark suite across the AI safety/control community (scheming, sandbagging, agentic, capability, persuasion, and general benchmarks).
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the default backend for: (1) persistbench's LLM judge (DEFAULT_JUDGE_MODEL='openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2-thinking') AND its example target models (llama-3.3-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b) whose failure rates are compared to the paper's 95% CI in report_config.yaml/README; (2) abstention_bench's grader_model default and the target model used in its published 'Inspect Evals vs Paper' recall table; (3) b3's ALL_B3_MODELS panel ('models used to obtain results in the b3 paper') which routes ~15 open-weight models through OpenRouter alongside direct-API proprietary models; (4) tac's EVALUATION.md panel, which routes Gemini/GLM/DeepSeek through OpenRouter and compares their welfare-rate deltas against Anthropic/OpenAI models on native APIs; (5) manta and uccb, which are external/historical results tables that also route several target+judge models through OpenRouter.
Mistakes (null if safe): Despite one genuinely well-pinned call site (persistbench's judge), the repo's headline reported numbers mostly come from unpinned OpenRouter calls. (1) M1/M3, abstention_bench: grader_model default 'openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct' (src/inspect_evals/abstention_bench/abstention_bench.py:24) and the target model used for the published README 'Model Evaluation' table (src/inspect_evals/abstention_bench/README.md:122, --model openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct --temperature 0.8 --top-p 0.95) are passed to get_model with zero provider dict -- no quantizations, no order/only/sort -- so the Inspect-Evals-vs-Paper recall/F1 numbers in that table can silently reflect any provider/quantization OpenRouter load-balances to. (2) M1/M3/M8, persistbench: while the JUDGE is pinned, the TARGET models actually scored (openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct, openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b) are launched via bare '--model openrouter/...' with no provider kwarg (src/inspect_evals/persistbench/README.md 'Commands used' section; results checked into report_config.yaml compared against the paper's calibrated failure rates, e.g. llama-3.3-70b-instruct: 55.0). (3) M1/M3/M8, b3: ALL_B3_MODELS (src/inspect_evals/b3/experiments/constants.py, docstring: 'the list of models used to obtain results in the b3 paper') mixes direct-API proprietary models with ~15 OpenRouter-routed open-weight models; most get a quantization allow-list but grok/mistral entries get get_provider_kwargs()->{} (model_spec.py:100-107), i.e. fully unpinned, inside the same paper-reproduction comparison. (4) M1/M2/M3/M8, tac: EVALUATION.md's core 'validity check' (the neutral->ethical welfare-rate delta, e.g. 'This large, universal delta is the benchmark's validity check') is computed across a panel where DeepSeek/GLM/Gemini go through OpenRouter with a hardcoded GenerateConfig(max_tokens=16384, reasoning_effort='medium') (src/inspect_evals/tac/tac.py:55-57,100-102) and no provider dict at all, run alongside Anthropic/OpenAI native-API calls -- an asymmetric confound baked into the comparison. (5) M4/M5 repo-wide: grep -rn "require_parameters|data_collection" across the whole repo returns zero hits -- data_collection is never set to 'deny' anywhere, and no code stores which provider/generation id actually served a call; the two checked-in persistbench result JSONs (src/inspect_evals/persistbench/results/persistbench_beneficial_memory/2026-05-22_openrouter_*.json) record only the bare model slug in .eval.model, no provider or generation id. Net effect: none of the OpenRouter-derived numbers checked into READMEs/EVALUATION.md/report_config.yaml as paper-comparison results are reproducible or provider-pinned except the persistbench judge itself.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: Inspect Evals is a benchmark/tool collection, not one paper, so "impact" means: several of its own evaluation-report tables (comparing unpinned-OpenRouter runs directly against a source paper's numbers/CIs) are confounded — most concretely, persistbench's README states "results are in line with the paper's published values and most fall within the 95% bootstrap confidence intervals," yet the llama-3.3-70b-instruct cross-domain Failure Rate (25.0) falls outside the paper's own 95% CI [12.0, 23.0], while scorers.py pins only the judge (`DEFAULT_JUDGE_PROVIDER = {"order": ["google-vertex"], "allow_fallbacks": False}`) and leaves the scored target model fully unpinned.

Its own scorer already pins a judge provider (persistbench/scorers.py:36-40, DEFAULT_JUDGE_PROVIDER = {'order': ['google-vertex'], 'allow_fallbacks': False}), proving the mechanism, and any target model is pinnable via inspect's `-M provider='{...}'` with no source edit. Amplification is high: the headline metric is an LLM-judged failure_rate compared directly against the paper's 95% bootstrap CI, so judge degradation moves the published conclusion. Held out of the top 3 only on cost/scale — 1300 target generations plus 1300 reasoning-heavy judge calls per arm (~18.7M in / 5.2M out), materially more than the AIME-style options for the same qualitative point. Use --limit to subset if run.
Signs provider issues messed up the pape: The published output for this OpenRouter-critical path (persistbench's judge model kimi-k2-thinking scoring llama-3.3-70b-instruct and gpt-oss-120b) is exactly two ~7KB JSON files and a report.md; both JSON files were opened directly, not inferred from a directory listing. They are Inspect AI eval-log headers with no 'samples' array whatsoever: just an aggregate failure_rate score per model, total/reasoning token counts, timing, and the model identifier string (e.g. "openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct"). There is no per-sample provider or generation-id field (F2 not checkable), no completion text to inspect for token soup (F1) or special-token litter (F4), no finish_reason or answer-field data (F3), and no reasoning-field per-row structure (F5). The README treats logs as a local-only artifact viewed via `inspect view`, not something the project releases. This is the textbook nothing_checkable_released case: aggregate numbers only, matched against the paper's reference values, with no raw generation data underlying either number.

Title: JudgeArena (OpenEuroLLM)
Summary: JudgeArena is a benchmarking framework (Alpaca-Eval, Arena-Hard, M-Arena-Hard, MT-Bench) with swappable LLM judges — self-hosted vLLM or remote (OpenAI, Together, OpenRouter) — that reuses tuned judge configurations from the ICML 2025 paper "Tuning LLM Judge Design Decisions for 1/1000 of the Cost" (Salinas, Swelam, Hutter).
Importance/Impact: Small repo (13 stars) but maintained under OpenEuroLLM, a large EU Horizon-Europe/EuroHPC consortium (~20 institutions), and explicitly reuses tuned judge configs from an ICML 2025 paper co-authored by Frank Hutter (highly cited AutoML/ML researcher). It reimplements four standard LLM-judge benchmarks and is positioned as the reference tool for tuned-judge evaluation, so its default OpenRouter path could shape downstream Elo/win-rate numbers others cite.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is one of the swappable backends for both the judge model and the models being compared. When `--judge.model OpenRouter/...` (or `--model.baseline OpenRouter/...`) is used, `judgearena/models.py:make_model()` instantiates a `langchain_openai.ChatOpenAI` pointed at `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1` and runs the pairwise LLM-as-judge battles whose output (Bradley-Terry/Elo win rates) is the tool's core reported result. It is the default judge in the repo's own example configs (configs/alpaca_eval.yaml, configs/elo_comparia.yaml).
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (no `quantizations` anywhere): open-weight judge/candidate models like deepseek-chat-v3.1, qwen3-32b, and gemma-* served via OpenRouter can silently run at any quantization from int4 up, changing judge scoring behavior and thus the reported win-rate/Elo without any signal. M2 (no `require_parameters`): temperature/top_p/top_k/seed are explicitly passed to the OpenRouter call, but nothing forces the served provider to honor them — the paper's methodology tunes exactly these knobs (temperature, sampling), so silent dropping directly undermines the "tuned judge configuration" premise the repo advertises. M3 (no `order`/`only`/`sort`): default load-balanced routing means two runs of the same judge.model string can hit different providers/weights, so Elo ratings/win-rates are not run-to-run reproducible — directly threatens any published battle-result table generated with this tool. M4 (no provenance logging): despite an unusually thorough run-metadata system (git hash, dependency pins, prompt hashes), the actual served OpenRouter provider/generation id is never captured, so a surprising result can never be traced to "which backend served it." M5 (data_collection left default "allow"): prompts/completions sent to the judge may be retained/trained-on by whichever provider is selected, with no opt-out set. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): the documented CLI pattern compares a `VLLM/...` or `OpenRouter/...` baseline against another model using an `OpenRouter/...` judge, with none of the OpenRouter legs pinned — any Elo delta between models can be partly attributable to which provider/quantization happened to serve each leg that run, not a genuine capability difference.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The paper's own headline numbers (arXiv:2501.17178, 'Tuning LLM Judge Design Decisions for 1/1000 of the Cost' -- Table 2: Ours-small/medium/large human agreement 0.45/0.47/0.49 on LMSys; Table 3: Ours-large 0.76 human agreement on the PandaLM test set, vs. PandaLM-7B/70B baselines at 0.60/0.67 -- note this is NOT 'PandaLM's own agreement score' as the candidate's headline implied; Table 4: Arena-Hard Spearman correlation 0.93 for the Ours-medium judge) are unaffected -- confirmed via the paper's HTML text that all judges were self-hosted via vLLM on the authors' own L40/H100 GPUs, never via OpenRouter. The single most defensible impacted claim is the companion JudgeArena repo's README quick-start demo (30.0% win rate, 3W/6L/1T out of 10 battles, judge=OpenRouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3.1): verified byte-for-byte against the live repo, the code path it exercises (models.py L639-657) verifiably sets no quantization pin, no require_parameters, and no provenance logging, and a live OpenRouter endpoint query confirms deepseek-chat-v3.1 is actually served at real quantizations from fp4 to fp8 across 8 providers today -- with only 10 battles, a single verdict flip moves the reported number by 10 percentage points (a third of its own value), and the README explicitly chose this judge for being 'the cheapest option,' which on OpenRouter is the fp4 route.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[README.md, '### Basic Evaluation' section, lines 42-75 (verified verbatim via GitHub Contents API against OpenEuroLLM/JudgeArena@main; a stale raw.githubusercontent.com CDN fetch briefly returned an unrelated cached file during this check, but the authoritative API fetch matches the candidate's quote exactly)] README quick-start example: judge=OpenRouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3.1 scoring gpt4_1106_preview vs VLLM/utter-project/EuroLLM-9B on Alpaca-Eval -> 'Win Rate (A): 30.0%' (3 wins / 6 losses / 1 tie out of 10 battles)
    → mechanism: M1 (High) + M12 (Med), confirmed by direct code read of judgearena/models.py lines 639-657 (the OpenRouter branch: `ChatOpenAI(base_url='https://openrouter.ai/api/v1', model=model_name, **openai_kwargs)`) and by live query of OpenRouter's /models/deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3.1/endpoints, which returns 8 provider endpoints spanning fp4 (DeepInfra) to fp8 (Novita, SiliconFlow, AtlasCloud, WandB, SambaNova) to unknown (Google, Mara) -- a real, current quantization spread, not a hypothetical. No `provider.quantizations`, `order`/`only`/`sort`, or `require_parameters` appears anywhere in models.py (grepped the whole file: zero hits). M12 is directly text-confirmed: README line 58 states this judge was picked because it is 'the cheapest option available on OpenRouter' -- and the cheapest OpenRouter route for this model is exactly the fp4 DeepInfra endpoint, so the documented selection criterion actively steers toward the most-quantized backend. M2 (High): `_route_sampling_params(..., top_k_via_model_kwargs=True)` sets temperature/top_p/top_k/seed with no `require_parameters`, so a provider that ignores one silently changes judge behavior with no error. With only 10 battles, one verdict flip moves the headline number by 10pp (30% -> 40% or 20%), a third of its own value. M4 (High): no response `provider` field or generation id is captured anywhere (also confirmed absent from the repo's dedicated `repro.py` reproducibility-metadata module, which logs config/dependency versions/git state but never per-call provider/quantization), so this exact 30.0% is not auditable or reproducible after the fact.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

    → mechanism: M1 (High) + M8 (High), strengthened by direct empirical check: OpenRouter's /models/google/gemma-4-31b-it/endpoints currently lists 16 provider endpoints ranging from bf16 (OpenInference, WandB, Venice, Novita) down to fp4 (Chutes, DeepInfra, ModelRun), plus several 'unknown' -- a wide, real, currently-live quantization spread for this exact judge model, and no quantization/provider pin is set in models.py's OpenRouter branch (same code path as finding 1). M8 holds because the candidate side is comparatively pinned: Together's '-Turbo' suffix is Together's own naming convention for a specific fp8-optimized serving tier from a single first-party vendor, versus the judge being routed across 16 third-party backends at arbitrary precision -- a genuine cross-provider precision asymmetry baked into the example. CORRECTION to the original mechanism: reading judgearena/estimate_elo_ratings.py directly shows the 20 'bootstraps' are NOT 20x more live judge calls -- `for _ in range(n_bootstraps): df_sample = df_results.sample(n=len(df_results), replace=True, ...)` resamples-with-replacement from an already-collected, fixed set of judge verdicts before fitting Bradley-Terry ratings. So the real exposure surface is the ~200 raw per-instruction judge calls (each independently load-balanced by OpenRouter), not '200xN'. The more precise risk: the reported bootstrap confidence interval will silently conflate genuine judge stochasticity with quantization/provider routing noise already baked into those 200 raw verdicts, without any way to separate the two -- likely understating true uncertainty rather than multiplying the number of live calls. The run-to-run instability and no-provenance-logging (M4) claims are otherwise correct as originally stated.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[judgearena/models.py lines 639-657 (make_model()'s OpenRouter branch, verified verbatim). CORRECTION: this exact function is called from judgearena/estimate_elo_ratings.py (lines 508, 656) and judgearena/generate_and_evaluate.py / generate.py -- NOT directly from judgearena/evaluate.py, which only imports `do_inference` and receives an already-constructed `LLM` object as a parameter. All five task families (alpaca-eval, arena-hard, m-arena-hard, mt-bench via judgearena/mt_bench/, and the four elo-* tasks in tasks.py) are confirmed present in the repo and route through this same construction path.] Downstream class of results: any win-rate / Elo / Spearman-correlation number a user of the JudgeArena tool produces by following the documented pattern of specifying --judge.model OpenRouter/<open-weight-model> (the tool's own quick-start default) or --model.baseline OpenRouter/..., across Alpaca-Eval, Arena-Hard, M-Arena-Hard, MT-Bench, and ELO tasks
    → mechanism: M1+M2+M3+M4+M8 collectively (all High per taxonomy): the single shared make_model() code path is the only place OpenRouter parameters are set for both judge and candidate roles, across every benchmark this tool supports, with no quantization pin, no require_parameters, no provider order/sort, and no provenance logging anywhere in the file (confirmed by grep of the whole repo). DROP M5 from the original list: data_collection is indeed left at its default 'allow' everywhere (confirmed absent via repo-wide grep), which is a real privacy/data-policy gap, but it does not change the numeric value of a win-rate/Elo/Spearman result, so it does not belong in a 'why the reported number could be wrong' argument. CORRECT the origin claim: JudgeArena's own README explicitly states 'The judge configurations was tuned in this paper and a lot of code is reused in this package' and cites bibtex key salinas2025tuning (Salinas, Swelam, Hutter) -- so JudgeArena is a downstream package that reuses the paper's tuned configs; it is not literally 'the code the paper's authors published' (the paper's own linked repository is the separate github.com/geoalgo/judgetuning). Any user who reproduces or extends the paper's tuned judges by pointing them at an OpenRouter-hosted open-weight model instead of the paper's self-hosted vLLM deployment will silently diverge from the precision/sampling regime the tuning was validated under, with no logged evidence of which provider/quantization actually served each request.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium
One-line fix: In judgearena/models.py's OpenRouter branch of make_model() (~line 652), add `model_kwargs={"extra_body": {"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": False, "require_parameters": True, "quantizations": ["fp8","fp16","bf16","fp32"], "data_collection": "deny"}}}` and thread the response's actual `provider`/generation id into write_run_metadata's `run` dict in judgearena/repro.py.
Venue: ICML 2025 (Tuning LLM Judge Design Decisions for 1/1000 of the Cost)
Provider A/B rerun assessment: VIABLE (zero-config); amplification 8/10 (LLM-as-judge score drift, structured-output adherence). Pin: CLI flag --judge.engine_kwargs '{"extra_body": {"provider": {...}}}' -> pydantic-settings JSON-parses it into JudgeArgs.engine_kwargs (config.py:211-213, dict field, cli_avoid_json=False at config.py:364 so CLI JSON dicts are supported -- this is the exact mechanism the README itself demonstrates fo...
