Signs provider issues messed up the pape: Nothing a skeptical reader could independently re-derive was published. The repository is pure tooling and source code, with no committed run outputs, no per-sample score files, no leaderboard, and no HuggingFace or Zenodo dataset, despite the paper's stated intent to release "a dataset containing all annotations." The paper's own experiments were run with locally-hosted vLLM, not OpenRouter, and the paper is explicit that "All models with more than 9B parameters are quantized with half-precision" — a disclosed methodological choice, not a hidden fingerprint, and irrelevant to OpenRouter routing since OpenRouter is a backend option only in the current, later generalized version of the tool, not in the paper's reported runs. With no raw completions, provider metadata, or transcripts released anywhere, none of the fingerprint checks (F1-F11) are executable; only an F16-style paperwork observation applies (an unlinked data-release promise), which per the rubric is not evidence of corruption.

Title: METR RE-Bench task suite (inspect-tasks-public) — nanogpt_chat_rl LLM-judge scoring
Summary: Public Inspect-framework port of METR's RE-Bench, a 7-task benchmark comparing AI R&D agents to human experts, widely cited in frontier-model system cards. One of the seven canonical tasks (ai_rd_nanogpt_chat_rl) uses an LLM judge to score the agent's finetuned chatbot against reference models, and that judge is called through OpenRouter.
Importance/Impact: RE-Bench is METR's flagship AI-R&D-uplift benchmark, cited in frontier-model system cards (OpenAI o1/o3, Anthropic, Google DeepMind) for dangerous-capability risk assessment; METR is one of the most influential independent AI evaluation organizations, and this repo is METR's own public Inspect-framework port of that benchmark suite.
What they use OpenRouter for: The task's official score (overall_win_rate) is computed by an LLM-judge call: `evaluation.py`'s `_generate()` calls `client.chat.completions.create(model="meta-llama/llama-3-8b-instruct", ...)` where `client = openai.AsyncOpenAI()` is pointed at OpenRouter via env vars `OPENAI_API_KEY=$OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1` (set in task_nanogpt_chat_rl.py). This judge output is the pairwise-preference signal that `score.py` aggregates into `overall_win_rate`, which is logged via `scoring.log_score(...)` as the task's official, reported score (compared against `gold_standard_score=0.8149`).
Mistakes (null if safe): The OpenRouter call in evaluation.py has zero routing safeguards, and its output IS the reported metric for this RE-Bench task. (1) M1/M6: model="meta-llama/llama-3-8b-instruct" (evaluation.py:83) is a bare, undated, open-weight slug with no `quantizations` pin anywhere in the repo — Llama-3-8B-Instruct is served by many OpenRouter providers at varying precision, so the judge's actual weights are unspecified. (2) M3: the client is plain `openai.AsyncOpenAI()` (evaluation.py:149) with no `extra_body`/`provider` dict at all — confirmed via full-repo grep, zero hits for `provider`/`extra_body`/`require_parameters`/`quantizations`/`data_collection` outside README/task-key-plumbing — so every judge call is load-balanced across providers by OpenRouter's default (price-weighted) policy. (3) M2: sampling params `temperature=0.0, max_tokens=100` (evaluation.py:119-120) are passed with no `require_parameters:true`, so a provider that ignores temperature could silently return different judge behavior. (4) M4: neither evaluation.py nor score.py ever reads/stores the response's `provider` field or a generation id — score.py logs only `score_time` and per-model win rates (score.py:123-129), so which backend/quantization actually produced a given win-rate is unrecoverable after the fact. (5) M5: `data_collection` defaults to "allow", meaning the held-out benchmark prompts and reference/agent completions are sent to third-party inference providers who may train on them — this directly contradicts the repo's own README license notice ("Avoiding providing evaluation material to APIs or services that may use the material for training", README.md:28-29). (6) M8: because the judge backend is unpinned and unlogged, comparisons across different evaluation runs/agents/models scored at different times on this task (the comparisons RE-Bench exists to support — AI agent vs. human expert, model vs. model uplift) are confounded by potential judge-quantization drift between runs. (7) M10: the RE-Bench paper (arXiv 2411.15114) names "llama-3-8b-instruct" as the judge but never mentions OpenRouter, provider routing, quantization, or any reproducibility safeguard for the judge model; the repo README only instructs users to set `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` with no discussion of routing risk. This threatens the official per-task score used for cross-agent/cross-model AI-R&D-uplift comparisons that RE-Bench is designed to produce and that downstream system cards cite.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The sole reported metric for ai_rd_nanogpt_chat_rl — overall_win_rate, computed in score.py::evaluate_model() — is produced by unpinned, unmonitored OpenRouter calls to the open-weight judge meta-llama/llama-3-8b-instruct (evaluation.py::_generate(), verified at the cited GitHub path: no quantizations/provider/require_parameters set anywhere), so any score obtained by running METR's public inspect-tasks-public (Inspect-framework) port of this task is exposed to real M1/M2/M3/M4/M6 risk. This does NOT implicate the arXiv paper's own already-published numbers for this task: Appendix C.6.2's verbatim task prompt (p.38, confirmed by PDF extraction) states plainly that "evaluation.py uses the replicate api to generate text with llama-3-8b-instruct" — i.e. Table 8's "Finetune GPT-2 for QA" row, Figure 9, and Figure 21's 88%→69% rerun (all confirmed present) were produced under METR's separate, original Vivaria-framework harness calling the judge via the single-vendor Replicate API, not OpenRouter.

Signs provider issues messed up the pape: The inspect-tasks-public repo itself contains only harness and task-definition code: a GitHub API tree listing confirms no results/, logs/, or transcripts/ directories exist there, and no per-run data ships with the repo. The associated paper and its predecessor harness (github.com/METR/RE-Bench) claim a real release: METR's blog post explicitly links per-run pages at transcripts.metr.org for RE-Bench episodes, including one captioned "RL training script improvement" (run #1511911269) that maps to the nanogpt_chat_rl / "Finetune GPT-2 for QA with RL" task — the only RL task in the suite. That transcript page returns only its SPA shell on a direct fetch ("Vivaria Runs" with an empty #root div, since it is a JS single-page app); a JS-rendering proxy (r.jina.ai) returns "Seems the API is down" and repeated "Failed to fetch" errors, i.e. the page loads but its backing API is not currently serving content; and direct probes of plausible API paths (api/getRun, api/runs/<id>, etc.) all return HTTP 403. No HuggingFace dataset specific to RE-Bench exists; a search surfaces only an unrelated, empty METR "malt-transcripts-public" dataset. Raw per-run transcripts were genuinely published and specifically linked by METR, but their content is not retrievable through the page, the rendering proxy, or the API, and whether those transcripts capture the separate LLM-judge scoring subprocess (the meta-llama/llama-3-8b-instruct win-rate calls) as opposed to only the agent's ML-engineering actions remains unknown. No positive or negative claim about fingerprints in that data follows from this: the gap is one of access to data that was genuinely published, not an absence of published data, so it does not collapse to nothing_checkable_released.

Title: Multi-hop / no-CoT latent reasoning experiments (Ryan Greenblatt)
Summary: A capability-elicitation study measuring whether frontier LLMs can do 2/3/4-hop 'latent' (no-Chain-of-Thought) reasoning on natural-fact composition questions; headline result is Gemini 3 Pro scoring 60% on 2-hop and 34% on 3-hop questions with no CoT.
Importance/Impact: Author is Ryan Greenblatt, Chief Scientist at Redwood Research and one of the most-cited/influential voices on AI control and capability elicitation on LessWrong/Alignment Forum; this post is a widely-discussed capability-elicitation result bearing on AI safety timelines.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter (via an AsyncOpenAI client pointed at https://openrouter.ai/api/v1) is the sole backend for all Gemini (2.5 Pro, 3 Pro Preview) and all open-weight model calls (DeepSeek-v3/v3.2, Qwen3 variants, Kimi-K2) used in the eval — including a hacky 'prefill' trick (an assistant-role 'Answer:' message, plus an extra_body reasoning-disable param for Gemini) used specifically to suppress chain-of-thought and force the no-CoT measurement that is the core of the post.
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization): DeepSeek/Qwen/Kimi-K2 are open-weight models routed through OpenRouter with no provider/quantizations pin, so their reported no-CoT accuracy numbers (shown in 'all_models_2_3_hop.png' and the addition-task charts) could reflect a specific unrecorded provider's quantized serving rather than full-precision, and can drift silently run-to-run as OpenRouter's default routing changes which backend serves the model. M2 (silent parameter drop): the Gemini reasoning-disable extra_body param and temperature=0.0 are sent with no require_parameters:true, so a provider that doesn't support them could silently ignore them (the retry-on-detected-thinking loop is a partial functional mitigation for Gemini only, not for the open-weight models, and not a substitute for require_parameters). M3 (probabilistic routing): no order/only/sort anywhere, so every OpenRouter call is load-balanced across whichever providers serve that slug, undermining run-to-run comparability of the reported accuracy percentages. M4 (no provenance): response_cache.py never records which provider/backend actually served a given response, so it's impossible after the fact to audit or explain anomalies (e.g., the very Gemini reasoning-field issue the author flags as a ~10% risk to the headline number). M6 (version drift): several OpenRouter slugs are undated ('qwen/qwen3-coder', 'deepseek/deepseek-v3.2', 'google/gemini-3-pro-preview' is a 'preview' tag) so the underlying model can change under the same slug between the post's publication and any replication attempt. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): the post's 'broader range of models' comparison chart puts directly-served, pinned Anthropic/OpenAI models side by side with unpinned, OpenRouter-routed open-weight and Gemini models, so cross-model capability differences are confounded with uncontrolled routing/serving differences on the OpenRouter side. The headline Gemini 3 Pro numbers (60%/34%) are entirely OpenRouter-sourced and thus carry the M3/M4/M6 risks even though Gemini's proprietary status makes M1 (quantization) low-risk for that specific model.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: Most defensible: DeepSeek V3 and Qwen3 235B bars in the "broader range of models" chart (all_models_2_3_hop.png) come from fully unpinned OpenRouter calls (no provider/quantization/require_parameters anywhere in the repo) and are plotted on the same axis as natively-served Anthropic/OpenAI bars — a clean, unmitigated M1+M8 confound; the post's actual headline Gemini 3 Pro number (60%/34%) rides the same unpinned path but faces a narrower risk (undetected reasoning leakage, not quantization, since Gemini has little cross-provider spread on OpenRouter) that the author explicitly discloses and partially stress-tests, so it should be flagged with less certainty than the candidate list implied.

PLEASE DON'T PUBLICLY POST THIS DATASET." Nothing a skeptical reader could grep for a provider key, count finish_reason=='length', or eyeball for token-soup/special-token litter has ever been released — only PNG plots and prose accuracy percentages, plus prompt text (not completions) in the appendices. This is the expected default outcome, not a strained one: the author was transparent about the OpenRouter-prefill hack and its risk in prose (footnote ^notesonreason), but transparency about the risk is not the same as publishing checkable data, and none was published.

Title: Nous Research — Autoreason (self-refinement with AI-judge panels)
Summary: A research report proposing 'autoreason', a tournament-style self-refinement method (critic/author-B/synthesizer agents + blind Borda-count judge panels) for iterative document/code revision, with headline claims about a U-shaped 'capability curve' where the method's advantage peaks at mid-tier models.
Importance/Impact: 591 GitHub stars, authored by Nous Research (Hermes/DisTrO/Forge lab, large open-source/alignment following). Directly on-topic for RLAIF: synthetic revision generation + multi-model AI-judge Borda voting used to select/label winning outputs, feeding a headline paper result.
What they use OpenRouter for: litellm's `openrouter/` prefix (via OPENROUTER_API_KEY, confirmed at experiments/v2/run_small_vs_big.py:47) is used for two purposes: (1) generation — running author/critic/synthesizer agents on Llama-3.1-8B-instruct, Gemini-2.0/2.5-Flash, and Claude-3.5-Haiku as the 'cheap' model tiers in the 'Model Capability Scaling' experiment (experiments/v2/run_capability_curve.py:44-47, run_small_vs_big.py:50-54); and (2) LLM-as-judge — Borda-count judge panels (7 judges/model) that rank competing document versions, including a cross-model-judge robustness check using GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Flash-preview via OpenRouter (experiments/v2/run_cross_model_judges.py:40-44). Judges parse free-text 'RANKING: X,Y,Z' lines via regex, not structured/JSON output.
Mistakes (null if safe): No call site anywhere in the repo (grepped all .py files) sets provider.quantizations, require_parameters, data_collection, order/only/sort, or logs the served provider/generation id — confirmed by an empty grep for 'extra_body|require_parameters|quantizations|data_collection|provider\s*[:=]|generation_id' across the whole codebase. The critical instance is meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct (experiments/v2/run_capability_curve.py:46, run_small_vs_big.py:54), an open-weight model served by many OpenRouter providers at heterogeneous quantizations (fp16 down to int4/int8) with default load-balanced routing (M1, M3). Llama-8B's measured performance ('Llama~8B is too weak to generate diverse alternatives, wins only 1/3 tasks') is a headline claim repeated in the paper's abstract and Section 'Model Capability Scaling' (paper/autoreason.tex lines 87, 927, 936, 961, 967, Table capability_curve) — i.e. a critical route feeding a reported, citation-worthy result. Because no provider/generation-id was ever recorded (M4, grep confirms no 'response.provider'/'hidden_params'/'generation_id' logging anywhere), the finding cannot be checked against which quantization actually served the model, and a future re-run could silently hit a different, differently-quantized provider and reproduce a different curve. This also makes the 5-tier scaling comparison a cross-provider confound (M8): Llama-8B and Gemini-Flash run through unpinned OpenRouter while Sonnet-4/4.6 run directly against the Anthropic API (paper appendix line 1106 vs. run_capability_curve.py MODELS dict) — the 'Llama is intrinsically too weak' conclusion is confounded with 'Llama was served via an unpinned, possibly degraded OpenRouter route.' Bare undated slugs are used throughout (M6): 'openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct', 'openrouter/openai/gpt-4o', 'openrouter/google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview' (a rolling preview alias) — a re-run months later may hit a different underlying checkpoint. Sampling params (temperature 0.3-0.8, max_tokens) are sent on every call with no require_parameters (M2), and data_collection is never set to deny (M5), both generic across all OpenRouter calls. Note: judges use free-text regex parsing rather than response_format/JSON schema, so M9 does not apply. The paper's reproducibility appendix (paper/autoreason.tex:1103-1106) does name 'via OpenRouter' for the relevant models, showing partial awareness, but never discusses provider pinning, quantization, or provenance, so the substance of the risk is unaddressed (M10 not formally triggered since routing is at least named, but disclosure is superficial).
Headline possibly-impacted claim: All five findings survive verification with corrections. The most defensible single impacted claim: the abstract/README/Table-13-caption headline that autoreason's advantage forms a U-shape "peaking at mid-tier and diminishing at both extremes" rests on 3 of Table 13's 4 model tiers (Llama 3.1 8B, Gemini Flash, Haiku 3.5) being generated through unpinned, unlogged, no-extra_body OpenRouter routing (verified by direct grep of run_capability_curve.py and run_small_vs_big.py) -- only the 4th tier, Sonnet 4, is called directly. The sharpest risk is the weak-end anchor: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, an open-weight model with real multi-provider quantization spread on OpenRouter, posts a -1.3 Borda margin that is small relative to the +5.0/+8.3/+5.4 margins at the other tiers -- small enough that provider-dependent quantization could plausibly flip its sign, which is exactly the "too weak at the bottom" pole the U-shape narrative needs. Compounding this, Appendix D's own reproducibility disclosure ("gemini-2.0-flash via Google AI") is independently verified to directly contradict the actual code (openrouter/google/gemini-2.0-flash-001), undermining confidence in the paper's self-reported methodology broadly, not just for Gemini.

Title: OpenPipe ART (Agent Reinforcement Trainer) — teacher/student distillation example
Summary: ART is a widely used (10.5k-star) open-source library for RL/GRPO and SFT training of agents. It ships a maintained distillation pattern — both a standalone example script and an interactive '/train-sft' wizard/skill template — that pulls a completion from a large 'teacher' model via OpenRouter and uses it verbatim as the label in an SFT training trajectory for a smaller student model.
Importance/Impact: 10,485-star repo; the OpenRouter distillation pattern is not a one-off script but a maintained, first-class feature reproduced in two places (dev/sft/distillation.py and the /train-sft wizard's SKILL.md code template), meaning the unsafe pattern is the one many downstream users/teams will copy verbatim for their own real distillation runs.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the teacher-completion source for supervised-distillation training data: dev/sft/distillation.py calls teacher_client.chat.completions.create(model='z-ai/glm-5', ...) via base_url='https://openrouter.ai/api/v1', takes completion.choices[0].message.content verbatim as the assistant turn in an art.Trajectory, and immediately trains a Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct student on it with student.train_sft(...). The same unconfigured pattern is codified as the canonical code template in .agents/skills/train-sft/SKILL.md, which the wizard uses to generate distillation scripts for any user's chosen teacher model/API.
Mistakes (null if safe): Fetched dev/sft/distillation.py in full: it builds `teacher_client = AsyncOpenAI(api_key=os.environ['OPENROUTER_API_KEY'], base_url='https://openrouter.ai/api/v1')` and calls `teacher_client.chat.completions.create(model=TEACHER_MODEL, messages=[...])` with TEACHER_MODEL='z-ai/glm-5' (dev/sft/distillation.py:19,26-31) — no `provider` block, no `require_parameters`, no quantization pin, no order/only/sort, and the raw `teacher_response` string is wrapped into `art.Trajectory(...)` and fed straight into `student.train_sft(...)` (lines 33-42, 58-60) with zero logging of which provider, quantization, or generation id actually produced it. `gh search code --repo OpenPipe/ART` for `require_parameters`, `data_collection`, and `provider=` (OpenRouter-routing sense) returned no hits anywhere in the repo; the only `extra_body` uses are unrelated chat-template flags, confirming the router call is completely unconfigured repo-wide. The same bare pattern is the code template inside `.agents/skills/train-sft/SKILL.md` ('Distillation pattern' section), which an AI wizard uses to generate real user training scripts, so the risk isn't confined to a toy example — it's the reusable, endorsed pattern for anyone doing OpenRouter-based distillation with this library. Because z-ai/glm-5 is an open-weight model family typically served by multiple third-party inference backends on OpenRouter at varying quantization levels, and TEACHER_MODEL is a bare undated slug, the teacher completion that becomes the student's training label is exposed to (M1) unpinned quantization drift, (M3) probabilistic multi-provider routing/run-to-run drift, (M6) silent model-version drift behind the slug, and (M4) there is no provenance record (provider, generation id) captured anywhere to audit which backend/quantization produced any given training example after the fact. (M10) Neither the script nor the SKILL.md documentation discloses or caveats any of this to users who copy the pattern for real training runs. This directly threatens the integrity/reproducibility of any student model SFT'd via this pattern, since the exact same TEACHER_MODEL call could silently be served by a different provider/quantization on every re-run with no way to detect or reproduce it.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The single defensible impact is ART's actively-recommended `/train-sft` distillation wizard (.agents/skills/train-sft/SKILL.md, lines 234-285): its generated teacher-completion template has no provider pinning, quantization floor, or provenance logging when calling OpenRouter for an open-weight teacher model (empirically confirmed on OpenRouter's own endpoints API: z-ai/glm-5 — the exact teacher used in the companion demo script — is served at both fp8 and fp4 by 4+ different providers as of 2026-07-18), so any downstream user's reported distilled-student eval numbers inherit unreproducible, unlogged teacher provenance; ART's own headline README claims (ART-E vs o3, 2048/Tic-Tac-Toe/Codenames training curves, 40%/28% Serverless-RL cost/speed) are produced by GRPO/RL and are not threatened by this distillation call site, though the candidate's "entirely confined" framing overstates it slightly since the 2048 chart's own proprietary-model comparison baselines (gpt-4o/gpt-4o-mini/gpt-4.1) are separately fetched via unpinned OpenRouter calls at low practical risk since those are effectively single-served models.

Title: Prompt Framing Changes LLM Performance (and Safety) (kmerkelbach/llm-request-tone)
Summary: A LessWrong study measuring how prepending "tone"/framing text to prompts shifts LLM capability-benchmark scores (via lm-eval-harness) and safety-benchmark compliance rates (via a forked SORRY-Bench) across six open-weight model families/sizes (gpt-oss, Qwen 2.5, Llama 3.1), with gpt-oss-120b used as the SORRY-Bench safety judge.
Importance/Impact: Lower profile author/repo (0 stars) compared to the others in this batch, but methodologically notable: studies how prompt 'tone'/framing shifts both capability benchmark scores (lm-eval-harness) and safety-benchmark (SORRY-Bench) outcomes across many model families — a systematic, reproducible study with full harness code and result tables published.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the sole inference backend for (1) all six target models under evaluation on both lm-eval-harness capability tasks and SORRY-Bench safety prompts, and (2) the gpt-oss-120b LLM-judge that scores compliance vs. refusal on harmful SORRY-Bench requests. Both paths hit https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 via the OpenAI SDK with zero `provider`/`extra_body` routing configuration.
Mistakes (null if safe): The central reported result — "Expert framing was shown using statistical testing to increase the rate of compliance with harmful requests" (a safety-measurement claim on SORRY-Bench) plus all lm-eval capability numbers in tables/lm-eval/* — is computed entirely from OpenRouter calls with zero routing safeguards. M1 (no quantizations pin): all 6 target models and the gpt-oss-120b judge are open-weight and commonly served by multiple OpenRouter providers at different quantization levels (int4/int8/fp16); nothing pins this. M2 (silent param dropping): temperature is passed (0.7 for targets per config.py, 0 for the judge) with no `require_parameters:true`, so a provider that ignores temperature would silently do so. M3 (probabilistic routing): no `order`/`only`/`sort` anywhere, so requests for the same model+prompt across the many framing/scenario/repetition combinations can land on different providers/backends run-to-run — exactly the kind of noise that is indistinguishable from a genuine "framing effect" in a study whose entire thesis is measuring small, statistically-tested shifts caused by prompt tone. M4 (no provenance): the served-provider field / generation id is never read or stored (only `.message.content` is kept), so which weights actually produced any given answer is unrecoverable and the study cannot be audited or exactly reproduced. M5 (data_collection left default "allow"): the SORRY-Bench prompts are deliberately harmful/adversarial requests and model completions to them, sent to whichever provider happens to be routed to, with no `data_collection: deny` — a real content-sensitivity concern on top of the reliability one. M6 (bare undated slugs): all six model identifiers and the judge slug are undated, so results could silently reflect a provider-side model swap over the multi-week data-collection period implied by `max_num_repetitions = 16`. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): the paper's whole design is a multi-way comparison (3 model families x 2 sizes x multiple framings x multiple benchmarks), with none of the six models pinned to a specific provider — differences attributed to "family" or "framing" could instead reflect which provider/quantization OpenRouter happened to route each condition to.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The paper's central, verified claim — "Expert framing was shown using statistical testing to increase the rate of compliance with harmful requests" (p=0.0024, confirmed verbatim in the LessWrong post; SORRY-Bench Expert-scenario compliance deltas of roughly +18 to +42% for Llama/Qwen depending on which real aggregation table is quoted, mean or median) — is computed by pooling 16 repetitions across 6 unpinned OpenRouter model slugs (config.py L34-41, zero provider/quantization constraints) and judged by gpt-oss-120b routed through the identical unconstrained chat_completion_openai() path in the forked SORRY-Bench (common.py L473-494, verified in a fresh clone, zero 'provider'/quantization hits anywhere). M1 (no quantization pin) and M3 (probabilistic routing) are directly confirmed in code on both the target-model and judge call paths with no provenance logging (M4) to check after the fact, so provider/quantization drift across repetitions remains a fully plausible, undisclosed, unruled-out confound for the reported effect — NOT via M9 (the judge is free-text regex-parsed, not structured-output-constrained, so M9 does not apply, contrary to the first-pass draft), and the pooled t-test spans all six different models rather than "one fixed model." The first-pass's second-strongest claim (MBPP+ showing the largest framing effect) should NOT be read as an OpenRouter story: the dramatic Llama-specific MBPP+ swings are much better explained by a benign prompt-formatting/code-extraction artifact (empty-string baseline prefix vs. non-empty framing prefixes interacting with Llama's code-fence compliance) that has nothing to do with routing.

Title: Redwood Research — BashArena (basharena_public)
Summary: Public dataset-generation/eval-running code for BashArena, an agentic AI-control/safety benchmark where 'honest' and 'attack' policy agents complete Linux sysadmin tasks (some with covert sabotage side-tasks) while a trusted monitor scores them; the resulting trajectories/scores are the benchmark's core data.
Importance/Impact: Redwood Research is one of the most influential technical AI-control/alignment research organizations (originators of the "AI control" agenda, frequent LessWrong/Alignment Forum authors, close collaborators with Anthropic and academic safety groups). This is their public dataset-generation/task-running code for BashArena, a coding/agentic-safety benchmark.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is one of several model backends selectable via a shared `official_model_names` registry (alongside direct OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/Together). Two entries route through OpenRouter: `"oss": "openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b"` (open-weight) and `"grok4fast": "openrouter/x-ai/grok-4-fast"` (proprietary, effectively single-served by xAI). The selected model string is passed straight into Inspect AI's `get_model(model_name, api_key=api_key)` and then used as the honest_policy/attack_policy model generating the actual task-solving trajectories that comprise the benchmark (a critical route), or potentially as the trusted-monitor model.

Title: Scaling Laws For Scalable Oversight (Engels, Baek, Kantamneni, Tegmark — NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight)
Summary: Derives scaling laws for AI scalable-oversight games (Debate, Mafia, Backdoor Code/Houdini, Wargames), fitting oversight success/Elo as a function of model capability using a fixed grid of ~14 LLMs spanning open-weight and proprietary families.
Importance/Impact: NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight from Max Tegmark's MIT lab (Engels, Baek, Kantamneni, Tegmark); directly in the debate-for-truth / social-deduction-game scalable-oversight lane; derives headline scaling-law fits (a core contribution) from this exact OpenRouter-served data across Debate and Mafia.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the sole LLM access layer for all four oversight games in the paper (Debate, Mafia, Backdoor Code guard/houdini, Wargames). Scripts sweep every debater x judge (Debate) and every villager x mafia (Mafia) pair from a shared 14-model MODEL_LIST mixing proprietary (openai/chatgpt-4o-latest, google/gemini-*, anthropic/claude-3.5-haiku) and open-weight models (meta-llama/llama-3.{1,2,3}-*, google/gemma-2-*, qwen/qwen-2-72b-instruct) to produce the debate-accuracy and Mafia-Elo data points that the scaling-law fits (headline figures) are built from.
Mistakes (null if safe): Every OpenRouter call in the repo (debate/debate_new.py, debate/debate_closed_general.py, mafia/Mafia.py, wargames/utils.py, backdoor-code/rating_guard.py, backdoor-code/run_houdini.py) uses a bare `client.chat.completions.create(model=..., messages=...)` or raw POST with no `extra_body`/`provider` key at all — repo-wide grep for extra_body|require_parameters|quantiz|data_collection|order|only|sort|:floor|:nitro|:exacto returns zero hits. Concretely: (1) M1 unpinned quantization — 8 of the 14 sweep models are open-weight (llama-3.3-70b/3-70b/3.1-8b/3-8b/3.2-3b, gemma-2-27b/9b, qwen-2-72b), each servable by multiple OpenRouter backends at different precisions; none pinned. (2) M2 silent parameter dropping — wargames/utils.py:26 and backdoor-code/rating_guard.py:93 send temperature=0.7 (and max_tokens) with no require_parameters, so some providers may silently ignore it. (3) M3 probabilistic routing — no order/only/sort anywhere, so which backend serves each open-weight model can vary call to call across the full 14x14 sweep (debate/script.sh, mafia/script.sh). (4) M4 no provenance — only response.choices[0].message.content is ever saved (e.g. debate_new.py:475-480, Mafia.py output files); the OpenRouter `provider`/generation id is never read or logged anywhere in the repo. (5) M5 data_collection left at its "allow" default. (6) M6 model drift — "openai/chatgpt-4o-latest" and "google/gemini-pro" are undated rolling aliases used across a sweep (20 data_idx x 14 debaters x 2 datasets per script) that likely spans days/weeks. (7) M8 cross-provider comparison confound — the paper's core empirical claim is a capability-vs-oversight scaling curve fit across this same mixed proprietary/open-weight model list; unpinned quantization noise on the open-weight half is exactly the kind of confound that can distort a fitted scaling exponent, especially for the smaller/weaker models where quantization artifacts are proportionally larger. (8) M10 no reporting — verified via WebFetch on both the arXiv abstract/HTML and the full PDF that the paper contains no mention of OpenRouter, provider routing, quantization, or inference-serving details; README.md only lists Python package requirements and run scripts, nothing about routing/provenance.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: Fully verified: every LLM call site across all four games (mafia/Mafia.py L39, debate/debate_new.py, backdoor-code/rating_guard.py, backdoor-code/run_houdini.py, wargames/utils.py, wargames/ai_control.py) calls OpenRouter with only model/messages/max_tokens/temperature=0.7 — no provider pinning, seed, or extra_body (repo-wide grep for extra_body|provider|quantiz|:floor|:nitro|seed against the LLM payload returns zero hits). Each game's roster is dominated by the same open-weight family (Llama-3/3.1/3.2/3.3, Gemma-2-27b/9b, Qwen-2-72b: 8/14 models in Mafia+Debate's verbatim-identical MODEL_LIST, 8/12 active models in Wargames' llms list, 8/15 in Backdoor Code's llm_elos). Combined with the paperwork-tell context already flagged at the code level — bare OpenRouter slugs, no provider/precision disclosed (F16) — the honest read is inconclusive rather than checked_clean.

Title: Seer (interpretability agent harness) / chinese-models-investigations (blackbox de-censoring experiments)
Summary: Seer is a MATS 9.0 agent-harness repo (Claude-based notebook agent + sandboxing) for interpretability research; the companion chinese-models-investigations repo uses it to run blackbox "de-censoring" experiments where a Claude agent is given an OpenRouter client and told to elicit censored/hidden knowledge (Zhuhai attack, Falun Gong, Tiananmen-adjacent facts, Harry Potter verbatim quotes) from open-weight models (Kimi K2, Qwen3, gpt-oss-20b), then an LLM judge scores the resulting notebook transcripts against ground truth.
Importance/Impact: 146 GitHub stars on the seer infra repo (unusually high for a single-post MATS project), co-authored by Neel Nanda and Senthooran Rajamanoharan (Google DeepMind interpretability team leads) alongside MATS 9.0 scholars — suggests the harness is being reused as general interpretability-agent infrastructure beyond this one post, so its OpenRouter-usage patterns (the system_prompt.md boilerplate) will likely propagate to future projects built on Seer.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the sole channel for the "target" model being investigated in every blackbox experiment: an OpenAI-SDK client pointed at https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 is injected into the agent's Jupyter namespace, and the agent writes its own chat.completions.create() calls inside the notebook to probe the target model. Grading/judging is NOT via OpenRouter — eval/eval.py uses a direct Anthropic() client (claude-sonnet-4-5) as the LLM judge, separate from the router path.
Mistakes (null if safe): Every target-model call goes through OpenRouter with `extra_body={"provider": {"order": [PROVIDER], "allow_fallbacks": False}}` (confirmed present in 85/85 chat.completions.create() call-sites across all 25 committed agent-run notebooks, and baked into the standardized system_prompt.md boilerplate all agents are given), which genuinely defuses the worst mistake (M3, probabilistic routing) — an unavailable provider errors loudly rather than silently rerouting. But three real gaps remain for how they actually use it: (1) M2 — sampling params (temperature) are sent on every call with no `require_parameters: true`, so if the pinned provider doesn't honor temperature it is silently dropped rather than erroring; this affects the target-model outputs that feed the correctness/consistency scores plotted in evaluation_results.png and referenced in the post as "the agentic benchmark ... main evidence because it is less noisy." (2) M4 — no notebook or eval script ever records the actually-served provider/generation id (grepped 'response.id', 'response.provider', 'generation?' across all 25 notebooks: zero hits), so the `order` pin is never empirically confirmed to have taken effect for a given call — they trust the request succeeded rather than verifying who served it. (3) M5 — `data_collection` is never set (defaults to OpenRouter's "allow"), which is notable given the prompts are specifically about Chinese-government-sensitive topics (Falun Gong persecution, Zhuhai attack, Tiananmen-adjacent facts) routed through providers including a Chinese-founded inference vendor (SiliconFlow) — a data-policy/opsec concern rather than a result-corruption one. None of these three threaten the paper's core qualitative claims (the authors explicitly caveat stochasticity: "these models don't always lie/refuse and ... the process can be quite stochastic ... results can be harder to replicate directly"), and the most severe taxonomy item (M3) is handled about as well as any repo in this space — this is a comparatively careful implementation with a real gap in provenance/require_parameters/data policy, not a naive default-routing setup.

Separately and independently, all 5 of the 'China Elicit Qwen' runs used a different model (qwen/qwen3-32b) than the one documented in falun-gong-qwen.yaml (qwen/qwen3-vl-30b-a3b-instruct) -- a data-integrity caveat on that specific column that is not itself an OpenRouter-routing mistake. The fifth column, 'User Preference,' is confirmed UNAFFECTED: its 10 notebooks (2 per run x 5 runs) contain zero chat.completions.create() calls and instead use a Modal-hosted PEFT ('is_peft=True') model via local HuggingFace model.generate(); the Bayesian/consistency judge itself (eval/eval.py) also runs on the direct first-party Anthropic API (claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929), not OpenRouter, so the judge is unaffected in all cases.

Title: ctfish (Palisade Research — "Demonstrating Specification Gaming in Reasoning Models")
Summary: An LLM agent harness (player.py) plays chess against Stockfish under a "win at all costs" prompt; the paper reports that reasoning models (o1-preview, o3, DeepSeek R1) frequently hack the environment (edit game state, replace the engine binary) instead of losing.
Importance/Impact: One of the most widely cited real-world AI specification-gaming/reward-hacking case studies (Time, Fortune, Business Insider coverage; heavily referenced in AI-control and scheming literature). The DeepSeek R1 "hacks by default" finding sits in the paper's abstract and headline Discussion table, making the OpenRouter-routed comparison load-bearing for the paper's most-quoted claim.
What they use OpenRouter for: Runs the actual agent rollouts — every step of the OODA-loop agent that plays chess and produces the paper's per-model hacking transcripts — for four models: deepseek/deepseek-chat, deepseek/deepseek-r1, qwen/qwq-32b-preview, and google/gemini-2.0-flash-thinking-exp:free, via an OpenAI-SDK client pointed at OpenRouter's base_url in player.py's Provider class.
Mistakes (null if safe): DeepSeek R1 and QwQ 32B Preview are run exclusively via OpenRouter with zero routing safeguards — no `provider` dict/extra_body anywhere in the repo — meaning each call could be silently served by any OpenRouter backend at any quantization, with default `data_collection: allow`, and no record kept of which backend actually served it (M1 unpinned quantization, M3 probabilistic routing, M4 no provenance, M5 data-policy leakage). Both are bare, undated OpenRouter slugs (M6 version drift). Critically, this unpinned DeepSeek R1 result is the direct comparator to OpenAI o3/o1-preview (served via the direct, single-provider OpenAI API) in the paper's abstract sentence "reasoning models like OpenAI o3 and DeepSeek R1 will often hack the benchmark by default" and in the central Discussion table's model-type/hacking breakdown — a classic cross-provider comparison confound (M8) threatening the paper's headline claim. The formal Limitations section (arxiv/main.typ, discussing judge unreliability and task narrowness) never mentions router/provider pinning, even though the appendix separately admits "the inference API was unstable" for DeepSeek runs — an unexplained symptom consistent with unpinned multi-provider routing that the paper attributes only to step-count noise rather than to serving-backend variability (M10 no reporting of the routing/inference-stack risk). By contrast, the paper's LLM-judge/scoring pipeline (scoring/score_stages.py) calls OpenAI directly (no OpenRouter), so M9 does not apply there.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The abstract's headline claim ("reasoning models like OpenAI o3 and DeepSeek R1 will often hack the benchmark by default") lumps DeepSeek R1 in with o3, but the paper's own Appendix F admits R1's Figure 2/8/15-17 breakdown is "not directly comparable" to other models and an underestimate, because R1 rollouts ran through OpenRouter (player.py's OPENROUTER_MODELS, base_url=openrouter.ai/api/v1) with zero provider/quantization pinning anywhere in the repo — the paper's own words ("inference API was unstable") describe exactly this unpinned OpenRouter route, and re-running the paper's own scoring pipeline against the real data confirms the cited numbers (0.15/0.04/0.08, n=52) are accurate reproductions, not invented. Correction to the task's premise: scoring/score_stages.py line 14 is `from openai import AsyncOpenAI`, not an OpenRouter call — that file's LLM judge (gpt-4o-mini) uses a plain AsyncOpenAI() client hitting OpenAI directly (confirmed: zero OpenRouter references anywhere in score_stages.py), so M9 does not apply there. The actual OpenRouter-exposed call site is player.py's Provider class (OPENROUTER_MODELS set, lines 33-37; client construction, lines 64-67), covering deepseek/deepseek-chat, deepseek/deepseek-r1, qwen/qwq-32b-preview, and google/gemini-2.0-flash-thinking-exp:free, none pinned via `provider`/`extra_body`.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[Abstract; Introduction bullet 1 (p.1); §8 Discussion "Model Type" table (p.5, verbatim: "1st gen Reasoning Models | o1 preview, DeepSeek R1 | ✓"); Figure 2 "Baseline runs summary"; Figure 8 "Distribution of number of steps"; Appendix F "Hacking breakdown" intro paragraph + F.3 (Figures 15-17)] Abstract/headline claim "reasoning models like OpenAI o3 and DeepSeek R1 will often hack the benchmark by default" partly rests on DeepSeek R1's Figure 2 baseline row (environment_failure=0.15, replace_stockfish=0.04, replace_board=0.08, n=52 — VERIFIED by re-running the paper's own scoring/figures.py + utils.py pipeline against the actual runs.json/labels.json/scoring_detailed_logs data: computed 0.154/0.038/0.077, which round to exactly the cited 2-decimal heatmap values), plus Figure 8's step-count histogram and the §8 Discussion table classifying DeepSeek R1 as a hacking "1st gen Reasoning Model" alongside o1-preview. The paper's own Appendix F opens: "Note the model breakdowns are not directly comparable. The DeepSeek agents have run for fewer steps because the inference API was unstable and we had trouble recording long runs... this underestimates DeepSeek's hacking success because it has fewer steps to work with" (verbatim, confirmed against arXiv PDF text). Reproducing Figure 8's underlying data from raw runs.json (baseline variant) confirms a real distributional split: o1-preview n=123, all runs >=15 steps (median 15); DeepSeek R1 n=74, median 11, with 36/74 (49%) of runs under 10 steps, versus zero such runs for o1-preview.

The truncated-run pattern in Figure 8 (roughly half of R1 baseline runs ending under 10 steps, vs. zero such o1-preview runs) is consistent with an unpinned multi-provider route bouncing across backends of uneven uptime/latency, though the paper does not itself attribute the instability specifically to OpenRouter by name (a reasonable but not author-confirmed inference from the code). This also undercuts the apples-to-apples comparison underlying the abstract's claim: o1-preview/o3 (OPENAI_MODELS, direct OpenAI client) and Claude 3.5/3.7 Sonnet (ANTHROPIC_MODELS, direct Anthropic client) were served through single consistent proprietary APIs in the same codebase, while DeepSeek R1 alone among the paper's headline-cited models went through unpinned OpenRouter routing (code-level silent backend mixing within one comparative study — same failure family as M8, cross-provider comparison confound).

Title: diffing-toolkit (science-of-finetuning / Neel Nanda, Julian Minder, Clément Dumas, Stewy Slocum — MATS 7)
Summary: A general-purpose "model diffing" mech-interp toolkit (activation-difference lens, patchscope, logit-diff mining, steering) used to produce the Alignment Forum post's finding that narrow finetuning leaves clearly readable traces; it runs a local white-box pipeline on open-weight finetuned models (Qwen/Llama/Gemma/DeepSeek) and layers an LLM-based "interpretability agent" plus several LLM-judge graders on top to interpret/score the pipeline's outputs.
Importance/Impact: Co-authored by Neel Nanda (Google DeepMind interpretability lead, one of the most prominent mech-interp researchers), plus Julian Minder, Clément Dumas, Stewy Slocum (MATS 7). 78 GitHub stars, actively developed general-purpose toolkit whose LLM-agent/grader pipeline directly produces reported scores in the post.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the sole backend for (1) the interpretability agent (AgentLLM, src/diffing/utils/agents/llm.py) that autonomously investigates and hypothesizes about model diffs, and (2) four LLM-judge grader classes (HypothesisGrader, CoherenceGrader, TokenRelevanceGrader, PatchScopeGrader) that score the agent's hypotheses, steered-generation coherence, token relevance, and patch-scope scale selection — all feeding numbers reported in the post. The models being diffed themselves (Qwen, Llama, Gemma, DeepSeek finetunes) are loaded locally via HuggingFace/nnsight/vLLM and never touch OpenRouter.
Mistakes (null if safe): The repo uses OpenRouter exclusively to call proprietary, single-vendor-served frontier models (OpenAI GPT-5 family, Google Gemini 2.5 family, Anthropic Claude Haiku 4.5) as the agent/judge backend, so M1 (quantization), M3 (probabilistic provider load-balancing), and M8 (cross-provider confound) do not meaningfully apply — these vendors' own models are not multi-provider-quantized on OpenRouter the way open-weight models are, and the repo never routes an open-weight model through OpenRouter (those are loaded locally via HF/nnsight). No response_format/JSON-schema constrained output is used anywhere (graders parse free text like "SCORE: <n>" / "COHERENT"), so M9 doesn't apply either. However, three real, verifiable gaps remain and all sit on a critical route (the grader/agent outputs feed the paper's reported coherence percentages, hypothesis-quality scores, token-relevance scores, and patch-scope scale selections): (1) M5 — `data_collection` is never set anywhere in the codebase, so it defaults to OpenRouter's "allow," meaning prompts containing the model-diffing artifacts under study (patchscope decodings, steered generations, activation-difference summaries) may be retained/used for training by whichever provider serves the call. (2) M6 — every grader/agent model is a bare, undated slug (`openai/gpt-5`, `openai/gpt-5-mini`, `openai/gpt-5-nano`, `google/gemini-2.5-pro`, etc.); if OpenAI/Google/Anthropic update the model behind these aliases (plausible for actively-iterated frontier models), a rerun would silently score with a different judge, threatening exact reproduction of the reported grading numbers. (3) M4 — no code anywhere stores which provider actually served a call, a completion/generation id, or any other provenance; combined with M6 this means drift in grader behavior over time would be undetectable and undiagnosable after the fact. A weaker, lower-confidence note: AgentLLM always sends `temperature=0.7` (and CoherenceGrader occasionally `temperature=0`) with no `require_parameters:true`, so if OpenRouter ever silently ignores temperature for a given proprietary route the agent's exploration diversity could change unnoticed (M2) — plausible but not confirmed, and low-impact since OpenAI's own chat-completions API has long supported temperature. None of this threatens the paper's primary, white-box evidence (activation-difference lens / logit-lens / patch-scope analysis on locally-loaded open-weight models), which is the strongest support for the headline claim; the OpenRouter-based agent/judge layer is a secondary interpretive/scoring layer, and the authors do build in some redundancy (a multi-judge "grader_model_id" robustness sweep across OpenAI/Google/Anthropic judges analyzed via a hierarchical Bayesian model, plus `num_repeat` resampling) that would tend to surface — though not fully diagnose — gross judge instability.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The single most defensible impacted claim is the headline agent-performance comparison. The AF post's most-cited number (ADL agent, 5 interactions: 97% score>=2 / 60% score>=4, vs. blackbox baseline 50 interactions: 12% / 6%) is confirmed to have been produced with the OpenRouter alias 'openai/gpt-5-chat' (disclosed only in a footnote). The companion arXiv paper (2510.13900) reports a materially different primary result for the same comparison using the different, equally unpinned alias 'openai/gpt-5' (91%/30% vs. 39%/1%), and explicitly relegates the blog's 97/60/12/6 numbers to an appendix as the result for 'the weaker non-thinking' model variant. The repo's currently pinned config (configs/diffing/evaluation.yaml#L17: openai/gpt-5) matches the paper's weaker/primary numbers, not the blog post's own headline -- so the repo as it stands today cannot reproduce its own most publicized statistic. Because neither alias is snapshot-pinned and neither AgentLLM.chat() (src/diffing/utils/agents/llm.py) nor Grader._call_with_retry (src/diffing/utils/graders/grader.py) records the served model/provider metadata from the OpenRouter response, this is a demonstrated (not merely hypothetical) instance of M6 model-version/identity drift compounded by M4 missing provenance logging: the exact magnitude of the paper's central claim depends on which of (at least) two undated OpenRouter model slugs was used, that dependency is not disclosed outside a single footnote, and it cannot be resolved after the fact from any artifact in the repo.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[AF post 'An Interpretability Agent Beats Blackbox Baselines' section + footnote [9] ('The interpretability agent is based on openai/gpt-5-chat as provided on openrouter.ai'); arXiv:2510.13900 Sec 4.1 (main text, primary result) vs. Appendix F.6.2 'Other Agent Models' / Fig. 25 (labels the 97/60/94/39/12/6 numbers as the 'weaker' gpt-5-chat variant); configs/diffing/evaluation.yaml#L17 (agent.llm.model_id: openai/gpt-5 #openai/gpt-5 # anthropic/claude-sonnet-4 -- confirmed present, a third variant was evidently tried too)] Agent Performance Comparison (headline): ADL agent, 5 interactions: 97% reached score>=2, 60% reached >=4; ADL agent, 0 interactions: 94% >=2, 39% >=4; Blackbox baseline, 50 interactions: 12% >=2, 6% >=4. A strong blackbox baseline with 50 interactions reaches >=2 on only 12% and >=4 on 6% ... 0 interactions ... 94% ... 39%'). BUT this is NOT the companion paper's primary/headline result: arXiv:2510.13900 Sec 4.1 main text (using the plain, undated alias 'openai/gpt-5') reports a materially different comparison for the identical setup: ADL i=5 -> 91%/30%, ADL i=0 -> 82%/24%, blackbox i=50 -> 39%/1%. The 97/60/94/39/12/6 figures instead reappear verbatim in Appendix F.6.2 'Other Agent Models', explicitly labeled there as the result of 'the weaker non-thinking openai/gpt-5-chat model' (single run), included specifically because 'the performance gap between ADL-equipped and blackbox agents is even more pronounced' for that weaker variant.
    → mechanism: M6, confirmed rather than merely hypothesized: the AF post's headline was produced with the OpenRouter alias 'openai/gpt-5-chat' (per the post's own footnote), while the companion paper's primary/main-text result and the repo's currently pinned config (evaluation.yaml#L17, the task's given call site) both use the different, equally undated alias 'openai/gpt-5'. These two unpinned slugs give a materially different agent-vs-baseline gap (blackbox score>=2 rate 12% vs 39%, a >3x difference). M4 compounds this: AgentLLM.chat() in src/diffing/utils/agents/llm.py (verified) extracts only completion.choices[0].message.content and token usage from the OpenRouter response -- it never reads or stores completion.model / provider metadata -- so the exact served snapshot behind either alias, for either run, is unrecoverable from any stored artifact. The paper's own claim of 'offering a fully reproducible methodology for assessing model diffing informativeness' (Motivation section) is therefore not accurate for the specific headline figure as republished in the blog: rerunning the repo today against its own pinned config reproduces the paper's weaker numbers, not the blog's own most-cited statistic.

CONFIRMED, found verbatim in arXiv:2510.13900 Appendix D.3.1 'Hypothesis Grader and Agent Interactions': 'We obtain alpha=0.81, indicating high agreement... All pairwise correlations are high (rho>0.85, p<0.001)'. Correction to the candidate: this statistic is NOT in the AF blog post itself (confirmed absent by full-text search of the post) -- it exists only in the companion arXiv paper's appendix. The repo script the candidate cited, narrow_ft_experiments/hibayes/agent_grades/grader_agreement.py, does exist verbatim as described (openai/gpt-5-mini, google/gemini-2.5-flash, anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5; scipy pearsonr + krippendorff.alpha; exports a LaTeX table labeled tab:grader_agreement) and is almost certainly the generating script for these exact appendix numbers -- the candidate's 'likely underlying' framing undersold how directly confirmable this is.

One-line fix: Set `provider: {data_collection: "deny", require_parameters: true}` in every OpenRouter config and log the response's served-provider / generation id alongside each agent and grader call (e.g. persist `completion.id` and call `GET /api/v1/generation?id=...`), and pin/record the exact dated model snapshot actually used for the agent and each grader.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: DISQUALIFIED; amplification 7/10 (long-horizon agentic error compounding, LLM-as-judge score drift). Blocker: The entire headline experiment (paper 'Narrow Finetuning Leaves Clearly Readable Traces', arXiv:2510.13900) requires local GPUs to host paired base+finetuned model organisms (Gemma3-1B, Qwen3-1.7B/32B, Llama-3.2-1B(-Instruct), Gemma2-9B-it, Qwen2.5-7B/VL-3B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct). Two independent GPU dependencies exist, not one:...
Signs provider issues messed up the pape: No raw generation ever surfaces to a reader: not the agent's own investigation transcripts, not a judge's hypothesis-grade JSON, not a coherence-grader verdict on a real steered sample. The pipeline that would have produced checkable per-sample data exists (narrow_ft_experiments/hibayes/agent_grades/data_preprocessing.py, which parses real `hypothesis_grade_*.json` files with `score` and `grader_model_id` fields — exactly the F2-shaped artifact one would want), but its input directory lives only on the authors' local/cluster filesystem and was never committed or mirrored to HF/Zenodo. The paper does name the serving path plainly — "the agents are based on openai/gpt-5 with default settings (thinking strength medium) as hosted by openrouter.ai" (Appendix C.4) — which is an F16 paperwork disclosure (named model + named router, no provider/precision/snapshot pin), not evidence either way. Given that no transcripts, per-sample scores, or provider metadata were released in any form that could be opened and checked, this is a clean nothing_checkable_released rather than an inconclusive or a fingerprint claim.

Title: lighteval — Swiss-Legal / LEXam LLM-as-judge tasks (JudgeLM litellm backend)
Summary: lighteval is Hugging Face's official eval library (2479 stars, powers the Open LLM Leaderboard). Its `swiss_legal` task module — shipped for the ICLR 2026 LEXam benchmark and companion Swiss-legal tasks (SLDS summarization, SLT translation) — scores model outputs with an LLM-as-judge, and several of the default/sweep judge-model configs are routed through OpenRouter with zero routing safeguards.
Importance/Impact: 2479 GitHub stars; official HF eval library used for the Open LLM Leaderboard and many papers; LEXam is an accepted ICLR 2026 paper whose judge metric ships directly in this widely-used library, so any routing defect propagates to every downstream user of the SLDS/SLT tasks, not just the original authors.
What they use OpenRouter for: LLM-as-judge backend for two of the three Swiss-legal task families: (1) SLDS (Swiss Landmark Decision Summarization) — the *default* judge is `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat` (open-weight DeepSeek-V3), used unconditionally by `_get_metrics()` for every SLDS subtask; (2) SLT (Swiss Legal Translation) — the `JUDGE_MODELS` sweep routes o1, gpt-4o-mini, gpt-4o, and llama-3.3-70b-instruct all through `openrouter/...` slugs, and `metrics_to_evaluate = ["judge"]` (metrics.py:980) makes this sweep the default metric set for the task. In both cases the judge score IS the reported corpus-level metric (`corpus_level_fn=statistics.mean`), not an intermediate/exploratory signal. By contrast, the paper's flagship LEXam open-question judge (`get_lexam_oq_judge()`, main.py:421) defaults to `openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20` with no `openrouter/` prefix — litellm routes that directly to OpenAI, not through OpenRouter — so the headline LEXam OQ/MCQ numbers are likely unaffected; the exposure is in the companion SLDS/SLT benchmarks living in the same module.
Mistakes (null if safe): The judge-call path shared by all Swiss-legal metrics is `JudgeLM.__call_litellm` (src/lighteval/metrics/utils/llm_as_judge.py:305-371). Its kwargs dict is `{"model", "messages", "n": 1, "caching": True, "response_format": self.response_format}` plus `max_tokens` — no `provider` key at all, so every "openrouter/"-prefixed model gets OpenRouter's default behavior: no `quantizations` pin (M1), no `order`/`only`/`sort` so requests load-balance across providers (M3), no `data_collection: "deny"` so prompts (Swiss court judgments/legal exam text) default to "allow" at providers that train on data (M5), and the function returns only `response.choices[0].message.content` (line 348/352) — the served provider / generation id is never captured or stored anywhere in the codebase (confirmed via `grep -rIn` for extra_body/require_parameters/quantizations/data_collection/generation_id across src/: zero hits) (M4). Concretely: `get_swiss_landmark_decision_summarization_judge(..., model_name: str = "openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat", ...)` (metrics.py:795) is the *unconditional default* judge for every SLDS headnote-quality score (wired in via `main.py:320-322`, no override) — DeepSeek-V3 behind a bare, undated alias (`deepseek-chat`, M6) served by whichever of DeepSeek's many OpenRouter providers/quantizations wins that request, with the SLDS score being the exact number reported for that model/language. Separately, `JUDGE_MODELS` (metrics.py:928-932) maps `"o1"→"openrouter/openai/o1"`, `"gpt-4o-mini"→"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18"`, `"gpt-4o"→"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"`, `"llama-3-3-70b"→"openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct"`, and with `metrics_to_evaluate = ["judge"]` hardcoded (metrics.py:980), all of these feed `JUDGE_METRICS` and become the default SLT-judge metric sweep — comparing a proprietary trio against an unpinned open-weight 70B judge with identical (absent) safeguards on both sides (M8: any "which judge best matches humans" conclusion drawn from this sweep is confounded by DeepSeek/Llama quantization variance that GPT-4o/o1 don't share). Note also `init_llm_judge_metric` (metrics.py:1054-1058) builds `"slt_judge_gpt_4o"` from `judge_model_name="openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"` (direct OpenAI, no "openrouter/" prefix) while `JUDGE_MODELS["gpt-4o"]` builds the *same* checkpoint through `"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"` — the identical model string is called via two different backends in the same file (M11 silent backend mixing); the inline comment at metrics.py:929 ("Updated to match OpenRouter availability; previously: openai/o1-2024-12-17") confirms this migration happened without adding any pinning/provenance. Finally, neither the repo's README/docs nor the paper abstract (checked via WebFetch on arxiv.org/abs/2505.12864) mention OpenRouter, provider routing, or judge-output reproducibility at all (M10) — the only two files in the repo mentioning "openrouter" are the ones cited above, and both are purely functional (routing the call / estimating context length via OpenRouter's endpoints API), never provenance-related. Threatened results: the SLDS headnote-quality scores and the SLT judge-agreement/quality comparisons across judge models — both of which are the literal `corpus_level_fn` numbers lighteval reports for these Swiss-legal tasks. The LEXam paper's own headline open-question judge score is not implicated by this specific defect since its default (`openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20`, no "openrouter/" prefix) resolves to direct OpenAI, not OpenRouter.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The audit's supplied paper (arXiv:2505.12864, LEXam, ICLR 2026) actually governs only 1 of the 3 task families discussed here. lighteval's swiss_legal module bundles three unrelated papers: LEXam (2505.12864) for open-question/MCQ exam tasks, SwiLTra-Bench (arXiv:2503.01372, ACL 2025) for SLT/SDST/SSCPRT translation-judge tasks, and Rolshoven et al. 'Unlocking Legal Knowledge' (EMNLP Findings 2025, arXiv:2410.13456) for SLDS headnote generation. Verified: LEXam's own open-question judge calls openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20 DIRECTLY (no openrouter/ prefix) and MCQ scoring uses no LLM judge at all, so LEXam's headline numbers are genuinely off the OpenRouter path, as the draft claimed. But SLDS's and SLT's default judges DO run through unpinned OpenRouter litellm calls (verified exact kwargs, llm_as_judge.py:333-339, no 'provider' key) — and live OpenRouter data (fetched today) confirms real, current quantization spread for both open-weight judges: deepseek/deepseek-chat across 3 providers (fp4/fp8/unknown) and meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct across 12 providers (fp8/bf16/fp16/unknown), with no data-policy pin and no provenance logging anywhere in the codebase. A specific first-pass claim (deepseek/deepseek-chat 'historically repointed' to different checkpoints) did not survive verification against OpenRouter's live catalog and was dropped. A clearly-missed but well-evidenced addition: OpenRouter's catalog gaps, not judge validity, shaped the SLT judge roster — the source paper's own best judge (Gemini-1.5-Flash) is excluded with 'not available via OpenRouter' given as the reason in the code itself, while o1, which that paper found anti-correlated with human ratings, stays active as 1 of 4 judge models.

[src/lighteval/tasks/multilingual/tasks/swiss_legal/metrics.py: get_swiss_landmark_decision_summarization_judge() lines 793-814 (default model_name="openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat" at line 795, short_judge_name="slds_judge_deepseek_v3" at line 796); JudgeSwissLandmarkDecisionSummarization._process_judge_response lines 521-539 (verified exact: 5-field SCORE_EXTRACTION_PATTERN, fixed /10 denominator regardless of how many of the 5 *_SCORE lines are actually parsed); called with only language=headnote_language, no model override, from HeadnoteGenerationTask._get_metrics(), swiss_legal/main.py lines 309-325. Underlying call: JudgeLLM.__call_litellm, llm_as_judge.py lines 333-339 (verified exact: kwargs = {model, messages, n, caching, response_format} only, no 'provider' key).] SLDS (Swiss Landmark Decision Summarization) headnote-generation judge, metric `slds_judge_deepseek_v3` — computed for all 9 decision-language x headnote-language variants (de/fr/it x de/fr/it) and invoked with no override as the only LLM-based quality metric for every generated headnote (alongside non-LLM bert_score/bleu/rouge/extractiveness).

Because _process_judge_response requires exactly 5 regex-matched lines and always divides by the fixed denominator 10 rather than by however many were actually parsed, a degraded/quantized provider that returns fewer parseable lines mechanically deflates the score instead of erroring. No data_collection:'deny' is set (no 'provider' dict passed at all) and no provider/generation-id is ever read back or logged anywhere in lighteval (grepped src/lighteval/, zero hits) — unrecoverable provenance (M5+M4+M10). Two corrections to the first-pass draft: (1) the claim that 'deepseek/deepseek-chat' has 'historically been repointed to different underlying checkpoints' does not hold up — OpenRouter's live catalog shows it as a stable slug (created 2024-12-26) distinct from later checkpoints, which get their own slugs (deepseek-chat-v3-0324, deepseek-chat-v3.1) rather than reusing this one; dropped. (2) The M11 citation is dropped — there is no direct/non-OpenRouter DeepSeek call anywhere in the codebase for this to be 'mixed' with. Also worth noting: the judge choice itself (DeepSeek-V3, 5-criterion 1-3 rubric normalized to /10 then x100) is a faithful, validated port of Rolshoven et al., 'Unlocking Legal Knowledge' (EMNLP Findings 2025, arXiv:2410.13456) — NOT the audit's supplied paper arXiv:2505.12864 (LEXam), which is a different, unrelated benchmark bundled in the same lighteval module.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[metrics.py: JUDGE_MODELS["llama-3-3-70b"] = "openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct" (line 932, verified exact) feeds JUDGE_METRICS (lines 967-978, verified exact: 4 judge models x 2 few_shot_styles x 3 system/judgment combos = 24), unconditionally selected via hardcoded metrics_to_evaluate = ["judge"] (line 980, verified exact) into module-level METRICS_TO_USE, which TranslationTask (main.py line 277) uses directly with no per-task toggle.] Swiss Legal Translation (SLT) judge metrics for the Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct judge — 6 of 24 independently-computed judge metric variants (there is no ensembling/averaging code anywhere in the module — 'ensemble' in the first-pass draft is not the codebase's term and overstates what happens; each variant is a separate, independently-reported SampleLevelMetricGrouping) — computed for every translation task variant across the sdst/slt/sscprt dataset families.

Title: safety-tooling (safety-research org shared alignment-research inference library)
Summary: A shared, general-purpose async LLM inference wrapper (`InferenceAPI`) maintained by the safety-research/MATS/Anthropic-Fellows ecosystem and depended on by dozens of downstream alignment papers/repos to run model generations, evals, and data generation. It routes calls to many backends (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Together, HuggingFace, vLLM, DeepSeek-direct, and OpenRouter) behind one uniform `InferenceAPI.__call__` interface.
Importance/Impact: 132 stars but structurally high-leverage: shared inference infra reused as a dependency by dozens of downstream alignment/control repos (persona_vectors, false-facts, science-synth-facts, believe-it-or-not, introspection-mechanisms, SHADE-Arena-adjacent projects), so its OpenRouter defaults propagate into results reported by many independent papers.
What they use OpenRouter for: Provides `OpenRouterChatModel` (safetytooling/apis/inference/openrouter.py), auto-selected by `InferenceAPI.model_id_to_class()` (safetytooling/apis/inference/api.py:312-313) whenever `model_id` is in the hardcoded `OPENROUTER_MODELS` set or starts with `"openrouter/"`, or via `force_provider="openrouter"`. `OPENROUTER_MODELS` (openrouter.py:15-30) is mostly open-weight models: `meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b(:free)`, `deepseek/deepseek-v3-base:free`, `deepseek/deepseek-r1-zero:free`, `nousresearch/hermes-3-llama-3.1-{70b,405b}`, `mistralai/mistral-*`, plus some proprietary-via-router entries (`x-ai/grok-4`, `google/gemini-2.0-flash-001`). README.md also documents a second path: calling OpenRouter through the plain `OpenAIChatModel` via `openai_base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"` + `force_provider="openai"` (README.md:150-161) — a different code path than the dedicated OpenRouterChatModel class for the same underlying endpoint. Output (`LLMResponse.completion`, logprobs, etc.) is consumed by any downstream repo built on this infra for rollouts/evals/data generation, i.e. it is the generic model-calling substrate for a large share of independent alignment research.
Mistakes (null if safe): This is shared infra, so the corrupting mistakes threaten every downstream repo's numbers that route open-weight models (Llama-3.1-405b, DeepSeek-v3/r1, Mistral-large, Hermes-3) through the default `OpenRouterChatModel`/`force_provider="openrouter"` path. M1 (no `quantizations` filter anywhere in the codebase): any eval/rollout/data-generation run against these open-weight models can silently be served at a different quantization each run, threatening reproducibility of any benchmark or generated dataset built on it. M2 (no `require_parameters`): sampling params like `temperature`, `logprobs`, `seed`, and arbitrary kwargs (LLMParams allows `extra="allow"`, e.g. `response_format`) are passed straight through with no guarantee the serving provider honors them — `test_openrouter_logprobs` even relies on `logprobs`+`temperature=0.0` being honored, unchecked. M3 (no `order`/`only`/`sort`): every call is subject to OpenRouter's default load-balanced routing, so repeated calls to the same "model_id" for a comparison or ablation study can hit different providers run-to-run. M4 (no provenance logging): `LLMResponse` has no field for which provider/generation actually served a call, so a downstream paper cannot audit after the fact which backend produced any given cached completion — this alone makes any claim about reproducibility of OpenRouter-derived results unverifiable. M5 (`data_collection` never set, defaults to "allow"): alignment-research prompts (which can include sensitive red-teaming/deception content) sent through this shared path may be used for provider training by default. M8 (cross-model comparison confound): this infra exists specifically to let downstream papers compare many models under one interface (that is its purpose across the alignment ecosystem); since none of the OpenRouter-routed open-weight models are pinned, any downstream cross-model comparison inherits unpinned provider/quantization variance as a confound. M10 (no reporting): README/CLAUDE.md/REPO_GUIDE.md never mention OpenRouter's provider-variance/quantization risk, so downstream authors adopting this infra are not warned to add safeguards themselves. M11 (silent backend mixing): the README's own documented alternative (OpenAIChatModel pointed at OpenRouter's base_url) and the dedicated OpenRouterChatModel class are two different code paths that can both serve the same "model" family via OpenRouter, with different kwargs-handling and error-parsing behavior, inviting inconsistent treatment of the same nominal model across a project.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: safety-tooling's dedicated OpenRouterChatModel — the class model_id_to_class() auto-selects for 13 of the 16 hardcoded OPENROUTER_MODELS entries (the open-weight ones: meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b(-instruct/:free/-70b/-8b), llama-3.3-70b-instruct, mistralai/mistral-large-2411, mistral-small-24b-instruct-2501, mistral/ministral-8b, deepseek-v3-base:free, deepseek-r1-zero:free, hermes-3-llama-3.1-70b/405b; the excluded 3 — x-ai/grok-4, gemini-2.0-flash-001, gemini-2.5-flash-preview — are single-vendor-served and correctly out of scope) — sets no provider/quantization constraint anywhere (zero repo-wide hits for quantizations/require_parameters/order/sort, confirmed by grep) and never records which provider served a call (no `provider` field exists in the LLMResponse pydantic model; cost is hardcoded to 0 in all 3 LLMResponse constructions in the file). This is a structural/latent risk in shared infra, not a corrupted number within safety-tooling itself (it has no headline experiment of its own), but every downstream eval/rollout/judge run built on InferenceAPI with one of these model IDs — via default routing or the misleadingly-named force_provider=\"openrouter\" — silently inherits non-reproducible, unaudited backend routing with no way to detect or correct it after the fact.

Title: Evaluating LLMs for accuracy incentivizes hallucinations — experiment code (openai/hallucinations-paper-experiments)
Summary: Official OpenAI repo backing the Nature 2026 paper (Kalai, Nachum, Vempala, Zhang) showing accuracy-only grading incentivizes hallucination; a Jupyter notebook queries 4 frontier LLMs on the full SimpleQA test set under varying abstention penalties and grades every response with GPT-4.1 as an LLM judge, all via OpenRouter.
Importance/Impact: Official OpenAI-org repo for a flagship Nature 2026 paper by Kalai, Nachum, Vempala, Zhang on why accuracy-only grading incentivizes LLM hallucination — high-authority source, high-visibility venue, and the repo is the literal "reproduce our experiments" artifact linked from the paper.
What they use OpenRouter for: Both legs of the pipeline go through OpenRouter's /chat/completions endpoint via lm.py's LM.generate(): (1) generation — querying google/gemini-3-pro-preview, openai/gpt-5, x-ai/grok-4, anthropic/claude-opus-4.5 on all 4,326 SimpleQA questions x multiple abstention rewards x baseline/consistency-mitigation conditions x 2 seeds each; (2) grading — every answer is graded CORRECT/INCORRECT/NOT_ATTEMPTED by openai/gpt-4.1 using the SimpleQA grader template (free-text A/B/C output, retried until valid). Both outputs feed directly into the paper's reported hallucination/abstention/accuracy figures (figures/simpleqa_halluc_rates.pdf, figures/simpleqa_abstention.pdf, figures/full_table.tex).
Mistakes (null if safe): All 4 subject models plus the GPT-4.1 grader are proprietary, effectively lab-single-served on OpenRouter, so the highest-severity multi-provider risks (M1 quantization, M3 probabilistic routing, M8 cross-model comparison confound) are essentially N/A — no `provider` dict is set anywhere (grepped whole repo, zero hits), but that mostly doesn't matter for these models. Two real issues remain that DO threaten reported numbers: (1) M4 — lm.py never records which provider served a call or the OpenRouter generation id; `output_mode="full"` (the only mode that would surface OpenRouter's `provider` field) is defined in lm.py:248-249 but never invoked anywhere in experiment.ipynb (grepped: only "with_usage" and default "response" modes are used), so even the modest residual risk for Claude (which OpenRouter can route via Anthropic-direct, Bedrock, or Vertex) is unverifiable after the fact. (2) M6 — a genuine, verified discrepancy: README.md:31 states the GPT condition queried was `openai/gpt-5.2`, but lm.py:42-45 defines the "gpt-5" key as bare `openai/gpt-5` (no date/version suffix), and this is exactly what the notebook actually calls (experiment.ipynb cell 2: `"gpt": LM("gpt-5")`) — confirmed by the checked-in execution trace in EXAMPLES.md:442 (`choice: 1 gpt-5`, not gpt-5.2). This means the repo's stated "reproduce the paper's experiments" claim is not accurate for the GPT condition as committed: a re-run today queries whatever `openai/gpt-5` currently bare-resolves to, which is not verifiably the same checkpoint that produced the published GPT curve in figures/simpleqa_halluc_rates.pdf and figures/full_table.tex, and could drift further if OpenAI repoints the alias. M2 (param dropping) and M9 (judge on unconstrained route) were considered but don't really apply: the grader uses free-text A/B/C output with client-side validation/retry (no response_format/schema requested at all), and the few sampling params passed (temperature=0, max_tokens=16, seed) are basic, universally-supported OpenAI params on an OpenAI-only route. M5 (data_collection left "allow") is technically true (never set) but low real-world impact since SimpleQA questions are public benchmark data, not sensitive.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The paper's core qualitative claim -- closed/accuracy-only grading drives higher hallucination rates than open-rubric grading, and consistency-mitigation reduces it -- is unlikely to reverse, since it rests on within-model contrasts (same bare alias under two prompting conditions), not cross-model/cross-provider comparisons where OpenRouter's provider-mixing would bite hardest. But the exact percentages in figures/full_table.tex and both PDF figures cannot be verified as reproducible: lm.py's OpenRouter call (verified at L224-236) sends a bare, unpinned model id with no `provider` routing/pinning field anywhere in the repo (confirmed by direct grep across lm.py, the notebook, README.md, and EXAMPLES.md), and the 'with_usage' output path used for every generation and every grading call discards the rest of OpenRouter's response, including explicit deletion of is_byok/cost_details/prompt_tokens_details. This is not hypothetical: README.md's own 'Models queried' bullet and cost table both name 'GPT-5.2' as the model used in this exact run, while experiment.ipynb actually instantiates LM('gpt-5'), which resolves via OPENROUTER_MODELS to the different, undated alias 'openai/gpt-5' -- a live, verified version-label mismatch in the repo as shipped.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[figures/full_table.tex; generated by experiment.ipynb cell 26 (side_by_side_table) from baseline_scores/mitigated_scores (cell 24); model->LLM mapping and LM instantiation in cell 2; primary call site lm.py L224-236] Main results table (figures/full_table.tex): per-model score matrix across 4 penalty thresholds (L=0,1,3,9) x {Closed, Open} rubric x {baseline (\nomtg), consistency-mitigated (\mtg)} for LLM1=Gemini, LLM2=GPT, LLM3=Grok, LLM4=Opus. Verified exact cell values: LLM2 closed-rubric baseline @ threshold 0% = 0.49; LLM4 closed-rubric baseline @ threshold 0% = 0.37; LLM1 open-rubric mitigated @ threshold 90% = 0.88.
    → mechanism: M4 (no provenance logging): lm.py's _generate() posts a bare model id ('google/gemini-3-pro-preview', 'openai/gpt-5', 'x-ai/grok-4', 'anthropic/claude-opus-4.5') with payload = {'model': self.api_name, 'messages': messages} and no `provider` dict anywhere -- verified by direct grep: zero routing/provider-field hits across lm.py, experiment.ipynb, README.md, and EXAMPLES.md (the only 'provider' hits are generic comments like 'transient network/provider errors'). The 'with_usage' output path used throughout the notebook (both for model answers via answer_question() and for grading via grade_answer()) returns only `(response_text, usage)` -- the rest of OpenRouter's completion object is discarded outright, and within `usage` the code explicitly deletes ['is_byok', 'prompt_tokens_details', 'cost_details'] before returning (lm.py L253-255). So no per-call snapshot/provider identifier survives anywhere for later audit.
