(Note: an earlier pass of this finding cited '~138k+ calls' behind this table; that figure does not appear anywhere in the repo and has been dropped as unverifiable/likely fabricated.)

    → severity: low | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium
One-line fix: Capture OpenRouter's returned `provider`/`id` fields for every call (use output_mode="full" instead of discarding them) and pin exact, dated model slugs — the README already drifted from the code (claims openai/gpt-5.2, code+execution trace show bare openai/gpt-5), so provenance would have caught this before publication.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: DISQUALIFIED; amplification 4/10 (LLM-as-judge score drift, pass@k variance). Blocker: FATAL: all 5 OpenRouter model IDs this repo calls (google/gemini-3-pro-preview, openai/gpt-5, x-ai/grok-4, anthropic/claude-opus-4.5, openai/gpt-4.1) are single-provider first-party-only listings on OpenRouter (verified live via openrouter.ai/<model>/providers on 2026-07-18) -- there is no alternate/weak/quantized third-party host to pin ...
Signs provider issues messed up the pape: EXAMPLES.md was read in full (all 570 lines) and grepped for provider/generation-id keys, special tokens, and finish_reason/truncation markers; none appear. Every completion present — all from google/gemini-3-pro-preview or openai/gpt-5, e.g. the Frank Munn, John Lewis, KLM/Pan Am, Round Table Pizza, and D&D-lore examples — is coherent, on-topic, natural prose with no token soup (F1), no special-token litter or U+FFFD (F4), no empty-answer/length-truncation pattern (F3), and no reasoning field at all to check for shape drift (F5), consistent with short direct-answer SimpleQA completions rather than CoT. This is a genuinely clean read of what's there, but it does not settle the serving-integrity question: the file is a ~60-completion illustrative slice of the 17,304 total, covering only 2 of the 4 models, with no claude-opus-4.5 or grok-4 examples appearing anywhere in it. lm.py's full source was read directly, not inferred from a listing, and on no code path does it request or store a provider/generation-id field, making F2 — the one fingerprint that could actually prove or disprove mixed backends — structurally unrecoverable from anything this project could ever publish, at any sample size. The clean read is real but thin and partial; the decisive check is permanently unavailable.

Title: Inspect AI (UK AI Safety Institute)
Summary: Inspect AI is UKAISI's open-source LLM evaluation harness (the substrate METR, Apollo, inspect_evals, and many published scheming/sandbagging/control papers run rollouts and scoring through). It ships a first-class `OpenRouterAPI(OpenAICompatibleAPI)` provider alongside ~15 direct providers.
Importance/Impact: The standard eval harness for the AI safety/control field: 2,369 GitHub stars, 609 forks, built and maintained by UK AISI. It underlies inspect_evals and is used directly or via wrappers by METR (hawk, inspect-agents, inspect-action), Apollo Research, and many published scheming/sandbagging/control papers as the execution substrate for model rollouts and scoring.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is one of many pluggable model backends: `inspect eval task.py --model openrouter/org/model` routes chat-completion calls for eval/agent rollouts and (via user-configured judge models) scoring through OpenRouter's OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
Mistakes (null if safe): As shipped, the documented/idiomatic OpenRouter usage path has no default safeguards: (1) M1 no quantizations pin — open-weight model evals (a large fraction of what OpenRouter routes, e.g. Llama/DeepSeek/Mixtral variants used in capability/safety evals) can silently run at int4/int8 on one backend vs bf16 on another, confounding any capability or control-evaluation score computed via Inspect+OpenRouter run-to-run or across papers. (2) M2/M7 no require_parameters — sampling params (temperature, seed, etc.) can be silently dropped by whichever provider OpenRouter load-balances to; the library's own `seed` docstring confirms OpenRouter isn't in the list of providers where seed is honored. (3) M3 no order/only/sort — provider selection is probabilistic by default, so repeated eval runs (or the same eval across two papers using the same model string) may hit materially different backends/quantizations, threatening reproducibility of any capability/safety score. (4) M5 data_collection is never set/documented, so safety-sensitive eval transcripts (e.g. red-team prompts, scheming/sandbagging rollouts) default to whatever OpenRouter account-level data policy is in force, unflagged to the researcher. Because Inspect is described as the execution substrate for rollouts/scoring across a wide swath of the safety-eval ecosystem, any downstream paper that follows the basic README example without additionally learning and setting OpenRouter's `provider={...}` object inherits all four gaps. The one significant mitigant is that Inspect logs the full raw response (including OpenRouter's actual-provider and generation-id fields) into every `.eval` transcript by default, so drift is at least auditable after the fact even though it isn't prevented.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: In the UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_evals PersistBench reproduction (run through Inspect AI's default, unpinned OpenRouterAPI path, model_args={} and no -M provider=... on any run), the measured cross-domain-leakage failure_rate for openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct is 25.0, which falls outside the PersistBench paper's own reported 95% bootstrap CI of [12.0, 23.0] (arXiv:2602.01146, independently confirmed against the paper's Table 2) -- consistent with, though not proof of, silent quantization/provider variance on the unpinned route, since subject-model temperature/seed are also left unpinned as an independent confound.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[inspect_evals repo (main): src/inspect_evals/persistbench/README.md 'Evaluation Report' table row 1. Paper numbers independently cross-checked against arXiv:2602.01146v2 Table 2 ('PersistBench: When Should Long-Term Memories Be Forgotten by LLMs?') — paper text gives Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct cross-domain leakage as 17.5% [12.0, 23.0], an exact match to the README's 'Paper Result' column.] PersistBench `persistbench_cross_domain` failure_rate for `openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct` = 25.0, outside the paper's own reported 95% CI [12.0, 23.0] (point estimate 17.5)
    → mechanism: M1 (no quantization pin) + M3 (probabilistic provider routing), with one confound noted below. Verified in the archived .eval log for the sibling persistbench_beneficial_memory run in the same eval-set (results/persistbench_beneficial_memory/2026-05-22_openrouter_meta-llama_llama-3.3-70b-instruct.json): eval.model_args={}, eval.model_generate_config={}, plan.config={}. No archived .eval log exists in the repo for the cross_domain task itself, so these empty configs are inferred (not directly observed) for the row-1 run -- a reasonable inference since README's 'Commands used' shows all three tasks launched from a single `inspect eval-set ... --model openrouter/...` invocation with no -M flags. Traces to inspect_ai/model/_providers/openrouter.py: `self.provider = collect_model_arg('provider')` (L108-112) defaults to None, only injected into extra_body['provider'] inside completion_params() (L372-383) when -M provider={...} is passed, which never happens here. CAVEAT: model_generate_config={} also means subject-model temperature/seed were unpinned, so ordinary decoding stochasticity is a real confound alongside quantization/provider variance -- the CI breach is consistent with, not proof of, an OpenRouter-routing-specific cause.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[inspect_evals repo: src/inspect_evals/persistbench/README.md 'Evaluation Report' table rows 2-6. All six paper-side [95% CI] values cross-checked and confirmed exact against arXiv:2602.01146v2 Table 2.] Remaining 5 persistbench failure_rate figures for the same two open-weight subject models across the three tasks (sycophancy 85.0/99.0, beneficial_memory 54.0/22.0, cross_domain 53.0 for gpt-oss-120b) -- confirmed to match both the README table and the paper's own published values, and all fall inside the paper's CI on this run
    → mechanism: M1 + M3, same unpinned-default path as row 1 (no -M provider=... on any of these runs). None is currently out-of-CI, so this is a latent-risk finding, not a demonstrated distortion: run-to-run reproducibility is not guaranteed because neither provider/quantization (subject-model side) nor decoding temperature/seed (also unset per model_generate_config={} in the archived sibling log) is pinned or logged. Two values (53.0 and 99.0) sit exactly on a CI boundary, suggestive that the measurement is not far from the edge of the paper's own resampling variance.
    → severity: low | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium

[inspect_evals repo: src/inspect_evals/persistbench/scorers.py (DEFAULT_JUDGE_MODEL, DEFAULT_JUDGE_PROVIDER, _get_judge()) and README.md 'Scoring' section -- quote confirmed verbatim.] Default PersistBench judge openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2-thinking, routed via DEFAULT_JUDGE_PROVIDER = {'order': ['google-vertex'], 'allow_fallbacks': False}, described in the README as chosen because 'the paper's judge calibration was performed against Vertex AI-hosted Kimi K2 Thinking, ensuring score consistency with published results'
    → mechanism: Partial mitigation, residual M2 risk. Provider routing is genuinely pinned in code (order: ['google-vertex'], allow_fallbacks: False), verified directly against scorers.py, which avoids M3/M8 cross-backend drift for the judge itself. But require_parameters is never set, so GenerateConfig(temperature=0, reasoning_effort='high') + reasoning_enabled=True sent to the judge (forwarded as OpenRouterAPI model_args per _providers/openrouter.py L120-123 and L339-370) could be silently dropped/altered rather than erroring if unsupported on that route. ADDITIONAL CAVEAT: fetching arXiv:2602.01146v2 directly, the paper's own text does NOT mention Vertex AI, google-vertex, or any specific inference host for its judge calibration -- only that judges were run 'with temperature set to zero.' The README's claim that calibration specifically used 'Vertex AI-hosted Kimi K2 Thinking' is unverifiable against the cited paper and may be an unsupported assertion by the eval's contributor, undercutting the stated rationale for this pin (though the pin itself is real and still a legitimate M3/M8 mitigation regardless of the stated reason).
    → severity: low | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium

[Inspect AI docs: docs/providers.qmd, 'OpenRouter' section (~lines 1516-1543 on main), quickstart command and the -M args table row `provider | -M "provider={ 'quantizations': ['int8'] }"`. Code: src/inspect_ai/model/_providers/openrouter.py L95-135 (provider/models/transforms/reasoning_enabled model-args all default to None via collect_model_arg) and L334-385 (completion_params only adds extra_body['provider'] when self.provider is truthy).] Inspect AI's own documented/idiomatic OpenRouter invocation, `inspect eval <task> --model openrouter/<org>/<model>` (verbatim quickstart example: `inspect eval arc.py --model openrouter/gryphe/mythomax-l2-13b`), ships with no default quantization pin, no default require_parameters, and no default provider order/only
    → mechanism: M1 + M2 + M3 systemically, confirmed in code and docs. The docs present -M "provider={...}" (including a quantization-pinning example) purely as one of four optional custom model args in a reference table -- never a default, never recommended for reproducibility -- and the quickstart command immediately above it omits it entirely. CORRECTION to the original finding: the draft quoted docs text 'OpenRouter may distribute consecutive requests for the same model across multiple ... backends' as if a general routing-risk disclosure; in the actual source this sentence is scoped specifically to 'multiple Anthropic-compatible backends (Anthropic-direct, Bedrock, Vertex)' in the context of prompt-cache hit rates for openrouter/anthropic/* models -- not a general statement about open-weight quantization/provider spread. Citing it that way overstated what the docs warn about; the narrower, defensible claim rests on the code + the -M args table alone. DROPPED: unverified claims extending this to specific third parties (METR, Apollo Research, published scheming/sandbagging/control papers) -- no evidence for those was checked in this pass, and naming them without verification is exactly the overclaim this review is meant to catch.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[inspect_evals repo: src/inspect_evals/persistbench/results/persistbench_beneficial_memory/2026-05-22_openrouter_meta-llama_llama-3.3-70b-instruct.json (results.scores[0].metrics.failure_rate.value = 50.0) and .../2026-05-22_openrouter_openai_gpt-oss-120b.json (value = 16.0), vs. README.md 'Evaluation Report' table rows 3 and 6 (54.0 and 22.0).] ADDED: The archived raw .eval log (results/persistbench_beneficial_memory/2026-05-22_openrouter_meta-llama_llama-3.3-70b-instruct.json, status=success, task_version=2) reports failure_rate=50.0, but the README's published Evaluation Report table lists 54.0 for the identical task+model pair; the sibling gpt-oss-120b archived log reports 16.0 vs. the table's 22.0 -- 4pp and 6pp mismatches between the citable artifact and the published summary for the same nominal run.
    → mechanism: Ambiguous -- could reflect M1/M3 run-to-run routing/quantization drift, but confounded because model_generate_config={} in both archived logs, meaning subject-model temperature/seed were also never pinned, so ordinary decoding stochasticity alone could produce a 4-6 point swing on a 100-sample, 1-epoch task. Both archived and table values sit comfortably inside the paper's wide CI ([45.0,65.0] / [12.0,28.0]), so this does not change the AT-RISK verdict, but it is direct, reproducible evidence that the repo's own published numbers do not cleanly trace back to a single citable artifact.
    → severity: low | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium
Safeguards present: provenance_logging (automatic, via full raw response capture), quantizations (opt-in via provider dict, documented), order/only/sort (opt-in via provider dict, mentioned once for caching not reproducibility), require_parameters and data_collection (mechanism works via free-form dict passthrough but is completely undocumented — zero hits anywhere in repo)
One-line fix: Add `require_parameters` and `data_collection` to the documented custom-model-arg table and change the OpenRouter quickstart example to `-M "provider={'require_parameters': true, 'quantizations': [...], 'order': [...], 'data_collection': 'deny'}'` instead of a bare unpinned model string.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: Not a rerun target — it is a framework, not an experiment — but it is the REFERENCE IMPLEMENTATION for this whole exercise and the reason several other rows are zero-config. Verified in upstream source: OpenRouterAPI.__init__ collects a `provider` dict model arg and completion_params() emits it as extra_body['provider'], so `-M provider='{"only": ["cerebras/fp16"], "allow_fallbacks": false}'` pins any inspect-based eval with zero code changes. Its own source also documents cross-provider behavioural divergence: a comment notes that AkashML and Parasail reject requests with more than one system message even though the OpenAI API is permissive — corroboration that 'same model, different provider' is not only a quantization story.
Signs provider issues messed up the pape: Inspect AI is infrastructure other people run their own evals with, not itself a study that produced a results set. The main repo, the docs site, and the companion inspect_evals repo (eval task collection) contain no raw generations: none of the three publishes committed run logs, sample .eval files, transcripts, per-sample score files, or a leaderboard. The `logs/` mentions that appear are explicitly local output a user's own `inspect eval` invocation creates, not something UKGovernmentBEIS ships. The eval-logs documentation page describes the log/transcript format in prose and screenshots but links no actual sample file. Since the project has never published a dataset of its own OpenRouter-routed completions, no artifact exists for a skeptical reader to inspect for provider mixing, token soup, truncation, or any other fingerprint. The code-level "at_risk" finding remains a code-review concern only, with no observational evidence for or against it in anything this project has released.

Title: MathArena (ETH Zurich SRI Lab)
Summary: MathArena is a near-live evaluation harness/leaderboard that tests frontier LLMs on newly-released, contamination-free competition math problems (AIME, HMMT, USAMO, Putnam, etc.), publishing a widely-cited public leaderboard.
Importance/Impact: 271 GitHub stars; run by ETH Zurich's SRI lab (well-known for security/verification ML research); MathArena's near-live tracking of brand-new competition math results is frequently cited in AI-capability discourse as a trustworthy, contamination-resistant benchmark, so its leaderboard numbers get taken at face value by third parties.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter (api: openrouter) is one of ~10 API backends the unified APIClient supports; it is used to query a specific subset of models -- Llama-4-Maverick, the Kimi K2 family (k2, k2-thinking, k2.5, k2.6), Phi-4-reasoning-plus:free, the Qwen3/Qwen3.5/Qwen3-VL family, and Grok-4 -- whose transcripts/scores feed directly into the public leaderboard (13 of 140 model configs use api: openrouter). Judges/graders (main_judge, arxiv_judge, simple_judge, norm_judge) all use direct Google/other APIs, never OpenRouter.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The MathArena leaderboard row "Llama-4-Maverick" (meta-llama/llama-4-maverick, run via PureModelSolver on OpenRouter) has a provider pin — `provider: {allow_fallbacks: False, order: [Fireworks]}` — that is a code-verified no-op: it sits at the YAML top level instead of nested under `extra_body:` (the only one of 9 OpenRouter configs in the repo to do so), so it never reaches OpenRouter at all. I reproduced this empirically: installing the repo's pinned `openai==1.102.0` and calling `client.chat.completions.create(model=..., messages=..., provider={...})` raises `TypeError: Completions.create() got an unexpected keyword argument 'provider'` client-side, before any HTTP request, while the sibling pattern (`extra_body={...}`, which the 8 correctly-configured OpenRouter rows use) is confirmed by `inspect.signature` to be a real, accepted parameter of that same method. Either this row runs on different/older code than what's on `main`, or it silently falls back to fully unpinned default OpenRouter routing exactly as if no mitigation existed — while being ranked head-to-head on the public leaderboard against properly pinned and proprietary rows. Three further rows (Kimi K2, Qwen3-235B-A22B's original entry, Phi-4-reasoning-plus:free) are confirmed to have zero provider pin of any kind, each demonstrably the sole unpinned exception among its own sibling family of same-vendor OpenRouter configs.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[configs/models/moonshot/k2.yaml (api: openrouter, no provider/extra_body key at all) vs. k2-thinking.yaml / k25.yaml / k26.yaml (all `extra_body: {provider: {allow_fallbacks: False, order: [moonshotai]}}`) — leaderboard name rendered via human_readable_id (app/app.py:61-79)] Kimi K2 leaderboard row (human_readable_id "Kimi K2", model moonshotai/kimi-k2) has no OpenRouter provider pin, unlike every later sibling config for the same model family
    → mechanism: Verified by reading all 4 moonshot configs directly: k2.yaml has zero provider/extra_body key; k2-thinking.yaml (2025-11-06), k25.yaml (2026-01-27) and k26.yaml (2026-04-20) — all added after k2.yaml's 2025-07-11 date — pin `extra_body.provider: {allow_fallbacks: False, order: [moonshotai]}`. This is M1 (unpinned quantization) + M3 (probabilistic routing): with no pin, the trace runs through matharena's own APIClient.kwargs -> `client.chat.completions.create(**payload)` unfiltered (api_client.py:1745), so OpenRouter's default load-balancer picks the serving backend. Tightened from the first-pass claim: a live OpenRouter endpoints check (2026-07-19) shows moonshotai/kimi-k2 currently resolves to exactly ONE active endpoint (Novita, fp8 quant) rather than moonshotai's own first-party listing -- so today's exposure is 'stuck on one uncontrolled third-party quantized host' rather than 'switches between many providers run to run.' The multi-provider rotation risk was almost certainly larger at the row's actual 2025-07-11 evaluation time, when a high-profile fresh 1T-param MoE release typically has many more OpenRouter resellers than survive a year later -- but I could not directly verify the historical (mid-2025) provider count, only today's, so I am not asserting a specific historical N.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[configs/models/llama4/maverick.yaml (top-level `provider: {allow_fallbacks: False, order: [Fireworks]}`, no `type:` field); actual code path is runner.py:209 (`solver_config.get("type", "pure_model")`) -> runner.py:293-297 (`_prepare_default_api_client_args`; `API_CLIENT_IRRELEVANT_KEYS = ["date"]` strips only `date`) -> src/matharena/solvers/pure_model_solver.py:25 (`self.client = APIClient(**default_api_client_args)`) -> api_client.py:81 (`self.kwargs = kwargs` catch-all) -> api_client.py:223-241 (`_kwarg_remover`, never mentions `provider`) -> api_client.py:1732-1745 (`client.chat.completions.create(**payload)`)] Llama-4-Maverick leaderboard row's provider pin is a code-verified no-op: it crashes the OpenAI SDK call rather than reaching OpenRouter
    → mechanism: Confirmed maverick.yaml is the ONLY one of 9 OpenRouter configs in the repo that attempts a provider pin at the YAML top level instead of nested under `extra_body:` (the 8 that do it correctly: qwen/qwen3_30b_a3b.yaml, qwen3.5_397b.yaml, qwen3_235b_a22b_2507.yaml, qwen3_vl_235b_a22b_instruct.yaml, qwen3_vl_235b_a22b_thinking.yaml, moonshot/k2-thinking.yaml, k25.yaml, k26.yaml). README.md:150 documents `provider` as a supported optional key without specifying it must nest under `extra_body:`, a plausible source of the bug. IMPORTANT CORRECTION to the first-pass finding: maverick.yaml has no `type:` field, so it defaults to `type: pure_model` (runner.py:209) and is served by `PureModelSolver`, NOT `MathAgent` -- the first-pass citation of `math_agent.py:77-81`/`math_static_agent.py`/`selfcheck_agent.py`'s `_clean_client_args` was WRONG for this row: those are agent-scaffold-only methods this row never executes (I confirmed all three exist and none strip `provider`, but none of them run for Maverick either). The actually-relevant code is `runner.py`'s `_prepare_default_api_client_args` (only pops `date`) and `PureModelSolver.__init__`, which passes `default_api_client_args` straight into `APIClient(**...)` with no cleaning step at all. The end conclusion is unchanged and I independently re-verified it: installed the repo's pinned minimum `openai==1.102.0` in a clean venv and confirmed `client.chat.completions.create(model=..., messages=..., provider={...})` raises `TypeError: Completions.create() got an unexpected keyword argument 'provider'` client-side (no HTTP request made), while `inspect.signature` confirms `extra_body` (but not `provider`) is a real named parameter of that same method -- exactly explaining why the 8 correctly-nested sibling configs work and this one cannot.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

    → mechanism: Confirmed by reading the file directly: no provider pin of any kind. M1 + M3: the `:free` route is documented OpenRouter behavior to load-balance across whichever (often rate-limited/community) providers are currently offering the model for free, with no cost signal to detect a downgrade since the row is already priced at $0. One update from a live check (2026-07-19): OpenRouter's `/models/.../endpoints` API now returns an empty endpoint list for both `microsoft/phi-4-reasoning-plus` and `microsoft/phi-4-reasoning-plus:free` -- the model appears to have been fully discontinued on OpenRouter since MathArena's original evaluation. This doesn't change the historical exposure (the row's reported score was still produced under an unpinned `:free` route at eval time) but means the specific mechanism can no longer be reproduced or checked live today, which is itself worth noting.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

Venue: ETH Zurich SRI Lab (Martin Vechev group)
Provider A/B rerun assessment: RECOMMENDED #1 — zero-config. configs/models/moonshot/k26.yaml already carries `extra_body: {provider: {allow_fallbacks: False, order: [moonshotai]}}`, splatted verbatim into the OpenAI SDK call at src/matharena/runner.py:293; six sibling configs use the same shape, so pinning is an established in-repo pattern and needs no source edit — copy the YAML and change the `order` list. Notable in its own right: the pinned `moonshotai` endpoint is int4 on OpenRouter, so MathArena's published Kimi-K2.6 leaderboard number is measured on an int4 backend while an fp8 endpoint (streamlake/fp8) of the same slug exists. A/B = moonshotai/int4 ($0.95/$4.00 per Mtok) vs streamlake/fp8 ($0.855/$3.60), both ~100% uptime. Amplification is frontier-reasoning: near-threshold competition math where one flipped token changes the final boxed integer, auto-graded so no judge confound. 30 problems x n=4 x 2 competitions x 3 conditions (strong / weak / strong-replicate for the noise floor) = 720 samples, ~7.2M output tokens, ~$42 total, ~1.9 h at the repo's own concurrent_requests=32.
Signs provider issues messed up the pape: Across MathArena's published raw-generation data, microsoft/phi-4-reasoning-plus:free is the only one of 159 model configs in the eth-sri/matharena repo tagged ":free," and its outputs hit output_tokens==31000 on exactly 25 of 120 AIME 2025 rows and 38 of 120 HMMT Feb 2025 rows, with the maximum output length capped at 31000 in both datasets despite the config's max_tokens: 31500 — a 500-token shortfall that repeats identically across two independent competitions. Row problem_idx=14, idx_answer=1 shows this directly: the assistant's response text cuts off mid-sentence at "...We want this to be a perfect square of something of," with no closing and no boxed answer, and the recorded parsed_answer of 66248 (gold_answer 60, correct False) is simply the coefficient lifted from the truncated line "147196 - 66248√3" — a value outside AIME's valid 0-999 answer range, the signature of truncation followed by garbage answer-extraction rather than a reasoning error. The same two files show comparison models running well within their configured limits: Llama-4-Maverick tops out at 2080/3025 output tokens against a configured 32000, and Kimi K2 Thinking at 115084/91400 against a configured 256000, with Kimi's assistant-turn structure a clean Counter of {('cot','response'): 118, ('cot','response','cot','response'): 2} across all 120 rows in both datasets. Searching all three models' all_messages text for special tokens (<|eot_id|>, <|im_start|>, </s>, <|endoftext|>), the U+FFFD replacement character, and literal think-tags returns zero hits. No file in the repo — not any config, not api_client.py — hardcodes a 31000-token constant anywhere, so the ceiling does not originate in the harness itself. Nor does any config or code path set a provider-routing block for any model, including Phi-4: no provider: key, no quantization or endpoint filter, no explicit pin — requests to OpenRouter carry only model, max_tokens, temperature, and top_p, leaving this free-tier model's routing fully default and unpinned. That absence is itself the exposure: an unpinned free-tier OpenRouter route appears to impose a completion-length wall well below the declared max_tokens, corrupting a nontrivial share of scored answers through truncation followed by nonsense answer-extraction.

Title: OSWorld (NeurIPS 2024 D&B) — mm_agents/{maestro,os_symphony,vlaa_gui} OpenRouter engine
Summary: OSWorld is the 369-task real-computer-use benchmark from the NeurIPS 2024 Datasets & Benchmarks paper. Its repo also hosts a growing library of third-party agent baselines under mm_agents/ (added well after the original paper); several of these (maestro, os_symphony, vlaa_gui) implement an OpenRouter engine as one of several pluggable LMM backends.
Importance/Impact: 3,019 GitHub stars; NeurIPS 2024 D&B paper; the standard real-computer-use benchmark for GUI/computer-use agents. Note: the OpenRouter-touching code is NOT part of the original paper's headline numbers — maestro/os_symphony/vlaa_gui are later community-contributed agent baselines (they reference gpt-5, GLM-4.5, MiniMax-M3, Kimi-K2 — models that didn't exist in April 2024) bundled into the same widely-used repo as pluggable agents researchers can run against OSWorld and report scores for.
What they use OpenRouter for: Agent rollout / action-generation backend, not just a judge: LMMEngineOpenRouter's output is parsed into the next pyautogui/tool action during live OSWorld task execution. In maestro's shipped default config (mm_agents/maestro/tools/new_tools_config.json), 10 of the agent's core cognitive roles (planner_role, supplement_role, dag_translator, operator_role, technician_role, analyst_role, worker_success_role, worker_stale_role, periodic_role, final_check_role, objective_alignment) default to "provider": "openrouter", "model_name": "openai/o3" — confirmed as the actual out-of-the-box config via the hardcoded load path in mm_agents/maestro/maestro/controller/config_manager.py:30-34.
Mistakes (null if safe): The maestro agent's shipped default config routes most of its decision-making roles through OpenRouter via the bare, undated slug "openai/o3" (M6) with data_collection left at OpenRouter's default "allow" (M5) despite full desktop screenshots being sent as images with every call, and the OpenRouter engine wrapper (duplicated near-verbatim in os_symphony and vlaa_gui) discards the API response after pulling out `.choices[0].message.content` and token usage, so the actually-served provider / generation id is never recorded anywhere (M4). Because this is the default action-generation policy for the maestro agent, any OSWorld benchmark score reported for maestro (or the analogous os_symphony/vlaa_gui agents) run with the shipped OpenRouter config cannot be tied to a specific backend snapshot, and could silently drift if OpenAI updates what "o3" resolves to behind that alias. Corrupting risks that would matter most for open-weight models — M1 unpinned quantization, M3 probabilistic provider load-balancing, M8 cross-provider confound — are not seriously in play here because every model actually routed through OpenRouter in this codebase (openai/*, x-ai/*, anthropic/*, google/*) is proprietary and effectively single-provider, so this does not corrupt the benchmark's task-success measurements the way it would for an open-weight policy model; the remaining risk is reproducibility/audit-trail and data-governance, not silently-wrong scores.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: Agentic Lybic's (mm_agents/maestro) published SOTA claim of 57.07% success rate on OSWorld (50-step, Table 1/abstract of arXiv:2509.11067 — all four numbers verified exact by direct fetch: 57.07% vs CoAct-1 w/ o3 56.39%, Agent S2.5 w/ o3 54.21%, Jedi-7B w/ o3 50.65%) is produced by routing 11 of 19 tool-config entries (corrected from the first-pass agent's miscounted "10 of ~15") — all core reasoning roles (Controller/Manager/Technician/Operator/Analyst plus dag_translator/worker_success/worker_stale/periodic/final_check/objective_alignment) — through OpenRouter via the bare, undated, single-provider slug "openai/o3". The paper's own text (verified exact quote) names mm_agents/maestro as its reproducibility artifact, the shipped config is loaded unconditionally (not opt-in), OpenRouter's data_collection is left at its "allow" default, and the engine code (verified exact) discards the full response object down to (content, total_tokens, cost) — so no one, including the authors, can verify which backend actually served the o3 calls behind the SOTA number.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[Table 1 / abstract, arXiv:2509.11067 ("Agentic Lybic: Multi-Agent Execution System with Tiered Reasoning and Orchestration") — verified via arxiv.org/html/2509.11067; paper states (exact quote) "Our implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/xlang-ai/OSWorld/tree/main/mm_agents/maestro to facilitate reproducibility and future research." Code verified by cloning xlang-ai/OSWorld: mm_agents/maestro/tools/new_tools_config.json and mm_agents/maestro/core/engine.py:601-642 (LMMEngineOpenRouter class).] "Agentic Lybic achieves a state-of-the-art 57.07% success rate in 50 steps" on OSWorld, beating CoAct-1 w/ o3 (56.39%), Agent S2.5 w/ o3 (54.21%), Jedi-7B w/ o3 (50.65%) — all four figures verified verbatim against the paper.
    → mechanism: Paper states (verified exact quote): "For all core reasoning components (Controller, Manager, and the three Worker roles - Technician, Operator, and Analyst), we utilize OpenAI o3." The shipped config is loaded unconditionally via a hardcoded path in ConfigManager.load_tools_configuration (not opt-in). CORRECTION to first-pass count: it routes 11 of 19 tool entries through provider:"openrouter", model_name:"openai/o3" (not "10 of ~15") — planner_role, supplement_role, dag_translator, operator_role, technician_role, analyst_role, worker_success_role, worker_stale_role, periodic_role, final_check_role, AND objective_alignment (omitted from the first-pass enumeration). M6 (version drift): verified OpenRouter's own model page lists only a bare "openai/o3" slug, single-provider (OpenAI direct, per OpenRouter's own text "hosted by one provider") — so per this project's taxonomy M1/M3/M8 legitimately don't apply (proprietary single-served model), but the slug remains unpinned to a dated snapshot, undercutting the paper's stated reproducibility goal. M5 (data-policy leakage): LMMEngineOpenRouter (engine.py:601-642, verified) never sets data_collection or any provider-routing extra_body anywhere in the file — confirmed by grep — so OpenRouter's default "allow" policy applies while full desktop screenshots are sent on every call, undisclosed in the paper. M4 (no provenance logging): engine.generate() (verified exact code, lines 639-642) executes `content = response.choices[0].message.content`, `total_tokens, cost = calculate_tokens_and_cost(response, self.provider, self.model)`, `return content, total_tokens, cost` — self.provider here is just the internal literal string "llm-openrouter", not OpenRouter's actual per-response serving-provider field, and the full response object is discarded — so neither the authors nor anyone reproducing 57.07% can verify which backend served the o3 calls.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[CORRECTED from first-pass: mm_agents/os_symphony/core/mllm.py (`elif engine_type == "open_router":` dispatch — this file has no ENGINE_REGISTRY dict) and mm_agents/vlaa_gui/core/mllm.py (`ENGINE_REGISTRY = {"open_router": LMMEngineOpenRouter, "anthropic_bedrock": LMMEngineAnthropicBedrock, ...}` — verified this literal dict exists only in vlaa_gui, not os_symphony); the LMMEngineOpenRouter classes themselves are defined in each agent's core/engine.py. Contrast with OS-Symphony Table 1 (arXiv:2601.07779) and VLAA-GUI Table 2 (arXiv:2604.21375), both verified via arxiv.org/html fetch.] Downstream/class risk: OSWorld runs of mm_agents/os_symphony or mm_agents/vlaa_gui that select an OpenRouter engine would inherit the same M4/M5/M6 exposure (both ship a structurally near-identical LMMEngineOpenRouter class, verified by direct code read, that also discards the full response and never sets data_collection). This is distinct from the two agents' own currently-published headline numbers — OS-Symphony's 65.84% (Table 1, arXiv:2601.07779, backbone "GPT-5", 100-step) and VLAA-GUI's 77.45%/74.89% (Table 2, arXiv:2604.21375, backbones Claude Opus 4.6/4.5) — all four figures verified exact against the papers, neither of which routes through OpenRouter.
    → mechanism: Same M4/M5/M6 pattern would apply to anyone who passes engine_type="open_router" (os_symphony) or model_provider="open_router" (vlaa_gui, reachable via ENGINE_REGISTRY, unrestricted CLI string), but as published neither paper's headline run uses it: verified scripts/python/run_multienv_os_symphony.py defaults --orchestrator_provider to "openai" (direct); verified mm_agents/vlaa_gui/README.md line 66 states (exact quote) "# Main reasoning model (Anthropic Bedrock — uses default AWS credential chain)" for its setup instructions. Both papers' methods sections (verified) name only direct OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/ByteDance API access and never mention OpenRouter. So this is a latent/inherited risk for future reproductions and leaderboard submissions of these two agents, not a confirmed distortion of an already-reported number — severity and dependency correctly rated low.

One-line fix: In LMMEngineOpenRouter.generate() (engine.py in maestro/os_symphony/vlaa_gui), add extra_body={"provider": {"data_collection": "deny", "require_parameters": True}} and persist response.provider/response.id alongside each generation; pin "openai/o3" to a dated OpenRouter snapshot if one is exposed.

Title: OpenHands (formerly OpenDevin)
Summary: OpenHands is a widely-used (81k+ star) open-source platform for CodeAct-style generalist coding/browsing agents, evaluated across SWE-bench, WebArena, GAIA, and 15+ other benchmarks; its actual LLM-calling code now lives in a companion SDK repo, OpenHands/software-agent-sdk.
Importance/Impact: 81,138 GitHub stars; one of the most widely deployed open-source coding-agent platforms; backbone for numerous downstream research forks/evals (per discovery evidence: SkyRL-OpenHands, ReaComp, DRPBench, rdi-berkeley/agents-last-exam) that wire their reported numbers through this exact OpenRouter config path.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is one of many pluggable "LLM Provider" backends an OpenHands user can select in Settings to run the agent's rollouts (coding tasks, SWE-bench-style, web browsing), implemented as a generic LiteLLM pass-through in openhands-sdk/openhands/sdk/llm/llm.py. Separately, OpenHands' own legacy AlgoTune benchmark adapter used OpenRouter directly to compare several open-weight models on a leaderboard.
Mistakes (null if safe): OpenHands ships zero default OpenRouter safeguards anywhere in its LLM class: no quantizations pin (M1), sampling params (temperature/top_p/seed/reasoning_effort) sent with no require_parameters (M2), no order/only/sort so routing stays load-balanced (M3), data_collection is never set so it defaults to "allow" (M5). The only lever is `litellm_extra_body`, an empty dict by default that a user must populate themselves with a `provider` block to get any of the above — nothing in the product docs (openrouter.mdx) shows this or warns about reproducibility, and the documented example model string is a bare undated slug (`openrouter/anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet`, M6). Provenance logging (`log_completions`, which would capture the raw served response) defaults to False (M4) — so served-provider/generation-id is not recorded unless a user explicitly opts in. Concretely, legacy/algotune/adapter/utils.py:36-39 shows OpenHands itself comparing multiple open-weight models (GLM-4.5, Qwen3-Coder, GPT-OSS-120b) served over unpinned OpenRouter routes on a leaderboard with no visible provider constraint (M8). Docs give zero routing/reproducibility guidance (M10). Mitigating factor: OpenHands' own currently-maintained, canonical eval harness (github.com/OpenHands/benchmarks/.llm_config/example.json) routes its flagship SWE-bench-style numbers through a self-hosted `litellm_proxy/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514` proxy straight to Anthropic, NOT OpenRouter — so OpenHands' own headline paper/leaderboard claims are not directly corrupted. The risk this taxonomy flags falls mainly on (a) the historical AlgoTune leaderboard numbers, and (b) the many downstream research repos that reuse this exact zero-default OpenRouter integration for their own reported results.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: Confirmed by direct extraction of the arXiv:2407.16741 PDF (pdftotext, all pages): the string "openrouter" appears zero times in the paper. Every OpenHands-attributed results row in Tables 3-6 ('OH CodeActAgent v1.5/v1.8', 'OH BrowsingAgent v1.0', 'OH GPTSwarm') uses a directly-called model: gpt-4o-2024-05-13, gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18, gpt-3.5-turbo / gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 / gpt-3.5-turbo-16k(-0613), gpt-4-turbo, or claude-3-5-sonnet(-20240620) -- confirming the candidate's headline claim is accurate as stated. (claude-3-opus appears only as part of a third-party baseline row, 'Aider: gpt-4o & claude-3-opus', not an OpenHands-run result.) The OpenRouter risk is real but sits entirely downstream and outside the paper, in the eval harness that now lives in the separate github.com/OpenHands/benchmarks repo (not, as the original candidate stated, inside github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands, whose main branch has pivoted entirely to the unrelated 'Agent Canvas' product and contains no eval code at all).
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[CORRECTED from the candidate: the eval harness is NOT at 'evaluation/benchmarks/{swe_bench,gaia,webarena}' inside github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands -- that path 404s on main today (verified via `gh api repos/OpenHands/OpenHands/contents/evaluation?ref=main`). As of 2026-06, OpenHands/OpenHands's README states the repo has pivoted entirely to 'Agent Canvas' (a product control-plane); it contains no LLM-calling or eval code. The real, currently-maintained harness lives in the separate repo github.com/OpenHands/benchmarks (README: 'migrating the benchmarks from OpenHands V0... to work with the OpenHands Software Agent SDK'), where SWE-Bench, SWE-Bench Pro, and GAIA are listed '✅ Active' but WebArena/VisualWebArena are demoted to benchmarks/legacy/{webarena,visualwebarena} (unmaintained) -- so WebArena should be dropped or heavily qualified as a live downstream-risk vector. The OpenRouter docs page also does NOT live in github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands; it is at docs/openhands/usage/llms/openrouter.mdx in the separate repo github.com/OpenHands/docs (fetched and confirmed verbatim: example model string is the bare undated `openrouter/anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet`, zero mention of provider pinning/quantization/reproducibility). None of this is inside arXiv:2407.16741 itself.] Class-of-results risk (not a specific corrupted number): downstream SWE-bench Verified / GAIA leaderboard entries and model-release blog posts of the form 'OpenHands + <open-weight model> resolved X%' inherit OpenHands' zero-safeguard default OpenRouter path when the model is served via OpenRouter. Verified-real examples of open-weight models OpenHands' own code names in an OpenRouter context: GLM-4.5, Qwen3-Coder, GPT-OSS-120b (found verbatim in benchmarks/legacy/algotune/adapter/utils.py:36-38 as `openrouter/z-ai/glm-4.5`, `openrouter/qwen/qwen3-coder`, `openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b`). DeepSeek-V3/R1, Kimi K2, GLM-4.6, and Llama-3.1-405B (present in the original candidate's example list) were NOT found anywhere in the repos checked -- they are plausible illustrative examples (all are real OpenRouter-servable open-weight models compatible with OpenHands' litellm-based `model` string config) but I could not independently confirm a specific published leaderboard row using them through OpenHands+OpenRouter, so they should be read as hypothetical, not observed.
    → mechanism: M1/M2/M3/M4 confirmed by re-cloning github.com/OpenHands/software-agent-sdk (current HEAD) and reading openhands-sdk/openhands/sdk/llm/llm.py directly: `litellm_extra_body: dict[str, Any] = Field(default_factory=dict)` (line 507, empty by default -- the only channel for an OpenRouter `provider:{...}` block), `log_completions: bool = Field(default=False)` (line 421, so served-provider/generation-id is not recorded unless a user opts in -- M4), `openrouter_site_url`/`openrouter_app_name` (lines 673-676) only set cosmetic HTTP-Referer/X-Title headers, not routing/reproducibility controls. `grep -rn "require_parameters|quantizations|data_collection" openhands-sdk/` returns zero hits repo-wide. Both benchmarks/swebench and benchmarks/gaia use the identical generic `llm_config.json` pattern (`{"model": ..., "base_url": ..., "api_key": ...}`, confirmed in .llm_config/example.json and gaia/README.md), so nothing benchmark-specific adds a safeguard -- any user pointing `model` at `openrouter/...` gets the SDK's default (unpinned quantization, no require_parameters, no order/only/sort, no provenance).
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium
One-line fix: Ship a safe default `provider` block (require_parameters:true, data_collection:"deny", pinned quantizations, order/sort set) whenever the OpenRouter backend is selected, and default `log_completions` on (or otherwise persist the served-provider/generation-id) so the actually-served weights are pinned and recorded instead of left to each downstream user/fork to configure themselves.

Grepping literal `<|...|>` tokens across this file returns 234 hits, all confined to these same two instances, versus zero hits across two pinned non-OpenRouter negative-control logs of 300 instances each (Fireworks-direct llama-v3p1-70b and DeepSeek-direct deepseek-chat) — topically unrelated word salad (F1) plus literal EOS-token litter surviving into prose (F4), not documented code-switching. The `empty_generation: true` field is not distinctive: it fires on 10-29% of instances in every file checked, including both pinned negative controls, so it carries no diagnostic weight on its own. Pure repetition loops without special tokens, such as a 24-line "static\nstatic\n..." loop, also occur in the DeepSeek-direct negative control, so repetition in isolation isn't diagnostic — only the co-occurring literal special-token litter is. The extent is small: these are 2 of 300 instances (0.67%) in this one run.

Title: Prometheus 2 / BiGGen-Bench (KAIST AI)
Summary: BiGGen-Bench evaluates 103 LLMs (28 base + 61 chat, run locally via vllm, plus 14 proprietary API models) across 9 capabilities using 5 evaluator LMs (Prometheus-2 variants + Claude-3-Opus + GPT-4-1106 + GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09); prometheus-eval is the companion open-source judge-LM library.
Importance/Impact: 1102 GitHub stars; NAACL 2025 + EMNLP 2024 Findings; Prometheus 1 has 300+ citations and Prometheus 2/BiGGen-Bench are widely used to power a public HF leaderboard (prometheus-eval/BiGGen-Bench-Leaderboard) and BiGGen-Bench-Results dataset, so this repo's model-serving hygiene affects a benchmark other papers cite as ground truth.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter (via litellm's `acompletion`/`completion`, wrapped in prometheus_eval/litellm.py's LiteLLM/AsyncLiteLLM classes) is used by BiGGen-Bench/run_api_inference.py to generate candidate responses from API-only ("proprietary") LLMs for the benchmark — these responses are then scored by the 5 evaluator LMs and feed directly into the paper's Table 1, Figures 4-5 (performance-vs-size trends), and Table 4 (the paper's headline "gap between open-source and proprietary LMs" analysis). The same wrapper is also written to support calling an external LLM (e.g. GPT-4) as judge in run_response_eval.py, though that specific call is currently commented out in favor of a MockLLM stub in the public repo.
Mistakes (null if safe): M4 (no provenance logging): confirmed — run_api_inference.py:70 only records the CLI `model_name` string as `response_model_name`; the actual served provider/backend/generation id for the OpenRouter-routed "proprietary LM" responses is never captured anywhere, so if OpenRouter ever silently switched backend for one of these models (e.g., OpenAI-direct vs Azure-hosted GPT-4), it would be undetectable and unreproducible. This threatens reproducibility of the candidate responses feeding Table 1/Figs 4-5/Table 4. M5 (data-policy leakage): confirmed — `data_collection` is never set to "deny" anywhere in the codebase, so prompts (including Safety-capability tasks about harm/secrets/moral dilemmas) default to OpenRouter's permissive data-collection routing. Doesn't corrupt results but is an unaddressed privacy/policy gap for a paper that explicitly discusses "Potential Risks" of its safety content without covering this angle. M6 (model version drift): the library's own documented usage example (libs/prometheus-eval/README.md:70) calls an undated `gpt-4-turbo` alias, and `run_api_inference.py --model_name` accepts any free-form string with no enforcement of dated slugs — this threatens exact reproducibility of any candidate-model responses generated this way, since OpenAI/other providers periodically repoint undated aliases to new checkpoints. M10 (no reporting): the BiGGen-Bench paper's Limitations section covers several reproducibility threats (stochastic generation, verbosity bias, self-enhancement bias) at length but is silent on the API-routing/inference-stack risk for the subset of the 103 evaluated models obtained via OpenRouter, despite this feeding the paper's own headline "gap between open-source and proprietary LMs" comparison (Table 4).
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The strongest surviving claim is a provenance gap, not a proven corruption: run_api_inference.py generates the proprietary-model responses feeding Table 4/11's headline Hedges's-g gap (verified: g=0.51 avg, 0.36-0.84 by capability, exact match to paper), and run_response_eval.py is the documented (but publicly mocked-out) call site for the GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09 judge behind Figure 4-5/Table 1-2/Table 7 -- and neither script records which backend/provider actually served any call (M4, no provenance logging, already the project's own established verdict for this repo). But there is no plausible OpenRouter-specific corruption mechanism: every model involved (GPT-4 family, Claude-3 family, Gemini-Pro-1.5) is a closed, single-vendor model with no alternate quantized backend for OpenRouter to route between (M1/M3/M8 excluded), the judge uses free-text parsing with no structured-output reliance (M9 excluded), and the one confirmed code bug -- AsyncLiteLLM silently drops all sampling kwargs before calling acompletion() -- is router-independent and would occur identically via direct OpenAI/Anthropic keys. Net effect: real reproducibility/documentation gaps exist, but no evidence any reported number was altered by OpenRouter routing specifically.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[Table 11 (Appendix D.3, confirmed exact section) / Table 4 (main text, Section 4, confirmed exact section)] Table 4/Table 11 Hedges's g effect sizes (Average 0.51; Instruction Following 0.38; Grounding 0.49; Planning 0.58; Reasoning 0.65; Refinement 0.46; Safety 0.36; Theory of Mind 0.59; Tool Usage 0.58; Multilingual 0.84) -- all 10 values independently verified exact against the arXiv PDF. run_api_inference.py's AsyncLiteLLM wrapper does generate the proprietary-side candidate responses for this comparison, and README.md:73 verbatim confirms 'we mainly used OpenAI API and OpenRouter' for that exact script; litellm.py:71-86's _get_completion_text_async is independently confirmed to accept **kwargs but never forward them into acompletion() (only model/api_base/messages/request_timeout=60 are passed), so the temperature=1.0/top_p=0.9/max_tokens=2048 params never reach any backend. HOWEVER two corrections to the original framing: (1) this kwargs-drop bug is backend-independent -- it would occur identically via direct OpenAI/Anthropic/Google keys, so it is not evidence OpenRouter specifically altered anything; (2) M8 (cross-provider confound) does not meaningfully apply because every compared proprietary model (GPT-4 variants, Claude-3 variants, Gemini-Pro-1.5) is a single-vendor closed model with no alternate quantized/third-party OpenRouter backend to switch between -- this matches the taxonomy's own explicit carve-out and this project's own already-filed verdict for this exact repo (mistake_ids = M4/M5/M6/M10, explicitly NOT M2/M8). The only mechanism that survives is M4 (no provenance logging): response_model_name only stores the raw CLI string, so nothing about which backend actually served each proprietary response is recorded, and the Appendix G claim of temperature=0.0 additionally conflicts with run_api_inference.py's actual default of temperature=1.0 for the language-model-being-evaluated -- a documented paper-vs-code hyperparameter mismatch, independent of any routing question.
    → mechanism: M4 (no provenance logging) -- NOT M8/M2 as originally claimed. The kwargs-drop bug is real and verified but router-independent; M8 is contradicted by this project's own established analysis of this repo (single-served proprietary models, no quantization/provider spread available on OpenRouter for closed models).

[Table 3 (Section 5.1, confirmed) plus Figures 4-5 and Tables 1-2, 7 (Section 4/Appendix C.1, confirmed), which all use GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09 as evaluator LM. Hyperparameter quote ('temperature 0.0... for both the evaluator language model and the language model being evaluated') is in the Appendix G intro paragraph, not 'Appendix G.1' as originally cited -- G.1 is the separate 'List of Evaluator LMs' subsection.] GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09 is confirmed as the sole evaluator behind Figure 4, Figure 5, Table 1, Table 2, and Table 7 (all verified via exact captions/prose: 'evaluated... by GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09 as the evaluator LM', 'judged by GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09'), giving this call site an unusually large blast radius. However the cited Table 3 Pearson-correlation numbers are WRONG: 'Claude-3-Opus 0.624, GPT-4-1106 0.641, GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09 0.647' are actually that table's Instruction Following column, not the Average/overall correlation. The real issue is that run_response_eval.py's production judge call is mocked out in the public repo, so whether/how OpenRouter (vs. direct API keys) served the GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09 judgments -- and whether the same router-independent kwargs-drop bug silently defeated the paper's temperature=0.0 reproducibility claim for the judge -- cannot be verified either way.

One-line fix: In prometheus_eval/litellm.py, forward `**kwargs` into the `acompletion()`/`completion()` calls and set `extra_body={"provider": {"data_collection": "deny", "require_parameters": True}}` plus store `response.get("provider")` and `response.id` alongside each output in run_api_inference.py's result dict; also require dated model slugs for any `--model_name` passed to run_api_inference.py.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: REJECTED — needs glue code; amplification 6/10 (LLM-as-judge score drift, frontier-difficulty reasoning). Blocker: litellm.py:71-86 `_get_completion_text_async` silently drops all **kwargs before calling acompletion() — must be edited before ANY extra param (temperature, extra_body/provider, etc.) reaches the OpenRouter request body; this is a real bug independent of our experiment.; run_response_eval.py:124-125 hardcodes MockLLM for any non-Prometheu...
Signs provider issues messed up the pape: The BiGGen-Bench-Results dataset published on Hugging Face (prometheus-eval/BiGGen-Bench-Results, data/llm_as_a_judge-0000{0,1}-of-00002.parquet) contains 68,805 rows across 99 models, with a `response` column holding full raw completion text alongside model_name, judge scores/feedback (prometheus_8x7b_*, gpt4_*, claude_score), and human_score. The schema contains no provider, generation-id, or finish_reason column anywhere. The 14 API-routed proprietary models — claude-3-haiku/sonnet/opus, gemini-1.0-pro/pro-1.5/flash-1.5, gpt-3.5-turbo-1106, gpt-4-0125-preview, gpt-4-1106-preview, gpt-4-turbo-2024-04-09, gpt-4o-2024-05-13, mistral-large/medium-hjpark, and qwen-110b-chat — each have exactly 695 rows (9,730 total), none empty. The three longest completions among these models are claude-3-opus-20240229/tool_usage_multi_step_8 at 8,811 characters, gpt-4-turbo-2024-04-09/grounding_json_csv_xml_0 at 8,063 characters, and gpt-4o-2024-05-13/theory_of_mind_writing_a_speech_6 at 7,510 characters, each ending in a complete, well-punctuated sentence rather than cut off mid-word. A substring search across all 9,730 rows for the chat-template artifacts `<|eot_id|>`, `<|im_start|>`, `</s>`, `[INST]`, and the U+FFFD replacement character returns zero hits. The project's GitHub README (prometheus-eval/prometheus-eval/BiGGen-Bench/README.md) states "With the help of litellm, you can use various APIs from different providers. We mainly used OpenAI API and OpenRouter" — a methods disclosure, not evidence of what actually happened — and run_api_inference.py sets max_tokens=2048 uniformly while delegating provider selection entirely to litellm with no explicit base_url or provider pin. This rules out word salad, truncation, and chat-template litter (F1, F3, F4) in the API-routed completions, but says nothing about which provider or quantization level actually served each request: no provider metadata exists in this schema to check (F2 stays open), and there is no matched full-precision arm to compare against (F15 is unaddressed).

Title: openbench (Groq)
Summary: openbench is Groq's official, provider-agnostic Inspect-AI-based evaluation harness (95+ benchmarks including GPQA Diamond, AIME, MATH, MathVista, MathArena) that ships OpenRouter as a first-class model backend via a dedicated `OpenRouterAPI` wrapper.
Importance/Impact: Groq-authored official org repo, 784 GitHub stars, positioned as general-purpose provider-agnostic eval infrastructure (built on UK AISI's Inspect AI) covering 95+ benchmarks including GPQA Diamond, multiple years of AIME, MATH, MathVista, and a MathArena mirror. Used by third parties to generate benchmark numbers they may report/cite.
What they use OpenRouter for: Dedicated OpenRouter provider wrapper (OpenRouterAPI, subclassing Inspect AI's OpenAICompatibleAPI) used as one of 30+ selectable backends to run chat-completion calls for openbench's 95+ evaluation suites (GPQA Diamond, AIME 2023-2025, MATH, MathVista, MathArena, MMLU, medical/legal/reasoning QA sets, etc.), producing the accuracy scores that are the tool's entire output/deliverable.
Mistakes (null if safe): The repo is best understood as infrastructure whose safety depends entirely on how a downstream user invokes it — but its shipped defaults and its own documented examples for the highest-stakes evals never demonstrate a safe invocation, threatening any benchmark score (GPQA Diamond, AIME, MATH, MathVista, MathArena, MMLU, etc.) a user reports after running `bench eval <task> --model openrouter/<open-weight-model>` without manually adding `-M` flags. M1 (unpinned quantization): default `provider` payload omits `quantizations`, so OpenRouter can silently serve int4/int8 weights, directly threatening any accuracy number from a reasoning/math eval, which is exactly the class of benchmark most sensitive to precision loss. M2 (silent parameter dropping): evals like gpqa_diamond.py set `temperature=0.5` and the CLI exposes `--seed`, but `require_parameters` is never set by default, so a provider that doesn't honor these params drops them without error, silently changing the sampling distribution behind a reported score. M3 (probabilistic provider routing): the base OpenRouterAPI ships no `order`/`only`/`sort` by default, so repeated runs of the same eval load-balance across different backend providers/engines — this is only mitigated in ~10 non-critical eval docstrings via `-M only=groq`, and never demonstrated for GPQA/AIME/MATH/MathArena. M5 (data-policy leakage): `data_collection` defaults to OpenRouter's server-side "allow", meaning eval prompts (which may include held-out benchmark items) can be logged/trained on by third-party providers unless a user manually passes `-M data_collection=deny`. M7 (seed→determinism): `--seed`/`BENCH_SEED` is documented as producing "deterministic generation" with no caveat that provider routing/backend choice breaks that guarantee, and the same param is subject to M2's silent-drop risk. Mitigating factor working against a harsher verdict: unlike most repos, ALL the necessary safeguards (quantizations, require_parameters, order/only/sort, data_collection) are fully implemented and exposed as first-class configurable `-M` flags — this is well above the median repo's OpenRouter hygiene — and the README's "Reproducibility Statement" shows the maintainers are aware quantization variance is a real issue, even though they don't act on that awareness by defaulting to safe settings or by logging provenance.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: Every OpenRouter invocation actually written out in openbench's own docs/README omits at least one of the three real safety controls (quantization pin, require_parameters, data_collection), and no single documented example combines all three. Confirmed: the flagship docs/providers.mdx example (`only=groq,together -M sort=price`) sets no quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection; the Tip callout (`-M only=groq`) sets none of the three; docs/index.mdx and docs/release-notes.mdx (`bench eval arc_agi_2 --model openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3.1`) show bare `openrouter/<model>` invocations with zero `-M` flags at all; and the one example anywhere in the docs that does set `quantizations=int8` (docs/release-notes.mdx L144-147) still leaves require_parameters and data_collection unset. Because OpenRouterAPI (src/openbench/model/_providers/openrouter.py L42-116, confirmed) builds `provider_params`/`self._extra_body` purely from constructor args that default to None and injects nothing when they're all None, any of the 95+ documented benchmark suites (README.md L27, confirmed) run by copying these examples verbatim inherits unpinned-quantization (M1) and silently-dropped-parameter (M2) risk, plus non-reproducible cross-/undeclared-provider routing (M3) wherever `only`/`order` aren't pinned to a single provider.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[docs/providers.mdx lines 119-140 ('OpenRouter Configuration Options' section, code block after the parameter table)] The flagship OpenRouter usage example in openbench's own docs sets `-M only=groq,together -M sort=price` but no `-M quantizations=`: `bench eval mmlu --model openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b --max-connections 100 -M only=groq,together -M sort=price` (docs/providers.mdx, 'OpenRouter Configuration Options' section). VERIFIED verbatim against the live docs file (found at lines 134-140, formatted across multiple lines in the source, content identical to the quoted command). VERIFIED that no `quantizations=` or `require_parameters=` appears anywhere in the example.
    → mechanism: M1 (confirmed): no `quantizations=` is set for gpt-oss-120b, an open-weight MoE model, so OpenRouter can serve it at any quantization down to int4 from either backend. M3 (confirmed, but note the parameter-table's own illustrative row for `require_parameters` shows the value `False` as its example, i.e. the doc's own reference table models the unsafe setting): restricting to `only=groq,together` still leaves two backends in play with no `order=`, so routing is not pinned to one provider and is not guaranteed reproducible run-to-run; `require_parameters` is also left unset here, so M2 (silently dropped sampling params) applies to this same example too, which the original write-up omitted. The `sort=price` clause is better classified per this project's own taxonomy as M12 (actively selecting the cheap/degraded route) rather than M3 — it deterministically prefers the cheaper of the constrained providers rather than adding randomness — but it still correlates with M1's degradation risk, which is the accurate framing (downgraded the 'M3/cost-bias' label in the original candidate). This is the primary worked example in the repo's main provider-configuration reference doc, so any MMLU (or other benchmark, since only the task name changes) score produced by copying it verbatim inherits M1 and M2/M3 risk.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[docs/providers.mdx lines 66-67, `<Tip>` box under 'General Model Configuration'] The docs' secondary OpenRouter 'Tip' example: `bench eval simpleqa --model openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b -M only=groq` (docs/providers.mdx, Tip callout). VERIFIED verbatim at lines 66-67, directly under the 'General Model Configuration' heading (line 59).
    → mechanism: M1 (confirmed): no `quantizations=` pin for gpt-oss-120b. M2 (confirmed): `require_parameters` defaults to `None` in the OpenRouterAPI constructor (verified: `require_parameters: bool | None = None` at openrouter.py, and only injected into `provider_params` `if require_parameters is not None`), so an unsupported sampling/format param is silently dropped rather than erroring. Restricting to a single named provider (`only=groq`) does genuinely eliminate cross-provider load-balancing for the request (M3 is essentially moot here, more than 'meaningfully reduced' as the original candidate hedged), which is why this example is lower severity than Finding 1 despite sharing the same M1/M2 exposure.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[src/openbench/model/_providers/openrouter.py lines 42-116 (constructor defaults all `None`; `provider_params` dict built only from non-None args; `self._extra_body` stays `{}` and the request-patching block is skipped entirely when empty) combined with docs/release-notes.mdx lines 30-43 (ARC-AGI section) and README.md line 27 ('95+ Benchmarks') and lines 118-142 ('Common eval Configuration Options' table: `--seed`/`BENCH_SEED` default `None` at row 129, `--temperature`/`BENCH_TEMPERATURE` default `0.6` at row 126 — note this README-documented default of 0.6 is NOT what the CLI code actually defaults to; src/openbench/_cli/eval_command.py line 512-518 shows `temperature: Optional[float] = None` in code, so the README table itself appears stale on this one field; this discrepancy does not affect the M7 mechanism, which concerns seed/provider-routing interaction, not the temperature value)] The general pattern of naive `openrouter/<open-weight-model>` invocations with zero `-M` flags, documented across openbench's own materials, is not merely hypothetical: a real doc example exists at docs/release-notes.mdx line 42 — `bench eval arc_agi_2 --model openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3.1` (deepseek-v3.1 is open-weight) — with no provider-routing flags at all, immediately under a 'Why it matters' bullet claiming 'Deterministic seeds and clear scoring for reproducibility' and a 'Tips' line telling users to 'control generation with `--temperature`, `--top-p`, and `--seed`'. Separately, docs/index.mdx line 106 shows the bare model string `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3.1` with no invocation context at all. VERIFIED: README.md line 27 states '95+ Benchmarks' (exact figure, not fabricated); VERIFIED GPQA Diamond, AIME (2023/2024/2025 variants), MATH, MathVista, MMLU, MathArena all exist as real eval modules under src/openbench/evals/.
    → mechanism: With every provider_params key left None (confirmed by source read), no `provider` key is injected into extra_body at all — full unconstrained OpenRouter default routing applies. M1 (confirmed): quantization unpinned for the named open-weight model. M2 (confirmed): no `require_parameters=True` anywhere in these examples, so dropped params fail silently. M3 (confirmed): with `order`/`only`/`sort` all unset, routing is unconstrained and not reproducible run-to-run. M5 (confirmed): `data_collection` unset means default retention applies unless the user separately adds `-M data_collection=deny`; this is documented as a real flag (docs/providers.mdx line 132) but never demonstrated set in any runnable example. M7 (confirmed, strengthened by real evidence): openbench documents a `--seed`/`BENCH_SEED` flag (src/openbench/_cli/eval_command.py lines 540-546) and its own docs explicitly pair reproducibility language ('Deterministic seeds ... for reproducibility', 'control generation with ... --seed') with an unpinned `openrouter/` invocation in the same release-notes section — this is a documented instance of the false-determinism framing, not merely an inferred risk.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high
Safeguards present: only/order/ignore/sort/max_price (opt-in via -M flags), quantizations (opt-in via -M flags), require_parameters (opt-in via -M flags), data_collection (opt-in via -M flags), raw response captured in Inspect AI transcript via completion.model_dump() (inherited from inspect_ai, not surfaced by openbench itself), README Reproducibility Statement discloses quantization-driven score variance
One-line fix: Default the OpenRouterAPI wrapper to a safe `provider` payload (quantizations allow-list, require_parameters=True whenever sampling/seed params are set, data_collection="deny") and surface the served `provider`/generation-id from OpenRouter's response into openbench's per-run results/log output, then demonstrate that safe pattern in the GPQA/AIME/MATH/MathArena eval docstrings instead of leaving pinning entirely opt-in via undocumented-by-example `-M` flags.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: RECOMMENDED #2 — zero-config, and the cheapest decisive test we found. `bench eval gpt_oss_aime25 --model openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b -M provider='{...}'` reaches the wire: openbench's `-M` is yaml.safe_load-parsed (_cli/utils.py:39-59) and forwarded as model_args into inspect_ai's eval(), whose OpenRouterAPI collects a `provider` dict kwarg and emits it as extra_body.provider (verified in inspect_ai/model/_providers/openrouter.py, __init__ + completion_params). NOTE: the `-M only=Cerebras` form is wrong — inspect wants the whole dict under `provider`. Best precision gap available anywhere in the survey: cerebras/fp16 ($0.35/$0.75, 100% uptime, logprobs) vs wandb/fp4 ($0.04/$0.14, 95% uptime, logprobs) — fp16 vs fp4 on the same slug, and both expose logprobs so the divergence can also be read out distributionally. 30 AIME-2025 questions x epochs=8 (hardcoded) = 240 completions per arm; 3 conditions = 720 completions, ~5.8M output tokens, ~$3.20 total, well under 1 h.
Signs provider issues messed up the pape: openbench is infrastructure — a CLI/framework other people run against their own model+provider choice — not a project that itself produces or publishes a benchmark result set, transcripts, or a leaderboard. The repo root, the docs/ tree, the tests/ tree (including tests/integration), the README, and the announcement blog post were directly checked for any committed results/outputs/logs/transcripts directory, HF dataset, or leaderboard site; none exists. Per-run results are written to a local `./logs/` directory and optionally pushed to a user's own Hugging Face dataset via `--hub-repo`, but that is an opt-in per-user action, not a published artifact from openbench itself. Nothing is published by this project for a reader to inspect for F1-F11 style fingerprints — the OpenRouterAPI wrapper's behavior can only be assessed by code audit (already done) or by a third party who chooses to run openbench with OpenRouter and publish their own logs.

Title: tau2-bench (Sierra AI)
Summary: tau2-bench is Sierra AI's widely-cited agentic tool-use/user-simulation benchmark (successor to tau-bench). It evaluates agent LLMs across domains (retail, airline, telecom, banking_knowledge) with a public leaderboard at taubench.com.
Importance/Impact: 1,601 GitHub stars; tau-bench/tau2-bench is a de facto standard tool-agent benchmark, with a live public leaderboard (taubench.com) accepting community submissions. Actively maintained (v1.0.1 released 2026-07-15, commits as recent as 2026-07-17).
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is used exclusively as the API backend for one component: dense-embedding retrieval in the `banking_knowledge` domain's RAG tool (`KB_search_dense`/`KB_search`). `OpenRouterEmbedder` (src/tau2/knowledge/embedders/openrouter_embedder.py) wraps the OpenAI SDK pointed at `base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"` and calls `.embeddings.create()` for Qwen3-Embedding-4B/8B (open-weight) and gemini-embedding-001. This is NOT the default retrieval config (default `alltools` uses OpenAI embeddings directly), but it is a first-class, documented, leaderboard-eligible option: `docs/leaderboard-submission.md` and `src/tau2/knowledge/README.md` both give runnable examples (`tau2 run --domain banking_knowledge --retrieval-config alltools-qwen --agent-llm gpt-4.1 --user-llm gpt-4.1 --num-trials 4`) that community members can submit to the public taubench.com leaderboard. The primary agent/user/judge LLM calls go through litellm directly to proprietary provider APIs (OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini), not through OpenRouter, by default — confirmed via `src/tau2/config.py` (DEFAULT_LLM_AGENT="gpt-4.1-2025-04-14", DEFAULT_LLM_EVAL_USER_SIMULATOR="claude-opus-4-5"). A test file (`tests/test_review_model.py`) shows an `"openrouter/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4"` litellm-prefixed string is accepted for the optional --review-model flag, but this is a mocked unit test of a generic litellm passthrough, not a built OpenRouter integration, and even if used it's a proprietary model (low routing risk).
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization): OpenRouterEmbedder.SUPPORTED_MODELS includes qwen3-embedding-4b/8b, open-weight models routed through OpenRouter's default load-balanced multi-provider serving with no `provider.quantizations` filter anywhere in the codebase — confirmed via full-repo grep for `quantizations` (zero hits). M3 (probabilistic provider routing): the `.embeddings.create()` call at openrouter_embedder.py:146 passes only `input`, `model`, `encoding_format` — no `extra_body={'provider': {...}}`, no order/only/sort anywhere (zero `extra_body` hits repo-wide), so OpenRouter's default inverse-price load balancing picks whichever backend serves Qwen3-Embedding on each call/run. M4 (no provenance logging): neither the embedder nor `embeddings_cache.py` (EmbeddingsCache keys purely on embedder_type/model/query_instruction hash) ever captures the response's `provider` field or a generation id, so which backend actually served a given embedding is unrecoverable after the fact. M5 (data-policy leakage): `data_collection` is never set to "deny" (zero hits repo-wide), so OpenRouter's default "allow" may route KB document/query text to providers that train on submitted data. Title: Bespoke Curator (bespokelabsai/curator)
Summary: Open-source Python library for LLM-based synthetic data curation/distillation (used to build OpenThoughts-114k, Bespoke-Stratos-17k, s1K-1.1). It supports many pluggable inference backends; OpenRouter is one optional generic-OpenAI-compatible base_url a user can configure.
Importance/Impact: 1,702 stars; datasets it produced (OpenThoughts-114k, Bespoke-Stratos-17k, s1K-1.1) are widely cited/reused in the 2025 open reasoning-distillation wave.

Title: Palisade Research — robot_shutdown_resistance
